# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
> *"Distinguish between direct and indirect methods of measurement. Give two examples of each."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Direct Method | Indirect Method |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Value of measurand obtained directly from instrument reading | Value obtained by computation using other measured quantities |
| Computation | Not required | Required |
| Example 1 | Length measured using Vernier caliper | Volume of cylinder from $V = \pi r^{2} h$ |
| Example 2 | Temperature using mercury thermometer | Density of liquid from $\rho = m/V$ |

---

### Question A2

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
> *"What is meant by error propagation in indirect methods? Why is it significant?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Error propagation refers to the manner in which small errors (uncertainties) in independently measured quantities $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ combine and accumulate to produce a final error in the indirectly computed quantity $y = f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$. It is governed by the formula:

$$\Delta y = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\vert \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} \right\vert \Delta x_i$$

**Significance:** It determines the **accuracy limit** of the entire measurement system. Even if each sub-instrument is highly accurate, the propagated error may render the final result unreliable. For Mechatronics systems relying on multi-sensor fusion, error propagation analysis is essential for system design and sensor selection.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B questions carry **14 marks**, typically divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each, OR one large numerical + one theory sub-part.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> **(a)** With the help of a clear classification diagram, explain the different methods of measurement. Differentiate between direct and indirect methods with suitable engineering examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The viscosity $\eta$ of a liquid is determined by the formula $\eta = \dfrac{\pi P r^{4}}{8 L Q}$, where $P$ is the pressure difference, $r$ is the tube radius, $L$ is the tube length, and $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate. Given $P = 100 \pm 2 \text{ Pa}$, $r = 2.00 \pm 0.01 \text{ cm}$, $L = 50.0 \pm 0.5 \text{ cm}$, $Q = 10.0 \pm 0.2 \text{ cm}^{3}/\text{s}$. Find the **maximum percentage error** in $\eta$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Present a classification table:

| Method | Sub-type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Direct | Comparison | Beam balance |
| Direct | Deflection | Voltmeter |
| Direct | Substitution | Wheatstone bridge (null deflection) |
| Indirect | Composite | Density $\rho = m/V$ |
| Indirect | Transduction | Strain from strain gauge |
| Indirect | Differential | Gauge pressure from differential transducer |

**Step 2:** Clearly state the defining difference:
- **Direct:** Instrument reads the measurand value directly. **No computation.**
- **Indirect:** Measurand is derived from other measured quantities via a known formula. **Computation mandatory.**

**Step 3:** Provide 2 examples each with engineering context (Vernier caliper for direct; $\rho = m/V$ for indirect).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Identify the function form:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{\pi}{8} \cdot P^{1} \cdot r^{4} \cdot L^{-1} \cdot Q^{-1}$$

This is in **product-of-powers** form. Apply the power-rule shortcut:

$$\dfrac{\Delta \eta}{\eta} = 1 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{P} + 4 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta r}{r} + 1 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} + 1 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta Q}{Q}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\Delta P}{P} &= \dfrac{2}{100} = 0.020 \\[6pt]
4 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta r}{r} &= 4 \cdot \dfrac{0.01}{2.00} = 4 \cdot 0.005 = 0.020 \\[6pt]
\dfrac{\Delta L}{L} &= \dfrac{0.5}{50.0} = 0.010 \\[6pt]
\dfrac{\Delta Q}{Q} &= \dfrac{0.2}{10.0} = 0.020
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the contributions:**

$$\dfrac{\Delta \eta}{\eta} = 0.020 + 0.020 + 0.010 + 0.020 = 0.070$$

**Step 4 — Convert to percentage:**

$$\boxed{\;\text{Percentage error in } \eta = 7.0\%\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:** Students frequently forget to take the **absolute value** of exponents in the power rule. Since errors are always additive in magnitude, $|a|$ is used. Marks deducted: **2 marks**.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #2:** Students often confuse **$L^{-1}$** (giving $-\Delta L / L$) and forget the absolute value. The correct contribution is $+\Delta L / L$. Marks deducted: **1 mark**.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**
> **(a)** Define the terms *Direct Method*, *Indirect Method*, and *Composite Measurement*. Explain with suitable examples why the indirect method is sometimes the **only** feasible option in engineering measurements. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** In an experiment to determine the acceleration due to gravity $g$ using a simple pendulum, the formula used is $g = \dfrac{4 \pi^{2} L}{T^{2}}$, where $L$ is the length of the pendulum and $T$ is the time period. Given $L = 100.0 \pm 0.1 \text{ cm}$ and $T = 2.00 \pm 0.01 \text{ s}$, calculate:
> - (i) The nominal value of $g$.
> - (ii) The maximum absolute error and percentage error in $g$.
> **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Direct Method:** A method in which the measurand is read directly from the instrument's calibrated scale. Example: Reading the temperature on a mercury thermometer.

**Indirect Method:** A method in which the measurand is determined by measuring other functionally related quantities and computing the result. Example: Determining density from mass and volume.

**Composite Measurement:** A sub-class of indirect measurement where the measurand depends on **two or more independently measured quantities**. Example: Electrical power $P = V \times I$.

**Why indirect is the only feasible option (give at least 3 valid points):**

1. **Inaccessible measurand:** Direct temperature measurement inside a nuclear reactor core is impossible — we use a thermocouple (indirect via EMF).
2. **Hazardous environment:** Pressure inside a deep-sea submersible cannot be read with a direct gauge — a strain-gauge-based pressure transducer converts it to voltage (indirect).
3. **Non-physical measurand:** Quantities like *strain*, *viscosity*, and *density* are defined mathematically and have no direct-reading scale. They MUST be measured indirectly.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Part (i) — Nominal value of $g$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
g &= \dfrac{4 \pi^{2} L}{T^{2}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{4 \times (3.14159)^{2} \times 100.0}{(2.00)^{2}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 100.0}{4.00} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{3947.84}{4.00} \\[6pt]
&= 986.96 \text{ cm/s}^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

So $\boxed{g \approx 9.87 \text{ m/s}^{2}}$.

**Part (ii) — Error Analysis:**

Using the power-rule form: $g \propto L^{1} \cdot T^{-2}$

$$\dfrac{\Delta g}{g} = 1 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} + 2 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T}{T}$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\Delta g}{g} &= 1 \cdot \dfrac{0.1}{100.0} + 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.01}{2.00} \\[6pt]
&= 0.001 + 2 \times 0.005 \\[6pt]
&= 0.001 + 0.010 = 0.011
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore percentage error = $\mathbf{1.1\%}$.

Absolute error:

$$\Delta g = 0.011 \times 986.96 \approx 10.86 \text{ cm/s}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\;g = 986.96 \pm 10.86 \text{ cm/s}^{2}\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #3:** In the pendulum problem, students often forget to **square the time error** contribution. The exponent of $T$ is **2**, so its contribution is $2 \times (\Delta T / T)$, not $1 \times (\Delta T / T)$. Marks deducted: **2 marks**.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #4:** Mixing up units (cm vs m, s² vs s) in the final answer. Always state units explicitly. Marks deducted: **1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorise Before Every KTU Exam**

✅ **Core Definitions**
- **Direct Method:** Measurand value read directly from the instrument's scale. **No computation needed.**
- **Indirect Method:** Measurand computed from other measured quantities using a functional formula.

✅ **Key Distinction**
- Direct = **one instrument, one reading**.
- Indirect = **multiple measurements, then calculation**.

✅ **The Universal Error Propagation Formula**

$$\Delta y_{\max} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\vert \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} \right\vert \Delta x_i$$

✅ **The Power-Rule Shortcut (MUST MEMORISE)**

For $y = k \cdot x_1^{a} \cdot x_2^{b} \cdot x_3^{c} \cdots$:

$$\dfrac{\Delta y}{\vert y \vert} = \vert a \vert \dfrac{\Delta x_1}{\vert x_1 \vert} + \vert b \vert \dfrac{\Delta x_2}{\vert x_2 \vert} + \vert c \vert \dfrac{\Delta x_3}{\vert x_3 \vert} + \cdots$$

✅ **RMS Error Formula (for independent random errors)**

$$\Delta y_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}\right)^{2} (\Delta x_i)^{2}}$$

✅ **Common Engineering Examples to Remember**
- Direct: Vernier caliper, beam balance, voltmeter, stopwatch.
- Indirect: Density ($\rho = m/V$), pressure ($P = \rho g h$), strain ($\varepsilon = \delta/L$), resistance ($R = V/I$), viscosity (Poiseuille's formula).

✅ **Important Behavioural Rules**
- Errors are **always additive in magnitude** (worst-case bound).
- The variable with the **largest exponent** in the formula contributes the most to the final error — this guides instrument selection.
- For power-form functions, **percentages add directly**, which simplifies exam calculations enormously.

✅ **Exam Strategy Tip**
- Whenever you see a problem asking for "maximum error", use the **worst-case (sum of absolute values)** form.
- Whenever you see "most probable error" or "statistical error", use the **RMS (square root of sum of squares)** form.

✅ **Common Mistake to Avoid**
- Do **NOT** confuse the **fractional error** ($\Delta y / y$) with the **percentage error** (multiply by 100).
- Do **NOT** forget to include the constant $k$ — its derivative is zero, so it has no error contribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When solving numerical problems in the exam, **always show the formula first, then the substitution, then the step-by-step arithmetic**. Examiners reward methodical working and deduct marks for skipped steps. The partial derivative / power-rule shortcut is your biggest time-saver — practice 5 problems before the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT205 - TRANSDUCERS & MEASUREMENTS (MECHATRONICS ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Instruments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Measurement Instruments?

A **Measurement Instrument** is a device used to determine a physical quantity's magnitude, state, or condition, typically by converting it into a readable, storable, or transmissible signal. In the context of KTU 2024 Mechatronics engineering, instruments form the critical interface between physical phenomena and the digital/analog processing units of an automated system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A measurement instrument is essentially a *man-machine interface* that senses, conditions, displays, and sometimes records a measurand. The accuracy of any mechatronic system is fundamentally limited by the quality of its instruments.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you visit a doctor. The doctor uses:
- A **stethoscope** to "listen" to your heartbeat (analog sensing)
- A **digital thermometer** to read your body temperature (digital display)
- A **blood pressure monitor** to quantify pressure (signal conditioning + display)

Each of these is a measurement instrument. In engineering, we have similar tools — but instead of the human body, they sense **temperature, pressure, force, displacement, voltage, current, and frequency** in machines and processes. The "doctor's eyes" (display), "doctor's ears" (sensing), and "doctor's brain" (signal processing) all combine into a single instrument in modern mechatronics.

## 1.3 Classification of Instruments

Instruments can be broadly classified as:

| Classification Category | Type | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **By Signal Type** | Analog Instruments | Analog ammeter, analog voltmeter |
| | Digital Instruments | DMM (Digital Multimeter) |
| **By Function** | Indicating Instruments | CRO, DSO |
| | Recording Instruments | X-Y plotter, data loggers |
| **By Application** | Electrical Instruments | Voltmeter, ammeter, ohmmeter |
| | Electronic Instruments | CRO, signal analyzer, Q-meter |
| **By Output** | Active Instruments | Transducers with built-in power |
| | Passive Instruments | Resistive sensors (need excitation) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Focus:** Module 3 specifically emphasizes **Indicating Instruments (CRO, DSO, Bridges)** and **Signal Analyzers**. Analog meters are covered as foundational context.

## 1.4 Functional Elements of a Measurement Instrument

Every generalized measurement instrument (per ISO/IEC standards adopted in KTU curriculum) consists of three functional blocks:

1. **Primary Sensing Element / Sensor** — directly interacts with the measurand.
2. **Variable Conversion Element / Signal Conditioner** — converts the sensor output into a usable form (amplification, filtering, linearization).
3. **Data Presentation Element / Display** — provides a human-readable or machine-readable output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **signal conditioning** stage is where most engineering design effort goes — it determines the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**, **bandwidth**, and **linearity** of the entire instrument chain.

## 1.5 Static and Dynamic Characteristics — Quick Foundation

Before diving into specific instruments, the KTU 2024 scheme expects students to recall these core performance parameters:

- **Accuracy:** Closeness of measurement to the true value (expressed as % of full scale or % of reading).
- **Precision:** Repeatability of measurements under identical conditions.
- **Sensitivity:** Ratio of change in output to change in input.
- **Resolution:** Smallest measurable increment.
- **Linearity:** Deviation of the output-input curve from a straight line.
- **Drift:** Slow change of output with time (zero drift, span drift).
- **Hysteresis:** Difference in output for the same input approached from opposite directions.
- **Dead Zone:** Range of input over which there is no output.
- **Bandwidth:** Frequency range over which the instrument operates within specified accuracy.
- **Response Time (Time Constant):** Time required to reach a specified percentage (usually 63.2% for first-order) of the final value.

> [!NOTE]
> The static characteristics describe instrument behavior when the measurand is **constant or slowly varying**, while dynamic characteristics describe behavior when the measurand **changes rapidly with time**. KTU questions often test this distinction with a numerical scenario.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Analog vs. Digital Instruments — Core Operational Differences

### A. Analog Instruments
Analog instruments use continuous signals and mechanical/electromagnetic deflection. Examples: **PMMC (Permanent Magnet Moving Coil) meters**, moving iron meters, dynamometer-type wattmeters.

**Operational Logic:**
1. Measurand produces a small physical effect (e.g., current → magnetic field).
2. This effect generates a **deflecting torque** ($T_d$).
3. A **controlling torque** ($T_c = K_s \cdot \theta$) opposes it, where $K_s$ is the spring constant and $\theta$ is the deflection.
4. A **damping torque** ($T_D = D \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}$) prevents oscillation.
5. At equilibrium: $T_d = T_c$, giving a steady deflection proportional to the measurand.

### B. Digital Instruments
Digital instruments convert the analog input into a discrete digital code (usually binary) and display numerically. Examples: **DMM**, **DMM with ADC**, **frequency counters**.

**Operational Logic:**
1. Analog input → **Signal Conditioner** (amplification, filtering).
2. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** discretizes the signal.
3. **Microcontroller/DSP** processes the digital data.
4. **Display Driver** shows the value on LCD/7-segment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Insight:** Digital instruments offer **higher accuracy, better resolution, and programmability**, but analog instruments still excel in **real-time continuous response** and are immune to aliasing.

## 2.2 Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO) — The Workhorse of Electronics

The **Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO)** is a versatile electronic instrument used to visualize voltage signals as a function of time on a phosphor screen.

### Block Diagram — Functional Flow:

**Signal → Vertical Amplifier → Vertical Deflection Plates → CRT/Display**

Simultaneously: **Time-base Generator → Horizontal Deflection Plates**

The electron beam, emitted by the **electron gun**, is accelerated, focused, and deflected to trace the waveform. The two pairs of deflection plates (vertical and horizontal) provide the $Y$ and $X$ coordinates respectively.

### 2.2.1 Key CRO Subsystems

| Subsystem | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Electron Gun** | Produces, accelerates, and focuses the electron beam |
| **Deflection Plates** | Y-plates (vertical) and X-plates (horizontal) deflect the beam |
| **Phosphor Screen** | Displays the trace (glows when struck by electrons) |
| **Time Base Generator** | Produces a linear sawtooth voltage for horizontal sweep |
| **Trigger Circuit** | Synchronizes the sweep with the input signal |
| **Vertical Amplifier** | Amplifies weak input signals to drive Y-plates |
| **Horizontal Amplifier** | Amplifies the time-base signal for X-plates |

### 2.2.2 Measurements Using CRO

1. **Voltage Measurement:** $V_{pp} = \text{Vertical Sensitivity (V/div)} \times \text{Number of divisions}$
2. **Time Period Measurement:** $T = \text{Horizontal Sensitivity (s/div)} \times \text{Number of divisions per cycle}$
3. **Frequency Measurement:** $f = \frac{1}{T}$
4. **Phase Difference:** $\phi = \frac{\Delta x}{x} \times 360°$, where $\Delta x$ is the horizontal shift and $x$ is the period length.
5. **Lissajous Figures:** For phase/frequency comparison — frequency ratio determined by counting tangent points.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Trend:** Questions on **Lissajous figures** and **phase measurement** appear frequently. Memorize the counting method: number of horizontal tangency points = $f_y / f_x$ ratio denominator, and vice versa.

## 2.3 Bridges for Measurement

Bridges are null-type instruments offering **high accuracy** because measurement is reduced to detecting a null (zero) condition.

### 2.3.1 Wheatstone Bridge
Used for measuring **unknown resistance** with high precision.

**Balance Condition:**
$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_4}$$

The unknown resistance $R_x$ is connected as one arm, and the bridge is balanced by adjusting the known resistors until the galvanometer reads zero.

### 2.3.2 Maxwell's Bridge
Used for measuring **unknown inductance** (especially with medium Q-factor coils).

**Balance Equations:**

$$R_x = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_3}{R_4}$$

$$L_x = R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdot C_4$$

where $C_4$ is a standard capacitor in the bridge.

### 2.3.3 Schering Bridge
Used for measuring **capacitance, dissipation factor, and insulation properties** of capacitors and dielectrics.

**Balance Equations:**

$$C_x = C_1 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3}$$

$$R_x = R_1 \cdot \frac{C_3}{C_1}$$

Dissipation factor: $D = \omega R_1 C_1$

### 2.3.4 Wien Bridge
Used for **frequency measurement** of audio-frequency signals and as a feedback element in oscillators.

**Balance Equation:**
$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$$

For the typical equal-ratio configuration ($R_1 = R_2$, $C_1 = C_2$):
$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}$$

## 2.4 Q-Meter

The **Q-meter** is an instrument used to measure the **Quality Factor (Q)** of an inductor or capacitor at radio frequencies.

**Definition Recap:** $Q = \frac{\omega L}{R} = \frac{1}{\omega R C} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{\text{Energy Stored}}{\text{Energy Dissipated per cycle}}$

**Operating Principle:**
1. A known RF voltage $V_1$ is applied across a small series resistance $R_s$.
2. The test component (inductor) is connected in series.
3. A calibrated variable capacitor $C$ is adjusted to **resonance**.
4. The voltage $V_2$ across the capacitor is measured.
5. $Q = \frac{V_2}{V_1}$ (since at resonance, the voltage across reactive elements is $Q$ times the applied voltage).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Application | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\theta = \frac{T_d}{K_s}$ | Deflection in analog meter (PMMC) | radians |
| 2 | $V_{pp} = S_v \times n_v$ | CRO voltage measurement | Volts |
| 3 | $T = S_h \times n_h$ | CRO time period measurement | seconds |
| 4 | $f = \frac{1}{T}$ | Frequency from time period | Hertz |
| 5 | $\phi = \frac{\Delta x}{x} \times 360°$ | Phase difference from CRO | degrees |
| 6 | $\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_4}$ | Wheatstone bridge balance | dimensionless |
| 7 | $L_x = R_2 R_3 C_4$ | Maxwell bridge inductance | Henry |
| 8 | $C_x = C_1 \frac{R_4}{R_3}$ | Schering bridge capacitance | Farad |
| 9 | $D = \omega R_1 C_1$ | Schering bridge dissipation factor | dimensionless |
| 10 | $f = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | Wien bridge frequency | Hertz |
| 11 | $Q = \frac{\omega L}{R}$ | Quality factor (inductor) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $Q = \frac{V_2}{V_1}$ | Q-meter measurement | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta x}$ | Instrument sensitivity | rad/unit |
| 14 | $\varepsilon = \frac{A_t - A_m}{A_t} \times 100\%$ | Percentage error | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbols used in this table:** $S_v$ = vertical sensitivity, $S_h$ = horizontal sensitivity, $n_v$ = number of vertical divisions, $n_h$ = number of horizontal divisions, $A_t$ = true value, $A_m$ = measured value, $\omega = 2\pi f$.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **CRO:** Used in **R&D labs, troubleshooting circuits, observing transients**, and verifying signal integrity in embedded systems.
- **Wheatstone Bridge:** Used in **strain gauge load cells**, RTD temperature sensors (as 3-wire/4-wire configurations), and **force/pressure transducers**.
- **Maxwell Bridge:** Essential in **motor winding testing**, transformer inductance measurements, and inductor quality control in power electronics manufacturing.
- **Schering Bridge:** Critical in **high-voltage insulation testing** of cables, transformer bushings, and **dielectric material characterization**.
- **Q-Meter:** Used in **RF inductor manufacturing**, antenna tuning circuits, and filter design validation.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Deflection Equation of a PMMC Instrument

**Statement:** Derive the expression for steady-state deflection of a Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC) instrument.

**Setup:** When a current $I$ flows through a coil of $N$ turns placed in a radial magnetic field of flux density $B$, with each side of the coil having effective length $l$:

**Step 1 — Deflecting Torque Calculation**

The force on each side of the coil:
$$F = B \cdot I \cdot l \cdot N$$

**Step 2 — Torque Generated**

If the coil has a width (effective diameter) $d$, the deflecting torque is:
$$T_d = F \times d = B \cdot I \cdot l \cdot N \cdot d$$

The term $l \cdot d$ is the effective area $A$ of the coil, so:
$$T_d = B \cdot I \cdot N \cdot A$$

**Step 3 — Controlling Torque from the Spring**

The phosphor-bronze hair spring provides a restoring torque:
$$T_c = K_s \cdot \theta$$

where $K_s$ is the spring constant (N·m/rad) and $\theta$ is the angular deflection in radians.

**Step 4 — Equilibrium Condition**

At steady state (deflection has stopped):
$$T_d = T_c$$

Substituting:
$$B \cdot I \cdot N \cdot A = K_s \cdot \theta$$

**Step 5 — Final Deflection Equation**

Solving for $\theta$:
$$\theta = \frac{B \cdot N \cdot A}{K_s} \cdot I$$

The term $\frac{B \cdot N \cdot A}{K_s}$ is the **current sensitivity** of the instrument, denoted $S_I$.

$$\boxed{\theta = S_I \cdot I}$$

**Conclusion:** The deflection is **directly proportional to the current** — this linear relationship is why PMMC instruments have a **uniform scale** and are highly accurate for DC measurements.

## 3.2 Derivation: Wheatstone Bridge Sensitivity

**Statement:** Derive the galvanometer current expression for an unbalanced Wheatstone bridge.

**Setup:** Consider a Wheatstone bridge with arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ powered by a battery of EMF $E$ with internal resistance negligible. A galvanometer of resistance $R_g$ is connected between nodes B and D.

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)**

Using mesh analysis with two loops (left loop: $R_1, R_2, R_g$ and right loop: $R_3, R_4, R_g$), and letting $I_g$ be the galvanometer current:

**Loop 1 (Left):**
$$E = I_1 R_1 + I_g R_g + I_2 R_2$$

**Loop 2 (Right):**
$$E = I_3 R_3 + (I_g - I_2) R_4$$

Wait — let me use the standard notation. Let $I_1$ = current through $R_1$, $I_3$ = current through $R_3$, $I_2$ = current through $R_2$, $I_4$ = current through $R_4$, and $I_g$ = galvanometer current.

**Node equations:** $I_1 = I_2 + I_g$ and $I_3 + I_g = I_4$.

**Step 2 — KVL Equation for Galvanometer Loop (B to D)**

$$I_2 R_2 - I_4 R_4 = I_g R_g$$

**Step 3 — Express in Terms of Battery Current**

Let the total battery current be $I = I_1 + I_3$. Then:
$$I_1 = \frac{E}{R_1 + \frac{R_2 (R_g + R_4)}{R_2 + R_g + R_4}}$$

For a **balanced bridge** ($I_g = 0$), the simpler condition is:

**Step 4 — Balance Condition Derivation**

When $I_g = 0$, no current flows through the galvanometer, so $I_1 = I_2$ and $I_3 = I_4$. Also, the potentials at B and D are equal:

$$V_B = V_D$$
$$I_1 R_1 = I_3 R_3 \quad \text{and} \quad I_2 R_2 = I_4 R_4$$

Dividing these:
$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_4}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_4}}$$

**Step 5 — Galvanometer Current for Unbalanced Case**

Using Thevenin's theorem at the galvanometer terminals:

Thevenin voltage:
$$V_{th} = E \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

Thevenin resistance:
$$R_{th} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} + \frac{R_3 R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

Galvanometer current:
$$I_g = \frac{V_{th}}{R_{th} + R_g}$$

$$\boxed{I_g = \frac{E \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)}{\frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} + \frac{R_3 R_4}{R_3 + R_4} + R_g}}$$

**Conclusion:** This expression forms the basis for designing **deflection-type Wheatstone bridges** and is fundamental to transducer-based measurements using strain gauges and RTDs.

## 3.3 Derivation: Maxwell Bridge Inductance Measurement

**Statement:** Derive the balance equations for Maxwell's bridge used to measure an unknown inductance.

**Setup:** Maxwell's bridge measures an unknown inductance $L_x$ with internal resistance $R_x$ by comparing it with a **standard capacitor** $C_4$ (since capacitors are more compact and stable than standard inductors).

The bridge arms are:
- Arm 1: Unknown inductor $L_x$ in series with $R_x$
- Arm 2: Known resistor $R_2$
- Arm 3: Known resistor $R_3$
- Arm 4: Standard capacitor $C_4$ in series with known resistor $R_4$

**Step 1 — Write the General Balance Condition**

At balance ($I_g = 0$):
$$Z_1 \cdot Z_4 = Z_2 \cdot Z_3$$

Substituting the impedances:
$$(R_x + j\omega L_x) \left( R_4 + \frac{1}{j\omega C_4} \right) = R_2 \cdot R_3$$

**Step 2 — Expand the Left Side**

$$(R_x + j\omega L_x)(R_4 + \frac{1}{j\omega C_4}) = R_2 R_3$$

$$R_x R_4 + \frac{R_x}{j\omega C_4} + j\omega L_x R_4 + \frac{j\omega L_x}{j\omega C_4} = R_2 R_3$$

$$R_x R_4 - j\frac{R_x}{\omega C_4} + j\omega L_x R_4 + \frac{L_x}{C_4} = R_2 R_3$$

**Step 3 — Separate Real and Imaginary Parts**

Real part:
$$R_x R_4 + \frac{L_x}{C_4} = R_2 R_3$$

Imaginary part (coefficient of $j$):
$$-\frac{R_x}{\omega C_4} + \omega L_x R_4 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve the Imaginary Equation for $R_x$**

$$\omega L_x R_4 = \frac{R_x}{\omega C_4}$$

$$R_x = \omega^2 L_x R_4 C_4$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Back into Real Equation**

$$(\omega^2 L_x R_4 C_4) R_4 + \frac{L_x}{C_4} = R_2 R_3$$

$$\omega^2 L_x R_4^2 C_4 + \frac{L_x}{C_4} = R_2 R_3$$

$$L_x \left( \omega^2 R_4^2 C_4 + \frac{1}{C_4} \right) = R_2 R_3$$

$$L_x = \frac{R_2 R_3 C_4}{1 + \omega^2 R_4^2 C_4^2}$$

**Step 6 — Final Result (Standard Form)**

This is the exact expression. However, if we choose $R_4$ such that $\omega^2 R_4^2 C_4^2 \ll 1$ (low Q-factor assumption), the equation simplifies to:

$$\boxed{L_x \approx R_2 R_3 C_4}$$

$$\boxed{R_x = \omega^2 L_x R_4 C_4 = \frac{\omega^2 R_2 R_3 R_4 C_4^2}{1 + \omega^2 R_4^2 C_4^2}}$$

**Conclusion:** Maxwell's bridge is best suited for measuring **medium Q-factor inductors** (Q between 1 and 10). For high-Q inductors, **Hay's bridge** is preferred.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Phase Measurement Using CRO

**Problem:** A CRO displays two sinusoidal waveforms of the same frequency. The horizontal distance for one complete cycle is **6 cm**, and the horizontal shift between the two waveforms is **0.5 cm**. Calculate the phase difference.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Given Data**
- Length of one cycle on screen, $x = 6$ cm
- Phase shift on screen, $\Delta x = 0.5$ cm

**Step 2 — Apply the Phase Difference Formula**

The phase difference is given by:
$$\phi = \frac{\Delta x}{x} \times 360°$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Values**

$$\phi = \frac{0.5}{6} \times 360°$$

$$\phi = \frac{0.5 \times 360}{6}$$

$$\phi = \frac{180}{6}$$

$$\boxed{\phi = 30°}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

The second waveform **leads or lags** the first by **30 degrees**, depending on the direction of the shift. If the second waveform appears to the **left** of the first, it **leads**; if to the **right**, it **lags**.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Wien Bridge Frequency

**Problem:** A Wien bridge has $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_1 = 100 \text{ nF}$, and $C_2 = 100 \text{ nF}$. Find the frequency at which the bridge is balanced.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the Wien Bridge Formula**

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Values**

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{(10 \times 10^3) \cdot (10 \times 10^3) \cdot (100 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (100 \times 10^{-9})}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the Expression**

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{10^4 \cdot 10^4 \cdot 10^{-14}}}$$

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{10^{8} \cdot 10^{-14}}}$$

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{10^{-6}}}$$

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{f = \frac{1000}{2\pi} \approx 159.15 \text{ Hz}}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion**

The Wien bridge will be balanced at approximately **159.15 Hz**, a standard audio-frequency reference. This is why Wien bridge oscillators are commonly designed around this frequency range.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Q-Meter Reading

**Problem:** A Q-meter is used to measure the Q-factor of an inductor. The injected voltage $V_1$ is 10 mV, and the voltmeter reads $V_2 = 1.5$ V at resonance. Calculate the Q-factor of the inductor.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the Q-Meter Formula**

$$Q = \frac{V_2}{V_1}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Values**

$$Q = \frac{1.5}{10 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Q = \frac{1.5}{0.01}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 150}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation**

The inductor has a Q-factor of **150**, indicating **low losses** and **high selectivity** at the resonant frequency. This is typical of a high-quality RF inductor.

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Bridges

| Parameter | Wheatstone | Maxwell | Schering | Wien |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Quantity Measured** | Resistance | Inductance | Capacitance | Frequency |
| **Standard Element** | Resistors | Capacitor | Capacitor | Resistors + Capacitors |
| **Balance Type** | DC | AC | AC | AC |
| **Source Frequency** | DC | Audio (1 kHz typical) | Power freq (50 Hz) or audio | Audio |
| **Range of Measurement** | 1 Ω to 1 MΩ | Medium Q inductors | Small capacitors | 100 Hz – 100 kHz |
| **Null Detector** | Galvanometer | Headphones / AC detector | AC detector | Headphones / AC detector |
| **Suitability** | Strain gauges, RTDs | Motor windings | HV insulation | Audio oscillators |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The choice of bridge depends on the **type of measurand**, the **required accuracy**, and the **operating frequency**. Wheatstone and Maxwell are the most commonly asked.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q:** Define the term "Quality Factor" of an inductor. How is it measured using a Q-meter?

**Model Answer:**

The **Quality Factor (Q)** of an inductor is defined as the ratio of its inductive reactance to its effective series resistance at a specified frequency:

$$Q = \frac{\omega L}{R} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{\text{Energy stored per cycle}}{\text{Energy dissipated per cycle}}$$

**Q-Meter Measurement Procedure:**

1. A small RF voltage $V_1$ is applied across a very small series resistance $R_s$ (typically 0.02 Ω).
2. The test inductor is connected in series with a calibrated variable capacitor $C$.
3. The variable capacitor is adjusted until the circuit reaches **resonance** (maximum $V_2$ on the voltmeter).
4. At resonance, the voltage across the capacitor is amplified by the Q-factor:
   $$Q = \frac{V_2}{V_1}$$
5. The Q-factor is read directly from the calibrated voltmeter scale.

**(3 Marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks working principle)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q:** Distinguish between analog and digital instruments. Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Analog Instruments | Digital Instruments |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Signal Type** | Continuous, varies smoothly | Discrete, binary-coded values |
| **Output** | Pointer deflection on a scale | Numerical digits on display |
| **Resolution** | Limited by scale marking | High (often 3.5 to 8.5 digits) |
| **Accuracy** | Typically 0.5% to 2% | Typically 0.01% to 0.1% |
| **Power Consumption** | Generally low | Higher (needs ADC, display) |
| **Immunity to Noise** | Susceptible to noise | Better (signal processing possible) |
| **Examples** | PMMC voltmeter, moving-iron ammeter | DMM, digital frequency counter, DSO |

**(3 Marks: 2 marks for the comparison + 1 mark for examples)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 3 Choice 1]**

**Q(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional operation of a Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO). List its main subsystems and explain the function of each. **(7 Marks)**

**Q(b)** Two sinusoidal signals of the same frequency are displayed on a CRO. The trace shows that one complete cycle occupies 8 horizontal divisions, and the phase shift between the two traces is 1.2 divisions. If the time-base is set to 10 µs/div, calculate:
   (i) The frequency of the signals
   (ii) The phase difference between the signals in degrees and radians **(7 Marks)**

---

### SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — CRO Block Diagram and Subsystems (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Signal] --> B[Attenuator]
    B --> C[Vertical Amplifier]
    C --> D[Delay Line]
    D --> E[Y Deflection Plates]
    F[Trigger Input] --> G[Trigger Circuit]
    G --> H[Time Base Generator]
    H --> I[Horizontal Amplifier]
    I --> J[X Deflection Plates]
    K[High Voltage Supply] --> L[Electron Gun]
    L --> E
    L --> J
    E --> M[Phosphor Screen]
    J --> M
    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#ed6c02,color:#ffffff
```

**Main Subsystems and Functions:**

1. **Electron Gun** (1 mark): Produces a focused beam of electrons using a heated cathode, control grid, focusing anode, and accelerating anode. The emitted electrons are accelerated toward the phosphor screen.

2. **Deflection Plates** (1.5 marks): Two pairs of plates:
   - **Y-plates (vertical)**: Deflect the beam vertically proportional to the input signal voltage.
   - **X-plates (horizontal)**: Deflect the beam horizontally proportional to the time-base voltage (sawtooth waveform).

3. **Phosphor Screen** (0.5 marks): Coated with phosphor that glows when struck by the electron beam, producing a visible trace.

4. **Time Base Generator** (1 mark): Generates a linear **sawtooth voltage** that sweeps the beam horizontally at a constant rate. The sweep is triggered by the input signal for synchronization.

5. **Trigger Circuit** (0.5 marks): Selects a specific point (level and slope) on the input signal to start the sweep, ensuring a stable, non-flickering display.

6. **Vertical Amplifier** (0.5 marks): Amplifies weak input signals to levels sufficient to drive the Y-plates.

7. **Horizontal Amplifier** (0.5 marks): Amplifies the time-base voltage for the X-plates.

8. **Power Supplies** (0.5 marks): High-voltage (kV range) for CRT, low-voltage for amplifiers and timing circuits.

9. **Delay Line** (0.5 marks): Delays the input signal slightly so the sweep starts before the signal reaches the Y-plates, allowing the leading edge of fast signals to be observed.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- One cycle = 8 divisions
- Phase shift = 1.2 divisions
- Time base, $S_h$ = 10 µs/div

**Step 1: Calculate the Time Period (1 mark)**

$$T = S_h \times \text{Number of divisions per cycle}$$

$$T = 10 \, \mu\text{s/div} \times 8 \, \text{div} = 80 \, \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Frequency (1 mark)**

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{80 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{f = 12{,}500 \, \text{Hz} = 12.5 \, \text{kHz}}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Phase Difference in Degrees (1 mark)**

$$\phi = \frac{\Delta x}{x} \times 360°$$

$$\phi = \frac{1.2}{8} \times 360° = 0.15 \times 360°$$

$$\boxed{\phi = 54°}$$

**Step 4: Convert Phase Difference to Radians (1 mark)**

$$\phi_{\text{rad}} = \phi_{\text{deg}} \times \frac{\pi}{180}$$

$$\phi_{\text{rad}} = 54 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{54\pi}{180} = \frac{3\pi}{10}$$

$$\boxed{\phi_{\text{rad}} = 0.3\pi \approx 0.9425 \, \text{rad}}$$

**Step 5: Verification (2 marks)**
- Sanity check: Phase difference should be between 0° and 360° → 54° ✓
- Frequency range is in the audio domain → consistent with CRO time-base of 10 µs/div ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse **time/div** with **frequency** units. The horizontal sensitivity is in **time per division**, not frequency per division. Also, do not forget to convert **µs to seconds** when applying $f = 1/T$. Mark deductions: -2 marks for unit error, -1 mark if radians are not computed.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 3 Choice 2]**

**Q(a)** Derive the balance equations of a **Maxwell Bridge** used to measure an unknown inductance. State the limitations of this bridge. **(7 Marks)**

**Q(b)** A Wheatstone bridge has the following arm resistances: $R_1 = 100 \, \Omega$, $R_2 = 200 \, \Omega$, $R_3 = 150 \, \Omega$, and $R_4 = 300 \, \Omega$. The bridge is energized by a 5 V DC source. Determine:
   (i) Whether the bridge is balanced
   (ii) If not balanced, find the value of $R_4$ that will balance the bridge
   (iii) The Thevenin voltage across the galvanometer terminals when the actual $R_4 = 300 \, \Omega$ **(7 Marks)**

---

### SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Maxwell Bridge Derivation (7 Marks)

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**

Maxwell's bridge measures an unknown inductance $L_x$ with resistance $R_x$ by comparison with a **standard capacitor** $C_4$ (arms 2 and 3 are pure resistors $R_2$ and $R_3$, and arm 4 is a standard capacitor $C_4$ in series with $R_4$).

**Derivation (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — General balance condition:**
$$Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$$

$$(R_x + j\omega L_x)\left(R_4 + \frac{1}{j\omega C_4}\right) = R_2 R_3$$

**Step 2 — Expand:**
$$R_x R_4 + \frac{L_x}{C_4} + j\left(\omega L_x R_4 - \frac{R_x}{\omega C_4}\right) = R_2 R_3$$

**Step 3 — Equate real and imaginary parts to zero separately:**

Real part:
$$R_x R_4 + \frac{L_x}{C_4} = R_2 R_3 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

Imaginary part:
$$\omega L_x R_4 - \frac{R_x}{\omega C_4} = 0$$

$$R_x = \omega^2 L_x R_4 C_4 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve (i) and (ii):**

Substituting (ii) into (i):
$$\omega^2 L_x R_4^2 C_4 + \frac{L_x}{C_4} = R_2 R_3$$

For low/medium Q (where $\omega^2 R_4^2 C_4^2 \ll 1$):
$$\boxed{L_x \approx R_2 R_3 C_4}$$
$$\boxed{R_x = \omega^2 R_2 R_3 R_4 C_4^2}$$

**Limitations of Maxwell Bridge (1 mark):**

1. The bridge is **suitable only for medium Q-factor inductors** (Q between 1 and 10). It gives large errors for high-Q inductors.
2. The balance equations are **frequency-dependent**, requiring a known and stable AC source frequency.
3. The variable standard capacitor must be of **high quality** and **precisely known**.

#### Part (b) — Wheatstone Bridge Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $R_1 = 100 \, \Omega$
- $R_2 = 200 \, \Omega$
- $R_3 = 150 \, \Omega$
- $R_4 = 300 \, \Omega$ (actual)
- $E = 5 \, \text{V}$ (DC source)

**Part (i) — Check Balance (2 marks):**

The balance condition is:
$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_4}$$

**Left side:** $\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{100}{200} = 0.5$

**Right side:** $\frac{R_3}{R_4} = \frac{150}{300} = 0.5$

**Result:** $0.5 = 0.5$ ✓

$$\boxed{\text{The bridge IS balanced.}}$$

**Part (ii) — Required $R_4$ for Balance (1 mark):**

Since the bridge is already balanced at $R_4 = 300 \, \Omega$, no adjustment is needed.

$$\boxed{R_4 = 300 \, \Omega}$$

**Part (iii) — Thevenin Voltage (4 marks):**

Since the bridge is balanced, the Thevenin voltage is **zero** (potential difference between nodes B and D is zero).

**Derivation of $V_{th}$:**

The Thevenin voltage is the open-circuit voltage between the galvanometer terminals. Using voltage divider:

**Voltage at node B (from left side, $R_1$-$R_2$ divider):**
$$V_B = E \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 5 \cdot \frac{200}{100 + 200} = 5 \cdot \frac{200}{300}$$

$$V_B = 5 \cdot \frac{2}{3} = \frac{10}{3} \approx 3.333 \, \text{V}$$

**Voltage at node D (from right side, $R_3$-$R_4$ divider):**
$$V_D = E \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} = 5 \cdot \frac{300}{150 + 300} = 5 \cdot \frac{300}{450}$$

$$V_D = 5 \cdot \frac{2}{3} = \frac{10}{3} \approx 3.333 \, \text{V}$$

**Thevenin voltage:**
$$V_{th} = V_B - V_D = \frac{10}{3} - \frac{10}{3} = 0 \, \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{th} = 0 \, \text{V}}$$

**Conclusion:** With a balanced bridge, the galvanometer current $I_g = V_{th} / (R_{th} + R_g) = 0$, confirming the null condition. This validates the bridge circuit.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. In the balance check, students often **invert the ratio** (writing $R_2/R_1 = R_4/R_3$ instead of $R_1/R_2 = R_3/R_4$). This gives the same numerical answer here by coincidence, but for non-symmetric cases, it would be wrong. Always match the bridge-arm labels with the circuit diagram.
> 2. For the Thevenin voltage, students forget to identify the **correct nodes** for the voltage divider. If the bridge is fed from the top, $V_B$ and $V_D$ must be measured at the **bottom** of the respective arms.
> 3. The Thevenin resistance is asked in some questions; remember:
>    $$R_{th} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} + \frac{R_3 R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3: Instruments**

### 1. Core Instrument Categories
- **Indicating Instruments** display the value at the moment (CRO, DMM).
- **Recording Instruments** store the value over time (X-Y recorders, data loggers).
- **Integrating Instruments** accumulate the value over time (energy meters).

### 2. CRO — Must-Know
- 5 main subsystems: **Electron Gun, Deflection Plates, Phosphor Screen, Time Base Generator, Trigger Circuit**.
- **Voltage** = vertical sensitivity × vertical divisions.
- **Time period** = horizontal sensitivity × horizontal divisions.
- **Phase difference** = (Δx / x) × 360° or (Δt / T) × 360°.
- **Lissajous figures** are used for phase and frequency comparison when input frequencies differ.

### 3. Bridges — Key Differentiators
- **Wheatstone** → Resistance (DC bridge).
- **Maxwell** → Inductance, medium Q (AC bridge, standard capacitor).
- **Hay** → Inductance, high Q (AC bridge, standard capacitor in parallel).
- **Schering** → Capacitance and dissipation factor (AC bridge, used in HV testing).
- **Wien** → Frequency (AC bridge, used in oscillators and audio measurements).

### 4. Q-Meter Essentials
- Definition: $Q = \omega L / R$.
- Measurement: $Q = V_2 / V_1$ at resonance.
- Best for **RF inductors**; measures effective Q including stray losses.

### 5. Static vs. Dynamic Characteristics
- **Static** deals with constant/slowly-varying inputs (accuracy, precision, linearity, drift, sensitivity, resolution, hysteresis, dead zone).
- **Dynamic** deals with rapidly-varying inputs (response time, time constant, bandwidth, fidelity, settling time).

### 6. Common KTU 14-Mark Question Patterns
- Derive the balance equations of a given bridge and solve a numerical.
- Explain CRO operation with block diagram and use it to solve a measurement problem.
- Compare two or more instruments on multiple parameters.
- Q-meter working principle + numerical.

### 7. Common Mistakes to Avoid
- Mixing up **Maxwell** and **Hay** bridge equations.
- Forgetting to convert **µs to seconds** in time/frequency calculations.
- Using **$V_1$ instead of $V_2$** in Q-meter formula.
- Confusing **sensitivity** with **accuracy** in instrument specifications.
- Drawing block diagrams **without arrowheads** (KTU deducts 0.5–1 mark).

### 8. Formulae to Memorize (Numerical Problem Solving)
- $\theta = S_I \cdot I$ (PMMC deflection)
- $V_{pp} = S_v \cdot n_v$ (CRO voltage)
- $T = S_h \cdot n_h$ (CRO time period)
- $\phi = (\Delta x / x) \cdot 360°$ (Phase difference)
- $L_x \approx R_2 R_3 C_4$ (Maxwell bridge)
- $f = 1 / (2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2})$ (Wien bridge)
- $Q = V_2 / V_1$ (Q-meter)

### 9. Real-World Engineering Connect
- **CRO** → R&D, fault diagnosis, signal integrity in embedded systems.
- **Strain gauge bridge** (Wheatstone) → Load cells, pressure transducers.
- **Schering bridge** → Insulation testing of HV cables and equipment.
- **Q-meter** → RF filter design, antenna tuning, inductor quality control.
- **Wien bridge** → Audio frequency generators (Wien bridge oscillator), distortion analyzers.

### 10. Exam Strategy Tips (From a Senior KTU Examiner's Perspective)
- Always **label the axes** in CRO block diagrams (X, Y).
- **State the assumptions** before deriving balance equations (e.g., "Assuming balanced condition with $I_g = 0$...").
- For numerical problems, **write down the formula first**, then substitute values.
- **Unit consistency** is critical — show conversions explicitly.
- For "**compare**" questions, use a **table format** with at least 4–5 parameters to get full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical, Electrical and Electronic Instruments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT205 - TRANSDUCERS & MEASUREMENTS (MECHATRONICS ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mechanical, Electrical and Electronic Instruments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Mechanical Instruments

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Mechanical measuring instruments are devices that employ purely mechanical elements — such as levers, gears, springs, bellows, diaphragms, and manometers — to convert an input physical quantity into a readable displacement or force, without relying on any electrical intermediate signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> In KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3, *mechanical instruments* cover **indicating, recording, and integrating types** used for direct measurement of pressure, force, displacement, temperature, and flow. The focus is on **principle of operation, constructional details, and the mechanical advantage / multiplying factor** produced by lever, gear, and spring systems.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine pushing a heavy door with a small force. A mechanical instrument works the same way — a *small input* (force, pressure, or displacement) is **magnified** by levers, gears, or springs into a *large pointer movement* on a calibrated scale. The instrument itself does the "heavy lifting" so the human eye can read the value easily.

Key mechanical sub-systems (Board-favourite topic):

| Sub-System | Function | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Levers** (simple/compound) | Multiply force or displacement | Weighing balance |
| **Gear trains** | Multiply or reduce rotational speed/torque | Speedometer cable drive |
| **Springs** (helical, leaf, spiral) | Convert force to displacement | Pressure gauge |
| **Bourdon tube** | Convert pressure to displacement | Industrial pressure gauge |
| **Bellows / Diaphragm** | Convert low pressure to large displacement | Aneroid barometer |
| **Manometer** | Convert fluid head to pressure reading | BP apparatus |
| **Dashpot** | Provide damping in pointer motion | Aircraft fuel gauge |

---

### 1.2 Electrical Instruments

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Electrical measuring instruments are devices that use the **electromagnetic effect of current or voltage** to produce a deflecting torque, balancing it against a controlling (spring) torque to give an analog pointer indication on a calibrated scale. The principal categories are PMMC, Moving Iron, Electrodynamometer, Hot-Wire, Thermocouple, Electrostatic, and Induction type instruments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Galvanometer Family:**
> All analog electrical meters are based on the **three-torque equation**:
> $$\theta = \frac{T_d}{T_c} = \frac{T_d}{K_s}$$
> where $T_d$ is the **deflecting torque** (proportional to measurand), $T_c$ is the **controlling torque** (from the spiral spring), and $K_s$ is the **spring constant**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of an electrical instrument as a *muscle that responds to electricity*. When current flows through a coil placed in a magnetic field, the coil twists (like a wrist turning a screwdriver). A tiny spring pulls it back. The *balance point* between magnetic twist and spring resistance is what gives you the reading — a beautifully simple tug-of-war.

The **Standard Symbols** used on instrument nameplates (KTU frequently asked):

- ⊙ — Ammeter
- Ⓥ — Voltmeter
- Ⓦ — Wattmeter
- ⊕ — DC only
- ∿ — AC only
- ⊞ — PMMC (Permanent Magnet Moving Coil)
- ⊟ — MI (Moving Iron)

---

### 1.3 Electronic Instruments

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Electronic instruments are those in which **active semiconductor devices (diodes, BJTs, FETs, op-amps, ICs, ADCs, DACs, microcontrollers)** are used for signal conditioning, amplification, conversion, processing, and digital display of the measurand. They provide **high input impedance, low output impedance, fast response, and high accuracy** over a wide frequency range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 — Electronic Instruments Coverage:**
> 1. **Digital Voltmeter (DVM)** — Ramp, Dual-Slope, Successive Approximation, Integrating type
> 2. **Digital Multimeter (DMM)**
> 3. **Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO)** & **Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO)**
> 4. **Signal & Function Generators**
> 5. **Frequency Counter / Timer-Counter**
> 6. **LCR Meter / Q-Meter**
> 7. **Recorders** — Strip chart, XY, Magnetic tape
> 8. **Data Loggers & Virtual Instrumentation** (LabVIEW brief)

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
If mechanical instruments are the **hands** and electrical instruments are the **muscles**, then electronic instruments are the **brain + nervous system** of measurement. They don't just *move a needle* — they **amplify, filter, convert, compute, store, and display** the signal, often in a digital form you can feed straight into a computer or PLC.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Generations of Instruments (KTU Conceptual Frame):**
> 1. **Mechanical (1st Gen):** Direct physical conversion (Bourdon tube → pointer)
> 2. **Electrical (2nd Gen):** Electrical analogy conversion (current → torque → pointer)
> 3. **Electronic (3rd Gen):** Signal processing + digital display (sensor → op-amp → ADC → LCD)

The three categories are **not rivals** — they are **complementary stages** in the modern measurement chain:
**Sensor → Mechanical Amplifier → Transducer (Electrical) → Electronic Signal Conditioner → Display/Recorder**.

---

### 1.4 The KTU "Big Picture" Classification

| Class | Energy Domain | Typical Output | Typical Accuracy | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Mechanical → Mechanical | Pointer displacement | ±1% to ±5% | Field pressure gauges, manometers |
| **Electrical** | Electrical → Mechanical → Pointer | Pointer + scale | ±0.5% to ±2% | Panel ammeters, voltmeters |
| **Electronic** | Any → Electrical → Digital | Digital display / waveform | ±0.01% to ±0.1% | Lab DVM, CRO, DSO |

The **standard reference value** for instrument performance is **$\text{accuracy} = \pm \frac{\text{max error}}{\text{full-scale deflection}} \times 100\%$**, with **accuracy** always quoted as a **percentage of full-scale (FS) reading**, never of the actual value, for analog meters.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanical Instruments — Operating Principles

#### A. Lever Systems
A **lever** is a rigid bar pivoted at a fulcrum that provides **mechanical advantage (MA)**.

$$\text{MA} = \frac{F_{out}}{F_{in}} = \frac{L_{in}}{L_{out}}$$

where $L_{in}$ and $L_{out}$ are distances from the fulcrum.

- **Simple lever:** one pivot (e.g., beam balance).
- **Compound lever:** multiple pivots in series — total MA is the **product** of individual MAs.
  $$\text{MA}_{total} = \text{MA}_1 \times \text{MA}_2 \times \text{MA}_3$$

**Why & How:** A compound lever overcomes the size limitation of a simple lever when very large mechanical advantage is needed (e.g., platform weighing machines). The trade-off is reduced pointer displacement, hence the **multiplying factor** must be carefully designed.

#### B. Gear Trains
Used to **multiply or reduce** rotation (torque/speed). The **gear ratio** $G$ is:

$$G = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1}$$

where $N$ = number of teeth, $T$ = torque, $\omega$ = angular speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU favourite:** A **speedometer** uses a ratio of roughly **$\frac{1}{1000}$ to $\frac{1}{2500}$** between the rotating wheel and the magnetic drag cup — that's a gear train problem.

#### C. Bourdon Tube
A curved metal tube of **elliptical/oval cross-section**. When internal pressure rises, the tube **tends to straighten**, producing a tip displacement:

$$\Delta x \approx \frac{P \cdot r^3 \cdot a^2}{E \cdot I^2}$$

where $P$ = internal pressure, $r$ = tube radius, $a$ = minor axis, $E$ = Young's modulus, $I$ = area moment of inertia.

**Types:** C-type, spiral, helical (helical gives the largest displacement per unit pressure).

#### D. Diaphragm & Bellows
- **Diaphragm:** Thin circular disc clamped at edges; pressure deflects the centre.
- **Bellows:** Corrugated tube; provides **larger displacement** than a flat diaphragm.

Centre deflection of a circular diaphragm:

$$\Delta x = \frac{3 P R^4 (1 - \nu^2)}{16 E t^3}$$

where $R$ = radius, $t$ = thickness, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio, $E$ = Young's modulus.

#### E. Manometers
- **U-tube:** Difference in column heights $\times$ density $\times g$ = pressure.
  $$P = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_{fluid}) \cdot g \cdot h$$
- **Well-type / Cistern:** Single well, second limb is a reservoir. Sensitivity improved using **inclined tube**:
  $$h_{inclined} = h_{vertical} / \sin\theta$$

#### F. Damping Systems
- **Air damping** (piston in cylinder)
- **Fluid damping** (oil dashpot)
- **Eddy-current damping** (aluminium disc in magnetic field — most common in PMMC)

**KTU-required damping coefficient** for critical damping:

$$\xi = 1 \Rightarrow C_c = 2\sqrt{K \cdot J}$$

where $K$ = spring constant, $J$ = moment of inertia, $C_c$ = critical damping coefficient.

---

### 2.2 Electrical Instruments — Operating Principles

#### A. PMMC (Permanent Magnet Moving Coil) — D'Arsonval Movement
**Deflecting torque** arises from interaction of field flux $B$ with coil current $I$:

$$T_d = B \cdot I \cdot A \cdot N = BINA$$

where $N$ = turns, $A$ = coil area, $N$ = number of turns.

**Pointer deflection** in steady state:

$$\theta = \frac{BINA}{K_s}$$

**Current sensitivity** $S_i$:

$$S_i = \frac{\theta}{I} = \frac{BNA}{K_s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Fact:** PMMC is **polarity-sensitive (DC only)**, has **linear scale**, **high accuracy (0.1% to 2%)**, and **high torque/weight ratio**. A rectifier can be added in front to measure AC (called **MI-rectifier type**).

**Extensions:**
- **Galvanometer → Ammeter:** low-resistance **shunt** $S$ added in parallel.
  $$I_m = I \cdot \frac{S}{R_m + S}$$
  where $R_m$ is meter resistance.
- **Galvanometer → Voltmeter:** high-resistance **multiplier** $R_s$ in series.
  $$V = I_m (R_m + R_s)$$

#### B. Moving Iron (MI) Instruments
- **Attraction type:** Soft-iron plunger pulled into current-carrying solenoid.
- **Repulsion type (more common):** Two iron vanes inside coil, similarly magnetised, repel each other.

**Approximate deflecting torque** (repulsion type):

$$T_d = K_1 \cdot I^2$$

$$\theta = \frac{K_1 I^2}{K_s} - \frac{K_2 I^4}{K_s^2}$$

The second term causes a **non-linear scale** (compressed at low end). KTU repeatedly asks why MI scale is **non-linear at low currents** — answer: due to $K_2 I^4$ term.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU favourite comparison:** PMMC is **DC + linear**, MI is **AC/DC but non-linear scale**, Electrodynamometer is **AC/DC + true RMS reading**.

#### C. Electrodynamometer
Two coils: **fixed** (current) and **moving** (voltage). Deflecting torque:

$$T_d = I_1 I_2 \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$

For wattmeter, $I_1 = I_{load}$, $I_2 = V/R_p$, giving:

$$T_d \propto V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = P$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the dynamometer-type wattmeter a "true RMS" instrument?**
> Because $T_d \propto I_1 I_2 \cdot \cos\phi$, which integrated over a cycle gives **average power = $V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi$** — independent of waveform shape. This is a **KTU 14-marker** favourite.

#### D. Hot-Wire Instrument
Current heats a wire, **expansion** produces pointer deflection.

**Length change** $\Delta L$:

$$\Delta L = \frac{I^2 R L_0}{J \cdot A \cdot S \cdot \rho_c}$$

where $J$ = Joule's constant, $A$ = area, $S$ = specific heat, $\rho_c$ = density.

**Truth:** It reads **true RMS** because heat $\propto I^2$, but it is **slow, fragile, and zero-drifts** — not used in modern labs.

#### E. Thermocouple Instrument
Heater + thermocouple junction; **EMF** $E \propto I^2 R$, then a PMMC reads $E$. Reads **true RMS** for frequencies up to **MHz** range.

#### F. Electrostatic Voltmeter
Force between two charged plates, $F = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \frac{dC}{dx}$. **True RMS**, **very high input impedance**, used for **high-voltage AC measurement**.

#### G. Induction Type
Used only for **AC energy meters** (kWh meters). Aluminium disc experiences **eddy current torque** proportional to $V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$.

---

### 2.3 Electronic Instruments — Operating Principles

#### A. Digital Voltmeter (DVM)
A DVM converts an analog voltage to a digital reading using an **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)**.

**Types (KTU 2024 Module 3 — must know all):**

1. **Ramp-type (Staircase) DVM:** Voltage compared with a linearly rising ramp; time interval measured with a counter.

2. **Dual-Slope Integrating DVM:** (Most popular in lab DMMs)
   - **Phase 1 (Integrate):** Input $V_{in}$ integrated for fixed $T_1$.
   - **Phase 2 (De-integrate):** Reference $-V_{ref}$ integrated until output returns to zero, time $T_2$ measured.
   $$\frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} \Rightarrow V_{in} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

   **Why it is excellent:** Excellent **noise rejection** (rejects mains 50 Hz hum), **high accuracy (0.005%)**, **auto-zeroing**.

3. **Successive Approximation (SAR) ADC:** Fastest type, 8/12/16-bit, used in **digital multimeters** and **data loggers**.

4. **Flash ADC:** Fastest (GHz range), used in **DSOs**.

**Resolution of a DVM:**

$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Question:**
> *"A 4½-digit DVM on 10 V range has resolution = ?"*
> A 4½ digit display → max 19999 → $n$ ≈ 14.32 bits → resolution = $10/(19999) \approx 0.5 \text{ mV}$.

#### B. Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO)
Major sub-systems:
1. **CRT** (Cathode Ray Tube) — electron gun, deflection plates, phosphor screen.
2. **Vertical amplifier** — bandwidth-limited.
3. **Horizontal / Time-base** — sawtooth generator.
4. **Trigger circuit** — synchronises sweep.
5. **Power supply** — EHT (Extra High Tension) up to 25 kV for CRT.

**Deflection sensitivity:**

$$S = \frac{y}{V_y} = \frac{L \cdot l_d}{2 \cdot d \cdot V_a}$$

where $L$ = screen-to-deflector distance, $l_d$ = plate length, $d$ = plate separation, $V_a$ = anode voltage.

**Measurement capabilities:**
- **Voltage:** $V_{p-p} = \text{vertical sensitivity (V/div)} \times \text{divisions}$.
- **Time/Frequency:** $T = \text{time base (s/div)} \times \text{divisions}$.
- **Phase:** $\phi = \frac{\Delta t}{T} \times 360°$ (Lissajous ellipse method).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRO vs DSO (KTU Comparison):**
> - **CRO** = real-time analog display, **bandwidth** up to 500 MHz, no memory.
> - **DSO** = digital, **samples** waveform at high rate, **stores** it, allows post-trigger, FFT, cursors, **bandwidth** up to several GHz.

#### C. Signal Generator / Function Generator
Produces **sine, square, triangular, ramp, pulse** waveforms. Frequency range: **0.01 Hz to 50 MHz** typical. Uses **direct digital synthesis (DDS)** in modern units.

#### D. Frequency Counter
Counts pulses in a **gate time** $T_g$:

$$f_x = \frac{N}{T_g}$$

**Accuracy** depends on **internal reference crystal** (typical $\pm 1$ ppm).

#### E. LCR Meter / Q-Meter
Measures **L, C, R, Q, D, Z** at a known test frequency. Operating principle is **AC bridge balance** or **vector voltmeter technique**.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Comprehensive Formula Table — keep this for the last-day revision.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mechanical advantage of lever | $MA = L_{in}/L_{out}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Compound lever MA | $MA = \prod MA_i$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Gear ratio | $G = N_1/N_2 = T_1/T_2 = \omega_2/\omega_1$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Bourdon tube displacement | $\Delta x \propto P r^3 a^2 / (E I^2)$ | m |
| 5 | Diaphragm centre deflection | $\Delta x = \dfrac{3PR^4(1-\nu^2)}{16Et^3}$ | m |
| 6 | Manometer pressure | $P = \rho g h$ | Pa |
| 7 | PMMC deflecting torque | $T_d = BINA$ | N·m |
| 8 | PMMC pointer deflection | $\theta = BINA/K_s$ | rad |
| 9 | Ammeter shunt | $S = \dfrac{I_m R_m}{I - I_m}$ | Ω |
| 10 | Voltmeter multiplier | $R_s = \dfrac{V}{I_m} - R_m$ | Ω |
| 11 | MI deflecting torque | $T_d = K_1 I^2$ | N·m |
| 12 | Electrodynamometer torque | $T_d = I_1 I_2 \, dM/d\theta$ | N·m |
| 13 | Wattmeter reading | $P = V I \cos\phi$ | W |
| 14 | Hot-wire expansion | $\Delta L \propto I^2$ | m |
| 15 | Electrostatic force | $F = \dfrac{1}{2} V^2 \, dC/dx$ | N |
| 16 | Dual-slope DVM | $V_{in} = V_{ref} T_2/T_1$ | V |
| 17 | DVM resolution | $\Delta V = V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$ | V |
| 18 | Frequency counter | $f = N/T_g$ | Hz |
| 19 | CRO deflection sensitivity | $S = L l_d / (2 d V_a)$ | m/V |
| 20 | Phase from Lissajous | $\sin\phi = a/b$ | rad |
| 21 | Critical damping | $C_c = 2\sqrt{KJ}$ | N·s/m |
| 22 | Spring torque | $T_c = K_s \theta$ | N·m |
| 23 | Pointer deflection (general) | $\theta = T_d / K_s$ | rad |
| 24 | Drift speed (Eddy damping) | $T_d = K_d \cdot \omega$ | N·m |
| 25 | True RMS definition | $I_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T i^2 dt}$ | A |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mechanical instruments** still dominate **field** applications — Bourdon-tube pressure gauges, manometers, thermometers — because they need no power, are intrinsically safe in explosive atmospheres, and survive harsh conditions.
- **Electrical instruments** are the workhorses of **switchboards, control panels, energy meters** in every building, substation, and ship.
- **Electronic instruments** (DVM, DSO, DMM) are the **backbone of every modern lab, production test line, and R&D centre** — they provide data for SCADA, IoT, and Industry 4.0 pipelines.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Deflection of a PMMC Instrument

**Given:** A moving coil with $N$ turns, area $A$, carrying current $I$ in a uniform radial magnetic field $B$, controlled by a spring of constant $K_s$.

**Step 1 — Force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field**

A straight conductor of length $l$ carrying current $I$ in flux density $B$ experiences force:

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot l$$

**Step 2 — Total force on the coil**

The coil has two active sides of length $l$ each, so total peripheral force:

$$F_{total} = B \cdot I \cdot l \cdot 2 = 2 B I l$$

**Step 3 — Convert to torque**

The force acts at the coil's two long sides, which are at radius $r$ (where $A = 2 r l \Rightarrow l = A/(2r)$):

$$T_d = F_{total} \times r = 2 B I l \cdot r = 2 B I \cdot \frac{A}{2r} \cdot r = B I A$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by number of turns $N$**

$$T_d = B \cdot I \cdot N \cdot A = BINA$$

**Step 5 — Equate to controlling spring torque**

The spring produces a restoring torque $T_c = K_s \theta$ where $K_s$ is the spring constant in N·m/rad.

At equilibrium $T_d = T_c$:

$$BINA = K_s \theta$$

**Step 6 — Final pointer deflection**

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{BINA}{K_s}}$$

This is the **fundamental pointer deflection equation** of the PMMC instrument. It is **linear in $I$** because all other quantities are constants for a given meter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's View:** This derivation is **mandatory** for the 14-mark question. Memorise it step-by-step — examiners mark each transition.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Ammeter Shunt Resistance

**Given:** A basic PMMC galvanometer with internal resistance $R_m$ and full-scale current $I_m$. We want to extend its range to measure current $I$ ($I > I_m$).

**Step 1 — Current through shunt**

By KCL, the current through the shunt $S$ is:

$$I_S = I - I_m$$

**Step 2 — Voltage across shunt = Voltage across meter**

The shunt and meter are in parallel, so:

$$V_S = V_m$$

$$I_S \cdot S = I_m \cdot R_m$$

$$(I - I_m) \cdot S = I_m \cdot R_m$$

**Step 3 — Solve for shunt $S$**

$$S = \frac{I_m R_m}{I - I_m}$$

**Step 4 — Multiplication factor $m$**

Define $m = I/I_m$ (the current multiplication factor):

$$S = \frac{R_m}{m - 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU exam question:** *"A 1 mA meter with 100 Ω resistance is to be converted to a 0–100 mA ammeter. Find $S$."*
> $I = 100$ mA, $I_m = 1$ mA, $R_m = 100$ Ω.
> $S = \dfrac{(1\text{ mA})(100\,\Omega)}{(100 - 1)\text{ mA}} = \dfrac{100}{99} \approx 1.0101$ Ω.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Voltmeter Multiplier Resistance

**Given:** Same galvanometer ($R_m$, $I_m$) is to be converted to read voltage $V$ full-scale.

**Step 1 — Total resistance needed**

For full-scale, total resistance $R_{total}$ must limit current to $I_m$:

$$R_{total} = \frac{V}{I_m}$$

**Step 2 — Multiplier**

$$R_s = R_{total} - R_m = \frac{V}{I_m} - R_m$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample:** For 1 mA, 100 Ω meter to read 10 V FS:
> $R_s = 10/0.001 - 100 = 9900$ Ω. **Voltage sensitivity** = $1/R_m = 10\,000$ Ω/V — a figure of merit KTU loves.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Dual-Slope DVM — The Complete Working

**Given:** A dual-slope integrating DVM integrating capacitor $C$, integrator resistor $R$, reference voltage $V_{ref}$.

**Phase 1 — Signal Integration (Run-up)**

Input $V_{in}$ connected for a **fixed time $T_1 = N \cdot T_{clock}$** where $N$ is a counter preset (e.g., 10 000 clocks):

$$V_{out}(T_1) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_0^{T_1} V_{in}\, dt = -\frac{V_{in} \cdot T_1}{RC}$$

**Step 1 — Peak value reached at end of $T_1$**

$$V_{peak} = -\frac{V_{in} T_1}{RC}$$

**Phase 2 — De-integration (Run-down)**

At $t = T_1$, the input is switched to a known **reference $V_{ref}$ of opposite polarity**. The output ramps back to zero in time $T_2$:

$$0 = V_{peak} - \frac{1}{RC}\int_{T_1}^{T_1+T_2} V_{ref}\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $T_2$**

$$V_{peak} = \frac{V_{ref} \cdot T_2}{RC}$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate $V_{peak}$ and $RC$**

$$\frac{V_{in} T_1}{RC} = \frac{V_{ref} T_2}{RC}$$

**Step 4 — Final result**

$$\boxed{V_{in} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1}}$$

**Why is this beautiful?**

- $RC$ **cancels out** — the conversion is **independent** of capacitor and resistor drift.
- $T_1$ is **fixed** (derived from a stable clock), so $V_{in}$ depends only on **$T_2$ (counted) and $V_{ref}$ (a stable band-gap reference)**.
- 50 Hz mains noise integrates to **zero** over a full cycle — hence **infinite noise rejection** at mains frequency and its harmonics.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Critical Damping of a Moving System

**Given:** A pointer of moment of inertia $J$, restrained by a spring of constant $K$, and damped by a coefficient $C$.

The equation of motion is:

$$J \ddot{\theta} + C \dot{\theta} + K\theta = T_{in}(t)$$

**Step 1 — Standard second-order form**

Divide by $J$:

$$\ddot{\theta} + \frac{C}{J}\dot{\theta} + \frac{K}{J}\theta = \frac{T_{in}(t)}{J}$$

**Step 2 — Natural frequency and damping ratio**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{K/J} \quad,\quad \xi = \frac{C}{2\sqrt{KJ}}$$

**Step 3 — Critical damping condition**

$\xi = 1 \Rightarrow C_c = 2\sqrt{KJ}$

**Step 4 — Behaviour zones**

| Damping | $\xi$ | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| Under-damped | $\xi < 1$ | Pointer oscillates before settling |
| Critically damped | $\xi = 1$ | Fastest non-overshoot |
| Over-damped | $\xi > 1$ | Sluggish |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU board expectation:** State the condition for critical damping **and** explain why most analog meters are designed for **slightly under-damped** ($\xi \approx 0.6$–$0.7$) so the user sees a *small* overshoot and reaches final value quickly.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Three Generations of Measurement Instruments

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Quantity: P, T, F, L] --> B[Mechanical Sensor Stage]
    B --> C[Mechanical Amplifier: Lever, Gear, Spring]
    C --> D[Pointer on Calibrated Scale]
    D --> E[Analog Readout - 1st Gen]
    A --> F[Transducer Stage]
    F --> G[Electrical Sensor PMMC MI Dynamometer]
    G --> H[Pointer on Scale]
    H --> I[Analog Readout - 2nd Gen]
    A --> J[Electronic Sensor Stage]
    J --> K[Signal Conditioner Op amp Filter]
    K --> L[ADC Converter]
    L --> M[Microcontroller DSP]
    M --> N[Digital Display LCD 7 segment]
    N --> O[Digital Readout - 3rd Gen]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — PMMC Instrument Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Current I enters coil] --> B[Magnetic Field B from permanent magnet]
    B --> C[Deflecting Torque Td on coil]
    C --> D[Pointer rotates through angle theta]
    D --> E[Spiral spring applies restoring Torque Tc]
    E --> F{Steady state reached?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Final deflection theta = BINA over Ks]
    F -->|No| C
    G --> H[Eddy current damping in aluminum former]
    H --> I[Stable pointer position on scale]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Dual-Slope DVM Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start conversion command] --> B[Reset counter to zero]
    B --> C[Switch connects Vin to integrator]
    C --> D[Integrator output ramps for fixed T1]
    D --> E[Counter counts up to N cycles then issues overflow]
    E --> F[Switch flips to negative Vref]
    F --> G[Integrator output ramps back to zero]
    G --> H[Comparator detects zero crossing]
    H --> I[Counter value N2 is read]
    I --> J[Digital display: Vin = Vref times N2 over N1]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — CRO / DSO Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input signal applied to Y input] --> B[Vertical attenuator]
    B --> C[Vertical amplifier]
    C --> D[Y deflection plates in CRT]
    E[Time base sawtooth generator] --> F[X amplifier]
    F --> G[X deflection plates in CRT]
    H[Trigger circuit samples input] --> I{Sync achieved?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Sweep starts]
    I -->|No| H
    J --> E
    D --> K[Electron beam deflected]
    G --> K
    K --> L[Phosphor screen shows waveform]
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Induction-Type Energy Meter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Supply voltage V applied to voltage coil] --> B[Flux phi1 through shunt magnet]
    C[Load current I through current coil] --> D[Flux phi2 through series magnet]
    B --> E[Aluminum disc experiences induced EMF and eddy currents]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Driving torque Td proportional to V I cos phi]
    F --> G[Disc rotates against braking magnet]
    G --> H[Braking torque Tb proportional to disc speed]
    H --> I{Steady speed reached?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Counting mechanism integrates rotations]
    J --> K[Energy reading in kWh]
```

### 4.6 Functional Architecture — Modern DSO with Signal Conditioning Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Transducer output mV range] --> B[Front end attenuator]
    B --> C[Low noise pre amplifier]
    C --> D[Anti aliasing low pass filter]
    D --> E[Flash ADC high speed sampler]
    E --> F[Acquisition memory buffer]
    F --> G[Trigger and time base circuit]
    G --> H[Display processor with FFT and math]
    H --> I[Color LCD display]
    H --> J[USB Ethernet GPIB output to PC]
```

### 4.7 Functional Architecture — Measurement Chain Integration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Mechanical: Bourdon manometer] --> S[Sensor: measures physical quantity]
    P2[Electrical: PMMC] --> S
    P3[Electronic: Strain gauge] --> S
    S --> SC[Signal conditioner: amp, filter, linearize]
    SC --> TR[Transmitter: 4 to 20 mA]
    TR --> RC[Receiver: DMM, DSO, PLC]
    RC --> DI[Display or recorder]
    DI --> AN[Analysis software and IoT cloud]
```
