# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**"List and briefly explain the three primary functions of a measurement system."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
The three primary functions of a measurement system are:

1. **Indicating Function:** The system displays the instantaneous value of the measurand in a human-perceivable form. For example, a pressure gauge on a compressor shows the current pressure on a calibrated dial.

2. **Recording Function:** The system stores the measured values over time to enable trend analysis, troubleshooting, and regulatory compliance. For example, a chart recorder in a pharmaceutical batch reactor logs temperature over a 24-hour cycle.

3. **Controlling Function:** The system provides a feedback signal to an automatic controller that maintains the measurand at a desired set-point. For example, a thermostat in an air-conditioner measures room temperature and switches the compressor on/off to maintain the user-defined temperature.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**"Define a generalized measurement system and name any four of its elements."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
A **Generalized Measurement System (GMS)** is a conceptual framework representing any instrument as a cascade of functional elements, each performing a specific signal-processing operation from sensing the measurand to presenting the result.

Any four of its elements are:
1. **Primary Sensing Element (Sensor)** — directly detects the measurand.
2. **Variable Conversion Element** — converts the sensor's output into electrical form.
3. **Variable Manipulation Element** — amplifies and filters the signal.
4. **Data Presentation Element** — displays or records the final value.

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH — INTERNAL CHOICE)

### Question A — Option (i): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q.A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the elements of a generalized measurement system. Describe the function of each element with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:**
*(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in SECTION 4.1 of these notes. A student should redraw it manually in the exam using a rectangular-box convention, labeling each block clearly.)*

**Element-wise Explanation:**

1. **Primary Sensing Element (Sensor):**
   This is the first element that comes in direct contact with the measurand. It converts a physical quantity (temperature, pressure, force) into a more usable signal, usually a change in resistance, capacitance, voltage, or another electrical parameter.
   *Example:* A **thermocouple** converts temperature (°C) into a small DC voltage (mV) based on the Seebeck effect. A **strain gauge** converts mechanical strain (dimensionless ratio) into a change in electrical resistance.

2. **Variable Conversion Element:**
   The output of the sensor is often too weak or in a non-standard form to be processed further. This element converts it into a standardized electrical signal, typically a voltage.
   *Example:* A **Wheatstone bridge** circuit converts the small change in strain gauge resistance into a proportional differential voltage output. A **charge amplifier** converts the charge from a piezoelectric sensor into a voltage.

3. **Variable Manipulation Element:**
   The signal from the conversion element is usually small in magnitude and contaminated with noise. This element amplifies, filters, integrates, or differentiates the signal to make it suitable for transmission and display.
   *Example:* An **instrumentation amplifier** (e.g., AD620) amplifies the millivolt-level bridge output to a 0–5 V range. A **low-pass filter** removes 50 Hz power-line noise.

4. **Data Transmission Element:**
   This element transmits the conditioned signal from the measurement site to a remote display or control station. It may use wires, fiber optics, or wireless links.
   *Example:* A **4–20 mA current loop** is widely used in process industries because it is immune to cable resistance variations. **HART protocol** superimposes a digital signal on the same loop.

5. **Data Presentation Element:**
   The final element presents the measured data to the user or a downstream process. It can be analog (pointer, pen) or digital (LCD, computer screen).
   *Example:* A **7-segment LCD** on a digital thermometer shows the temperature in °C. A **chart recorder** plots temperature versus time on a moving paper strip.

6. **Control Element (Optional):**
   In automated systems, the displayed value is fed back to a controller, which compares it with the set-point and actuates a final control element.
   *Example:* A **PID controller** in a furnace system compares the measured temperature with the set-point and adjusts the heater power.

---

**Q.A (b)** Discuss any **four major applications** of measurement systems in mechatronics and industrial automation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Application 1: Robotic Arm Joint Position Control (Mechatronics)**
A 6-DOF robotic arm uses **encoders** and **resolvers** at each joint to measure angular position. The measurement system provides real-time feedback to the motor controllers, enabling precise path tracking and pick-and-place operations in a manufacturing cell.

**Application 2: Automotive Engine Control Unit (ECU)**
Modern cars use a network of sensors — **MAF (Mass Air Flow) sensor**, **MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure) sensor**, **oxygen sensor**, and **crankshaft position sensor** — to monitor engine parameters. The ECU processes these measurements in real-time to optimize fuel injection, ignition timing, and emission control, satisfying stringent Euro-6 / BS-VI norms.

**Application 3: Industrial Boiler Drum Level Control (Process Control)**
A thermal power plant uses a **differential pressure transmitter** to measure the water level in a boiler drum. The signal is conditioned, transmitted to the control room, displayed on the HMI, and fed to a PID controller that modulates a feedwater control valve to maintain a constant water level — critical for safe and efficient steam generation.

**Application 4: CNC Machine Tool Quality Control (Manufacturing)**
A **Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM)** and **laser displacement sensors** are used to verify the dimensional accuracy of machined parts. The measurement system compares the actual dimensions with CAD nominal values and provides statistical process control (SPC) data, enabling closed-loop compensation of tool wear.

**Application 5 (Bonus, if asked for the 4th in detail):** Biomedical Instrumentation
**ECG and pulse oximeter** systems measure the electrical activity of the heart and blood oxygen saturation. These measurements are critical for patient monitoring in ICUs, enabling early detection of arrhythmias and hypoxia.

---

### Question B — Option (ii): [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Q.B (a)** Define the term **measurand**. With a block diagram, describe the function of each element of a generalized measurement system, and explain how the overall system sensitivity is computed. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Measurand:**
The **measurand** is the physical quantity (or property) of a body or system that is subjected to measurement. Examples include temperature, pressure, force, displacement, voltage, current, and flow rate. The measurand is the **input** to the measurement system, and the system produces an output that is a meaningful representation of the measurand.

**Block Diagram:** *(Draw the 6-element block diagram as in SECTION 4.1.)*

**Overall System Sensitivity Derivation:**

Let the transfer function (sensitivity) of each element be:

$$S_1 = \frac{q_1}{q_{\text{in}}}, \quad S_2 = \frac{q_2}{q_1}, \quad S_3 = \frac{q_3}{q_2}, \quad S_4 = \frac{q_4}{q_3}, \quad S_5 = \frac{q_5}{q_4}$$

By cascading (multiplying the individual sensitivities):

$$S_{\text{overall}} = S_1 \cdot S_2 \cdot S_3 \cdot S_4 \cdot S_5 = \frac{q_5}{q_{\text{in}}}$$

**Example:** Consider a temperature measurement system where:
- Sensor sensitivity $S_1 = 0.04\,\text{mV/°C}$ (thermocouple type K).
- Bridge gain $S_2 = 100\,\text{V/V}$ (Wheatstone bridge + amplifier).
- Filter gain $S_3 = 1$ (unity-gain filter).
- Transmission gain $S_4 = 0.98$ (slight loss over 200 m cable).
- Display scaling $S_5 = 50\,\text{°C/V}$ (analog meter scaling).

Then:

$$S_{\text{overall}} = 0.04 \times 100 \times 1 \times 0.98 \times 50 = 196\,\text{°C/°C}$$

This means a 1 °C change in temperature produces a 196 units of pointer deflection on the display (which is then calibrated to read directly in °C).

---

**Q.B (b)** Explain the **indicating, recording, and controlling** functions of a measurement system with one real-world example for each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Function 1: Indicating Function**
This function involves the *real-time display* of the measurand. The output is shown in either analog (pointer and scale) or digital (numerical) form.
*Real-World Example:* The **speedometer of a car** continuously indicates the vehicle's instantaneous speed. The driver reads the dial and adjusts the accelerator accordingly.

**Function 2: Recording Function**
This function involves the *long-term storage* of measurement data for later analysis, regulatory compliance, or process optimization.
*Real-World Example:* A **seismograph** in a geological observatory records ground vibrations continuously. The recorded data on magnetic tape or cloud servers is used by geologists to study earthquakes, volcanic activity, and underground nuclear tests.

**Function 3: Controlling Function**
This function involves the *automatic regulation* of a process variable using the measured value as feedback. The measurement system is integrated with a controller and an actuator in a closed loop.
*Real-World Example:* An **inverter-type air conditioner** measures room temperature using a thermistor, compares it with the user-set temperature, and uses a PID algorithm to vary the compressor speed to maintain the set-point. This closed-loop control saves energy compared to on-off control.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the three functions with the elements of a measurement system.** Students often mix up *functions* (indicating, recording, controlling) with *elements* (sensor, converter, conditioner, transmitter, display). The functions describe *what* the system does, while the elements describe *how* it does it.
> 2. **Omitting the conversion element.** When sketching the block diagram, students frequently draw only 3 or 4 boxes, skipping the variable conversion element. The 6-element model is the gold standard; a 3-element model is acceptable only if explicitly labeled.
> 3. **Not labeling signal domains.** Always annotate each block with the *input* and *output* domains (e.g., "°C → mV") to gain an extra mark for clarity.
> 4. **Forgetting the feedback loop in the controlling function.** A controlling function is *incomplete* without mentioning the set-point, error signal, and feedback path. Merely writing "it controls the process" is too vague and costs 2-3 marks.
> 5. **Writing applications in a generic way.** Instead of "used in industries," write "used in pharmaceutical batch reactors for FDA-compliant temperature logging." Specific, domain-named examples score higher.
> 6. **Mismeasuring Sensitivity.** Do not confuse *system sensitivity* (output/input ratio) with *sensor sensitivity* (output of sensor/measurand). The overall system sensitivity is the *product* of all individual element sensitivities.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 3**

- [ ] A **measurement system** converts an unknown physical quantity (measurand) into a meaningful, observable output.
- [ ] The **three primary functions** are **Indicating, Recording, and Controlling**.
- [ ] A **Generalized Measurement System** consists of up to **6 elements**: Sensor, Variable Converter, Variable Manipulator, Data Transmission, Data Presentation, and (optional) Control Element.
- [ ] **Sensitivity** of an element is the ratio of output change to input change; the **overall system sensitivity** is the product of all individual element sensitivities.
- [ ] The **Primary Sensing Element** is the only element that physically contacts the measurand; all subsequent elements process the *signal*, not the *physical quantity*.
- [ ] **Applications** span industrial process control, automotive, aerospace, biomedical, robotics, environmental, and consumer electronics domains.
- [ ] In **closed-loop control**, the measurement system provides feedback to a controller, which computes an error signal and drives an actuator.
- [ ] The **4–20 mA current loop** is the industry-standard transmission method due to its noise immunity over long cable runs.
- [ ] The **controlling function** differentiates a measurement system from a mere monitoring system; it is the gateway to automation and Industry 4.0.
- [ ] Always **draw a labeled block diagram** in 14-mark questions; a diagram without labels and example signals loses 2-3 marks.
- [ ] **Use specific real-world examples** (e.g., "strain gauge in a weighbridge" instead of "sensor in industry").
- [ ] **KTU 2024 Scheme pattern** for Module 3 typically asks one 7-mark question on this topic as part of a 14-mark module question; prepare both a definitional answer and a derivational answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of Instruments and Measurement Systems: Measurement System Performance, Static Calibration, Static Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT205 - TRANSDUCERS & MEASUREMENTS (MECHATRONICS ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Characteristics of Instruments and Measurement Systems: Measurement System Performance, Static Calibration, Static Characteristics # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Measurement System? A **Measurement System** is a combination of instruments, devices, and associated apparatus assembled and arranged to perform a complete measurement of one or more physical quantities. According to **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCMRT205)**, it is treated as a functional chain of elements that converts a physical input into a readable, quantifiable output. > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):** > *"A measurement system is an assembly of physical elements (sensing, conditioning, processing, and display elements) organized in a sequence such that the quantity under measurement is sensed, converted, and presented in a usable form to the observer."* ### 1.2 The Three Functional Stages of a Measurement System Every measurement system, regardless of complexity, can be decomposed into three fundamental stages: | Stage | Function | Typical Element | |---|---|---| | **Primary Sensing Element** | Makes first contact with the measurand | Thermocouple, Strain gauge, Bourdon tube | | **Intermediate / Variable Conversion Element** | Manipulates the signal (amplification, filtering, modulation) | Amplifier, ADC, Bridge circuit | | **Output / Termination Element** | Presents the measurement to the observer | CRO, Digital display, Recorder | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Bathroom Weighing Scale" Model Imagine you step on a **digital weighing scale**. Let us map this familiar device to the formal KTU terminology: - **You (the body weight)** → This is the **Measurand** (the physical quantity you want to measure). - **The load cell inside the scale** → This is the **Primary Sensor** (converts weight into a tiny electrical voltage). - **The internal microchip that amplifies the signal** → This is the **Variable Conversion Element**. - **The LCD display showing "72.5 kg"** → This is the **Output Element**. Now, think about the questions you ask about the scale: - *"Is 72.5 kg my true weight?"* → **Accuracy** - *"If I step off and on five times, will I get 72.5 each time?"* → **Precision / Repeatability** - *"Will the scale show 74 kg if I gain 1.5 kg?"* → **Sensitivity** - *"Can it read 0.05 kg changes?"* → **Resolution** - *"Will it show 73 kg tomorrow morning on an empty stomach?"* → **Drift / Hysteresis** Every one of these questions maps to a **Static Characteristic** that the KTU 2024 syllabus requires you to know. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction for KTU 2024:** > - **Static Characteristics** deal with the steady-state behaviour of the instrument (input is constant or varies very slowly). > - **Dynamic Characteristics** deal with the time-varying response (covered in Module 4 of PCMRT205). > - The topic in this module is purely **static**, meaning the measurand is assumed to be **time-invariant** during observation. ## 1.4 Performance of a Measurement System **Measurement System Performance** is a composite descriptor that quantifies *how well* a system performs its intended measurement function. It is evaluated through two broad categories: 1. **Static Performance** — assessed when the input is steady (this module). 2. **Dynamic Performance** — assessed when the input is time-varying (Module 4). Static performance is numerically expressed using parameters such as **Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, Linearity, Repeatability, Resolution, Hysteresis, and Drift**. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Static Calibration — The Foundation of Every Measurement **Static Calibration** is the process of comparing the output of an instrument against a **known standard reference input** under **static (no time variation)** conditions, in order to establish, verify, or correct the instrument's output-input relationship. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 — Definition to Memorise:** > *"Static calibration is the experimental procedure in which known input values (traceable to national/international standards) are applied to the measurement system under steady-state conditions, and the corresponding output readings are recorded to determine the static characteristics of the system."* ### 2.1.1 The Static Calibration Procedure (Step-by-Step) 1. **Select the Reference Standard** — Choose a calibrated, traceable standard of higher accuracy than the instrument under test (IUT). For example, a dead-weight tester for a pressure gauge. 2. **Zero Adjustment** — With zero input applied, adjust the IUT to read exactly zero. 3. **Apply Known Inputs** — Apply a series of known input values, evenly spaced, covering the full range of the instrument. 4. **Record Output Readings** — For each input, record the output, approaching from **both directions** (ascending and descending runs) to capture hysteresis. 5. **Plot the Calibration Curve** — Graph of output (Y-axis) vs. input (X-axis). 6. **Compute Static Characteristics** — Derive sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis, etc. from the curve. 7. **Document** — Record environmental conditions (temperature, humidity) since they affect calibration. ### 2.1.2 Calibration Curve — Ideal vs Real - **Ideal Calibration Curve:** A straight line passing through the origin with a slope equal to the design sensitivity. The output is a perfect, scaled replica of the input. - **Real Calibration Curve:** Deviates from the ideal due to **systematic errors, manufacturing tolerances, friction, hysteresis, and environmental effects**. ## 2.2 Static Characteristics — The Complete KTU Inventory Below is the comprehensive treatment of every static characteristic in the **KTU 2024 PCMRT205 syllabus**. ### 2.2.1 Range and Span - **Range** — The minimum and maximum values of the measurand for which the instrument is designed to operate. Stated as: $0$ to $100^{\circ}\text{C}$. - **Span (Full-Scale Input, FSI)** — The algebraic difference between the maximum and minimum input values. $$\text{Span} = \text{Maximum Input} - \text{Minimum Input}$$ ### 2.2.2 Accuracy **Accuracy** is the closeness of an instrument's reading to the **true value** of the quantity being measured. $$\text{Error} = \text{Measured Value} - \text{True Value}$$ $$\% \text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{True Value} - \vert \text{Error} \vert}{\text{True Value}} \times 100$$ $$\text{Static Sensitivity} = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{dY}{dX}$$ ### 2.2.3 Precision **Precision** is the degree of **mutual agreement** (closeness) among a series of **repeated measurements** of the same quantity under the same conditions. An instrument can be **precise but inaccurate** (e.g., a misaligned but stable scale). ### 2.2.4 Sensitivity (K) **Sensitivity** is the ratio of the change in output (response) to the corresponding change in input (stimulus) producing it. $$K = \frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X} \quad \text{(units: output units per input unit)}$$ For a linear instrument, the sensitivity is constant. For a non-linear instrument, sensitivity varies with the operating point and is given by the **local slope** of the calibration curve. ### 2.2.5 Linearity (and Non-Linearity) A measurement system is **linear** when its calibration curve is a **straight line**. **Non-linearity** is the maximum deviation of the calibration curve from the best-fit straight line. $$\% \text{Non-linearity} = \frac{(\text{Max deviation of actual curve from best-fit line})}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$$ where $Y_{FS}$ is the full-scale output reading. ### 2.2.6 Repeatability **Repeatability** is the ability of an instrument to give the **same output** when the **same input** is applied repeatedly under the **same conditions** (same observer, same instrument, same environment, same procedure). $$\% \text{Repeatability} = \frac{\Delta R_{max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$$ where $\Delta R_{max}$ is the maximum difference between any two readings at the same input. ### 2.2.7 Reproducibility **Reproducibility** is the ability of the instrument to give the same output for the same input when **conditions are varied** (different observer, different instrument of the same type, different location, different time, different ambient conditions). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip — Do not confuse Repeatability and Reproducibility:** > - **Repeatability** = same conditions → same result. > - **Reproducibility** = different conditions → same result. Reproducibility is always ≤ Repeatability. ### 2.2.8 Resolution **Resolution** is the **smallest change in the input (or output)** that can be detected and meaningfully displayed by the instrument. Example: A digital multimeter that displays 3 decimal places (e.g., 12.345 V) has a resolution of **0.001 V**. ### 2.2.9 Threshold **Threshold** is the **minimum input value** required to produce a detectable change in the output. If the input is below the threshold, the output remains zero (or its initial value). ### 2.2.10 Dead Zone / Dead Band **Dead Zone** is the range of input values over which the instrument produces **no change in output**. The output is "dead" (insensitive) within this band. Caused by backlash, friction, or play in mechanical linkages. ### 2.2.11 Hysteresis **Hysteresis** is the difference in output for the **same input**, depending on whether the input is **increasing (ascending) or decreasing (descending)**. $$\% \text{Hysteresis Error} = \frac{H_{max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100 = \frac{\max(Y_{up} - Y_{down})}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$$ ### 2.2.12 Drift **Drift** is the change in output over a period of time for a **constant input**, under constant environmental conditions. Sub-types: - **Zero Drift** — Output changes at zero input. - **Span Drift / Sensitivity Drift** — Slope of the calibration curve changes. - **Thermal Drift** — Caused by temperature variations (often the dominant source). ### 2.2.13 Static Error and Static Correction - **Static Error** = Indicated Value − True Value (at a single operating point, under static conditions). - **Static Correction** = − Static Error (the quantity to be added to the indicated value to obtain the true value). ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — it covers 90% of the numerical questions asked in KTU 2024 ESE on this topic.** | # | Characteristic | Formula | Typical Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Span** | $S = X_{max} - X_{min}$ | Same as measurand unit | | 2 | **Static Error** | $e_s = Y_{indicated} - Y_{true}$ | Output unit | | 3 | **Static Correction** | $c_s = -e_s$ | Output unit | | 4 | **Accuracy** | $\%Acc = \frac{Y_{true} - \vert e_s \vert}{Y_{true}} \times 100$ | % | | 5 | **Sensitivity** | $K = \dfrac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X}$ | Output/Input unit | | 6 | **% Non-linearity** | $\dfrac{\Delta Y_{max,NL}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$ | % | | 7 | **% Hysteresis** | $\dfrac{H_{max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$ | % | | 8 | **% Repeatability** | $\dfrac{\Delta R_{max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$ | % | | 9 | **% Reproducibility** | $\dfrac{\Delta R'_{max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$ | % | | 10 | **Resolution** | Smallest detectable $\Delta X$ or $\Delta Y$ | Input/Output unit | | 11 | **Drift** | $\dfrac{Y(t_2) - Y(t_1)}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$ | % per unit time | | 12 | **Loading Error** | $e_L = \dfrac{Z_{input}}{Z_{input} + Z_{measuring}} \times 100$ | % | | 13 | **Best-fit Slope (LSM)** | $m = \dfrac{N\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{N\sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2}$ | — | | 14 | **Best-fit Intercept (LSM)** | $b = \dfrac{\sum y - m \sum x}{N}$ | — | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Application of Static Characteristics | |---|---| | **Aerospace** | Calibration of altimeters, pressure transducers — sensitivity and linearity are critical. | | **Medical Devices** | Sphygmomanometers, infusion pumps — accuracy and resolution directly affect patient safety. | | **Automotive** | MAP/MAF sensors, fuel injectors — repeatability under varying temperature = reproducibility. | | **Process Industries** | Flow, level, temperature transmitters in refineries — span & range define process control loops. | | **Metrology Labs** | Reference standard calibration — traceability chain is built on static calibration. | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Static Sensitivity from the Calibration Curve **Given:** A measurement system produces output $Y$ for input $X$, related by a deterministic function $Y = f(X)$. For a static measurement, both are time-independent. **Step 1 — Define the Output-Input Relationship at a Point** At a specific operating point $X = X_0$, the output is: $$Y_0 = f(X_0)$$ **Step 2 — Apply a Small Increment** Apply a small input increment $\Delta X$, so the input becomes $X_0 + \Delta X$. The output changes to: $$Y_0 + \Delta Y = f(X_0 + \Delta X)$$ **Step 3 — Expand Using Taylor Series (1st Order)** Since $\Delta X$ is small and $f$ is differentiable (a basic requirement for a usable measurement system), use the first-order Taylor expansion: $$f(X_0 + \Delta X) \approx f(X_0) + f'(X_0) \cdot \Delta X$$ **Step 4 — Substitute Back** $$Y_0 + \Delta Y = Y_0 + f'(X_0) \cdot \Delta X$$ **Step 5 — Cancel $Y_0$ from Both Sides** $$\Delta Y = f'(X_0) \cdot \Delta X$$ **Step 6 — Define Sensitivity as the Local Slope** By definition, the **static sensitivity** $K$ is the ratio $\Delta Y / \Delta X$ at that point: $$K = \frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X} = f'(X_0) = \left. \frac{dY}{dX} \right|_{X = X_0}$$ **Step 7 — Special Case — Linear Instrument** If $f(X) = K_0 X + c$ (a straight line), then $f'(X_0) = K_0$ (constant). Hence the sensitivity is the same at every point — the calibration curve is a straight line through slope $K_0$. > **Engineering Interpretation:** For a *linear* instrument, $K$ is the slope of the calibration curve (constant). For a *non-linear* instrument (e.g., a thermistor), $K$ varies — this is why non-linear sensors need **linearisation circuits** in the intermediate stage. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Best-Fit Straight Line via the Method of Least Squares (Calibration Curve Fitting) This is the **most important derivation for KTU 2024** — used to determine the best-fit straight line from calibration data points, and hence compute sensitivity and linearity. **Given:** $N$ calibration data points $(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_N, y_N)$. The goal is to fit a straight line: $$y = mx + b$$ such that the **sum of squares of the vertical deviations** of the data points from the line is **minimised**. **Step 1 — Define the Error (Residual) at Each Point** The vertical distance from the $i^{th}$ data point to the line is: $$e_i = y_i - (m x_i + b) = y_i - m x_i - b$$ **Step 2 — Define the Objective Function** Sum of squares of all residuals: $$S(m, b) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} e_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - m x_i - b)^2$$ **Step 3 — Minimise S with Respect to m** Set $\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial m} = 0$: $$\frac{\partial S}{\partial m} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} 2(y_i - m x_i - b)(-x_i) = 0$$ $$\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i y_i) - m \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2 - b \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = 0$$ **Step 4 — Minimise S with Respect to b** Set $\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial b} = 0$: $$\frac{\partial S}{\partial b} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} 2(y_i - m x_i - b)(-1) = 0$$ $$\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i - m \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i - N b = 0$$ **Step 5 — Form the Two Normal Equations** Let us introduce compact notation: $\Sigma x = \sum x_i$, $\Sigma y = \sum y_i$, $\Sigma x^2 = \sum x_i^2$, $\Sigma xy = \sum x_i y_i$. Equation from Step 3: $$\Sigma xy - m \Sigma x^2 - b \Sigma x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad m \Sigma x^2 + b \Sigma x = \Sigma xy \quad \text{...(I)}$$ Equation from Step 4: $$\Sigma y - m \Sigma x - N b = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad m \Sigma x + N b = \Sigma y \quad \text{...(II)}$$ **Step 6 — Solve by Cramer's Rule (or elimination)** Solving the simultaneous linear equations (I) and (II) for $m$: $$m = \frac{N \Sigma xy - \Sigma x \Sigma y}{N \Sigma x^2 - (\Sigma x)^2}$$ Solving for $b$: $$b = \frac{\Sigma y - m \Sigma x}{N} = \frac{\Sigma x^2 \Sigma y - \Sigma x \Sigma xy}{N \Sigma x^2 - (\Sigma x)^2}$$ **Step 7 — Interpretation in Calibration Context** - $m$ = **best-fit sensitivity** $K$ of the calibrated system (output per unit input). - $b$ = **zero-offset** or intercept (output when input is zero). - Once $m$ and $b$ are known, the **deviation of any actual point** $(x_i, y_i)$ from the best-fit line is: $$\Delta y_i = y_i - (m x_i + b)$$ - The **maximum** $\vert \Delta y_i \vert$ gives the **non-linearity** of the system. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hysteresis Error Analysis **Given:** An instrument is subjected to an input that is increased in steps (ascending run) and then decreased in steps (descending run). For each input value $x_i$, two outputs are recorded: $y_{i,up}$ and $y_{i,down}$. **Step 1 — Define the Hysteresis at a Point** $$\Delta H_i = y_{i,up} - y_{i,down}$$ This is the vertical gap between the two runs at input $x_i$. By convention, $\Delta H_i$ may be positive or negative. **Step 2 — Identify the Maximum Hysteresis** $$H_{max} = \max_i \vert \Delta H_i \vert = \max_i \vert y_{i,up} - y_{i,down} \vert$$ This is the **worst-case hysteresis band** across the full range. **Step 3 — Express as a Percentage of Full-Scale Output** $$\% \text{Hysteresis} = \frac{H_{max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$$ where $Y_{FS}$ is the full-scale output (output at maximum input). **Step 4 — Worked Numerical Anchor (for KTU exam practice)** Suppose for a pressure gauge: $Y_{FS} = 100$ units. Ascending and descending readings at 5 bar give $y_{up} = 49.2$ and $y_{down} = 48.0$. Then: $$H_{max} = 49.2 - 48.0 = 1.2 \text{ units}$$ $$\% \text{Hysteresis} = \frac{1.2}{100} \times 100 = 1.2\%$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Characteristic | Engineering Situation | Primary Static Characteristic to Evaluate | |---|---| | Sensor used in a closed-loop control system | **Sensitivity** (gain of the loop) | | Pressure gauge for safety-critical application | **Accuracy + Hysteresis** | | Multiple operators use the same instrument | **Reproducibility** | | Same operator repeats measurement quickly | **Repeatability** | | Sensor output connected to a data logger | **Resolution** (LSB of ADC) | | Instrument left overnight, input constant | **Drift** (especially zero drift) | | Mechanical linkage with gears | **Dead Band / Backlash** | | Calibrating against a dead-weight tester | **Linearity** (deviation from reference line) | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generalised Measurement System (KTU Standard) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Measurand
Physical Quantity X] --> B[Primary Sensing
Element] B --> C[Variable Conversion
Element] C --> D[Variable Manipulation
Element] D --> E[Data Transmission
Element] E --> F[Output / Display
Element Y] F --> G[Observer /
Data Acquisition] H[Reference Standard] -.-> B style A fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#333 style F fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333 style H fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#333 ``` > The **dashed line** from "Reference Standard" represents the static calibration step — the standard's known input is applied to the sensing element to verify/correct the output. ## 4.2 Static Calibration Procedure Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Start: Select IUT
and Reference Standard] --> A2[Zero Adjust
at Zero Input] A2 --> A3[Apply Ascending
Input Steps X1 X2 ... Xn] A3 --> A4[Record Outputs
Yup1 Yup2 ... Yupn] A4 --> A5[Apply Descending
Input Steps Xn ... X2 X1] A5 --> A6[Record Outputs
Ydn1 Ydn2 ... Ydnn] A6 --> A7{Plot Calibration
Curve Y vs X} A7 --> A8[Compute Slope m
and Intercept b
via Least Squares] A8 --> A9[Derive Static Characteristics
Sensitivity, Linearity, Hysteresis
Repeatability, Drift] A9 --> A10[Document and
Issue Calibration Certificate] style A1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333 style A10 fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.3 Taxonomy of Static Characteristics (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB SC[Static Characteristics
of a Measurement System] --> P1[Input-Side
Descriptors] SC --> P2[Output-Side
Descriptors] SC --> P3[Cross-Comparison
Descriptors] P1 --> Q1[Range] P1 --> Q2[Span] P1 --> Q3[Resolution] P1 --> Q4[Threshold] P1 --> Q5[Dead Zone] P2 --> R1[Sensitivity] P2 --> R2[Linearity] P2 --> R3[Drift] P3 --> S1[Accuracy] P3 --> S2[Precision] P3 --> S3[Repeatability] P3 --> S4[Reproducibility] P3 --> S5[Hysteresis] P3 --> S6[Static Error] P3 --> S7[Static Correction] style SC fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P1 fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#333 style P2 fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#333 style P3 fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.4 Typical Static Calibration Curve (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Curve["CALIBRATION CURVE - Y vs X"] direction TB P1((Origin 0,0)) --> P2[Ascending Run
Y goes up smoothly] P2 --> P3[Full Scale
Y = Yfs] P3 --> P4[Descending Run
Y comes down] P4 --> P5[Back to Origin
may not be 0,0] P5 -. Hysteresis gap .-> P1 end L1[Ideal Straight Line
Y = K_ideal X] -. Best Fit .-> Curve style L1 fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#333 style P3 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333 ``` > The **Hysteresis gap** between ascending and descending runs is the principal visual signature of energy-dissipative elements (friction, magnetic hysteresis, elastic hysteresis) in the instrument. ## 4.5 Loading Effect — Interaction Between Source and Instrument ```mermaid flowchart LR Src[Source Impedance
Zs] --- A[Terminal A] A --- Inst[Instrument
Input Impedance Zi] Inst --- B[Terminal B] B --- Gnd[Ground] Src -. True Voltage Vtrue across terminals .-> A Inst -. Measured Voltage Vm = Vtrue x Zi/(Zs+Zi) .-> Inst style Src fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#333 style Inst fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333 ``` > **Loading Error** (in %): $e_L = \dfrac{V_{true} - V_m}{V_{true}} \times 100 = \dfrac{Z_s}{Z_s + Z_i} \times 100$. This is why a **voltmeter must have very high input impedance** (large $Z_i$) — to keep $e_L$ small. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Q: Define the following static characteristics of a measurement system: (i) Accuracy, (ii) Precision, (iii) Sensitivity. State one distinguishing feature for each.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks — Awarded as 1 + 1 + 1): **(i) Accuracy [1 mark]:** Accuracy is the degree of closeness of an instrument's measured value to the **true value** of the measurand. It is quantified by the static error or by the percentage accuracy: $$\% \text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{True Value} - \vert \text{Error} \vert}{\text{True Value}} \times 100$$ **Distinguishing feature:** Compares measured value to a *true* (absolute) value. **(ii) Precision [1 mark]:** Precision is the degree of **mutual agreement** among a series of repeated measurements of the same quantity under identical conditions. **Distinguishing feature:** Compares measurements *among themselves* — independent of the true value. A set of measurements can be precise but inaccurate (e.g., a faulty zero shift). **(iii) Sensitivity [1 mark]:** Sensitivity $K$ is the ratio of the change in output to the change in input producing it: $$K = \frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X}$$ **Distinguishing feature:** It is the **slope** of the calibration curve. For linear instruments, $K$ is a constant. --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q: Distinguish clearly between Repeatability and Reproducibility. Which is always larger, and why?** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): | Aspect | Repeatability | Reproducibility | |---|---|---| | **Conditions** | Same — same instrument, observer, environment, method | Different — varied conditions | | **Definition** | Ability to give same output for same input under same conditions | Ability to give same output for same input under different conditions | | **Spread** | Smaller scatter | Larger scatter | | **Magnitude** | Always ≥ Reproducibility | Always ≤ Repeatability | **Key Reason [1 mark]:** When conditions are kept identical, only random fluctuations affect the reading. When conditions change, additional sources of variation (temperature, observer bias, different instrument of the same type, drift) contribute, increasing the scatter. Hence **repeatability is always better (larger %) than reproducibility**. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PCMRT205 Module 3]** **(a)** Define static calibration of a measurement system. With a neat block diagram, explain the general procedure of static calibration. State the significance of using a reference standard of higher accuracy. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Define the terms: **(i) Hysteresis, (ii) Dead Zone, (iii) Drift**. A pressure gauge with a range of **0 to 10 bar** gives the following ascending and descending readings: | True Input (bar) | 0 | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Ascending Output (units) | 0 | 19 | 41 | 60 | 80 | 100 | | Descending Output (units) | 0 | 20 | 42 | 61 | 81 | 100 | Calculate the **% hysteresis** of the gauge. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** **Definition [2 marks]:** Static calibration is the experimental procedure in which known input values (traceable to national/international standards) are applied to the measurement system under **steady-state (time-invariant) conditions**, and the corresponding output readings are recorded. The output-input data are then used to construct the calibration curve and to evaluate the static characteristics of the system. **Procedure [3 marks] — refer to the flowchart in Section 4.2 of these notes:** 1. Select a reference standard of known, higher accuracy (≥ 4× better than the instrument under test — the "4:1 accuracy ratio" rule). 2. Zero-adjust the instrument at zero input. 3. Apply known input values in ascending order, recording the output at each step. 4. Apply the same inputs in descending order, recording the outputs. 5. Plot the calibration curve (Output vs Input). 6. Compute the slope, intercept (via least-squares if required), and all static characteristics. 7. Document the environmental conditions and issue a calibration certificate. **Block Diagram [1 mark]:** Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes. **Significance of higher-accuracy standard [1 mark]:** If the reference standard is not significantly more accurate than the IUT, the calibration result will be dominated by the standard's own error, leading to an invalid traceability chain. The 4:1 ratio ensures that the standard's contribution to total measurement uncertainty is < 25% of the IUT's, making it acceptable per ISO/IEC 17025. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** **Definitions [3 marks — 1 each]:** - **Hysteresis [1 mark]:** The difference in output for the same input value, depending on whether the input is increasing (ascending) or decreasing (descending). Caused by friction, backlash, magnetic/elastic hysteresis in the instrument. - **Dead Zone [1 mark]:** The range of input values over which there is **no change in output**. The output remains constant (often zero) for inputs below the dead-zone threshold. - **Drift [1 mark]:** The change in output of an instrument over a period of time for a **constant input**, under constant ambient conditions. May be zero-drift, span-drift, or thermal drift. **% Hysteresis Calculation [4 marks]:** Compute $\Delta H_i = y_{i,up} - y_{i,down}$ for each input: | Input (bar) | $y_{up}$ | $y_{down}$ | $\Delta H = y_{up} - y_{down}$ | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | 2 | 19 | 20 | −1 | | 4 | 41 | 42 | −1 | | 6 | 60 | 61 | −1 | | 8 | 80 | 81 | −1 | | 10 | 100 | 100 | 0 | Maximum absolute deviation: $H_{max} = 1$ unit. Full-scale output: $Y_{FS} = 100$ units (at 10 bar). $$\% \text{Hysteresis} = \frac{H_{max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100 = \frac{1}{100} \times 100 = 1\%$$ **Answer: The pressure gauge exhibits 1% hysteresis.** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PCMRT205 Module 3]** **(a)** Define the following terms and state the formula for each: **(i) Static Sensitivity, (ii) Linearity (Non-linearity), (iii) Static Error**. Briefly explain why an ideal measurement system is preferred to be linear. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** The following calibration data were obtained for a temperature transducer (input in °C, output in mV): | Input $x$ (°C) | 0 | 20 | 40 | 60 | 80 | 100 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Output $y$ (mV) | 0 | 21 | 41 | 62 | 79 | 101 | Determine the **best-fit straight line** $y = mx + b$ using the method of least squares, and hence compute the **sensitivity** and **zero error** of the transducer. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** **(i) Static Sensitivity [2 marks]:** The static sensitivity $K$ of a measurement system is the ratio of the change in output to the change in input at a specific operating point: $$K = \frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X} = \left. \frac{dY}{dX} \right|_{X = X_0}$$ Units: output unit per input unit (e.g., mV/°C). **(ii) Linearity / Non-linearity [2 marks]:** Linearity refers to the degree to which the calibration curve approximates a **straight line**. Non-linearity is the maximum deviation of the actual curve from the best-fit straight line: $$\% \text{Non-linearity} = \frac{\Delta Y_{max, NL}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$$ where $\Delta Y_{max, NL}$ is the maximum deviation and $Y_{FS}$ is the full-scale output. **(iii) Static Error [2 marks]:** Static error is the algebraic difference between the indicated (measured) value and the true value, measured under static conditions: $$e_s = Y_{indicated} - Y_{true}$$ Static correction $c_s = -e_s$. **Why linearity is preferred [1 mark]:** A linear system has **constant sensitivity** across the entire range, which simplifies calibration (single scale factor), avoids the need for linearisation circuits, and yields a predictable, easy-to-interpret output. Non-linear instruments require look-up tables, polynomial fitting, or analogue linearising circuits, increasing complexity and cost. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** **Step 1 — Tabulate the data and compute required sums [2 marks]:** | $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i^2$ | $x_i y_i$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | 2 | 20 | 21 | 400 | 420 | | 3 | 40 | 41 | 1600 | 1640 | | 4 | 60 | 62 | 3600 | 3720 | | 5 | 80 | 79 | 6400 | 6320 | | 6 | 100 | 101 | 10000 | 10100 | | **Sum** | **300** | **304** | **22000** | **22200** | So: $N = 6$, $\Sigma x = 300$, $\Sigma y = 304$, $\Sigma x^2 = 22000$, $\Sigma xy = 22200$. **Step 2 — Compute the slope $m$ [2 marks]:** $$m = \frac{N \Sigma xy - \Sigma x \Sigma y}{N \Sigma x^2 - (\Sigma x)^2} = \frac{(6)(22200) - (300)(304)}{(6)(22000) - (300)^2}$$ Compute the numerator: $$6 \times 22200 = 133200, \quad 300 \times 304 = 91200$$ $$\text{Numerator} = 133200 - 91200 = 42000$$ Compute the denominator: $$6 \times 22000 = 132000, \quad (300)^2 = 90000$$ $$\text{Denominator} = 132000 - 90000 = 42000$$ $$m = \frac{42000}{42000} = 1.000 \text{ mV/°C}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the intercept $b$ [1 mark]:** $$b = \frac{\Sigma y - m \Sigma x}{N} = \frac{304 - (1.000)(300)}{6} = \frac{304 - 300}{6} = \frac{4}{6} \approx 0.667 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 4 — Interpret the Results [2 marks]:** - **Best-fit straight line:** $y = 1.000\,x + 0.667$ (mV, with $x$ in °C) - **Sensitivity:** $K = m = 1.000$ mV/°C - **Zero error:** At $x = 0$, the best-fit output should be 0 mV but the transducer actually indicates (according to the fit) $0.667$ mV. Hence **static zero error = +0.667 mV**. > The transducer is **highly linear** (slope comes out to almost exactly 1.000) with a small **positive zero offset** of about 0.67 mV. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 PCMRT205 Module 3 (Static Characteristics):** > 1. **Confusing "Span" with "Range"** — Span is the *difference* (max − min); Range is the *interval* (e.g., 0 to 100). Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks. > 2. **Using span instead of full-scale output** in the denominator of % hysteresis, % non-linearity, and % repeatability. The denominator **must be $Y_{FS}$ (output at maximum input)**, NOT the input span. > 3. **Forgetting to take absolute values** when computing % accuracy or % hysteresis — always use $\vert \text{Error} \vert$ and $\vert \Delta H_i \vert$. > 4. **Confusing "precision" with "accuracy"** in definitions. Use the word "true value" for accuracy and "mutual agreement / scatter" for precision. > 5. **In least-squares problems, forgetting to use $N$ (the number of data points) in the formula** — a single missed $N$ gives the wrong slope and zero. > 6. **Not stating units** in the final answer (e.g., sensitivity must carry units like mV/°C). KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for unit omission. > 7. **Writing the calibration curve equation** as $y = mx + b$ but calling $m$ the "intercept" and $b$ the "slope" — these are routinely swapped by students. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Know for KTU 2024 ESE (PCMRT205 / Module 3):** - ☐ **Span** = Max input − Min input. **Range** = interval over which the instrument works. - ☐ **Static error** = Indicated value − True value. **Static correction** = − Static error. - ☐ **% Accuracy** uses the true value in the denominator; **% non-linearity / hysteresis / repeatability** use $Y_{FS}$ in the denominator. - ☐ **Sensitivity** $K = \Delta Y / \Delta X$ — slope of the calibration curve. For linear systems, $K$ is constant. - ☐ **Resolution** is the smallest *detectable* change; **Threshold** is the smallest input that *initiates* a detectable output. - ☐ **Repeatability** = same conditions; **Reproducibility** = different conditions. Repeatability is always ≥ Reproducibility. - ☐ **Hysteresis** = difference between ascending and descending runs. Caused by friction, backlash, magnetic/elastic effects. - ☐ **Drift** = change in output over time for a constant input. Sub-types: zero drift, span drift, thermal drift. - ☐ **Dead Zone** = input range over which output does not change. - ☐ **Static Calibration** requires a reference standard of higher accuracy (≥ 4:1 accuracy ratio recommended). Procedure: zero adjust → ascending run → descending run → plot → fit → compute characteristics. - ☐ **Least-Squares Formulae** (must memorise): $m = \dfrac{N\Sigma xy - \Sigma x \Sigma y}{N\Sigma x^2 - (\Sigma x)^2}$ and $b = \dfrac{\Sigma y - m \Sigma x}{N}$. - ☐ **Loading effect** is an interaction error due to finite instrument input impedance; minimised by making $Z_i \gg Z_s$ (source impedance). - ☐ Units are **mandatory** in the final numerical answer — KTU examiners actively deduct for missing units. - ☐ **Static characteristics** apply when the measurand is time-invariant. For time-varying inputs, the syllabus shifts to **dynamic characteristics** (covered in Module 4: zero-order, first-order, second-order systems).

topics.title: Errors in Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT205 - TRANSDUCERS & MEASUREMENTS (MECHATRONICS ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Errors in Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition:**
> An **Error in Measurement** is defined as the *deviation of the measured value from the true value (or actual value)* of the measurand. Mathematically, it is expressed as:
> $$\text{Error} (E) = \text{Measured Value } (A_m) - \text{True Value } (A_t)$$

Where the **true value** is the theoretically exact value that cannot practically be obtained, but is approached by averaging an infinite number of measurements. In engineering practice, we refer to the **conventional true value** — a value obtained from a high-precision, calibrated reference instrument traceable to national/international standards (e.g., **BIPM, NPL, NIST**).

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Measurement Quality

| Performance Index | Definition | Formula |
|-------------------|------------|---------|
| **Accuracy** | Closeness of measurement to the *true value* | $\text{Accuracy} = 1 - \frac{\vert E \vert}{A_t}$ |
| **Precision** | Closeness of two or more measurements to *each other* (Repeatability) | Statistical (Std. Deviation based) |
| **Sensitivity** | Ratio of change in output to change in input | $S = \frac{\Delta q_o}{\Delta q_i}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A measurement can be **precise but not accurate**, and **accurate but not precise**. The ideal instrument is both highly accurate and highly precise.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Archery Target

Imagine a target representing the **true value**:

- **Accurate & Precise**: All arrows tightly clustered at the bullseye.
- **Precise but Inaccurate**: All arrows tightly clustered, but away from the bullseye (systematic bias exists).
- **Accurate but Imprecise**: Arrows scattered around the bullseye (random errors dominate).
- **Neither**: Arrows scattered and far from the bullseye.

This analogy is the **single most commonly asked 3-mark question** in KTU exams. The bullseye = true value, arrow cluster = measured values, and the arrow's deviation = error.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Measurement Errors

The KTU 2024 syllabus divides errors into **three broad categories** with a critical sub-classification tree.

### 2.1.1 Gross Errors (Human Errors)

These arise from the **human factor** — carelessness, mistakes, or misinterpretation.

- **Misreading the instrument** (e.g., reading 21.4 instead of 31.4 on a scale).
- **Incorrect recording** of data.
- **Improper application** of correction factors.
- **Mistakes in transposition** of numbers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these matter:** Gross errors cannot be treated mathematically. They are detected and eliminated by **careful observation**, **re-reading**, and **double-entry of data** by a second observer.

### 2.1.2 Systematic Errors (Deterministic Errors)

These are **reproducible** and follow a definite physical law. They are predictable in magnitude and sign.

**Sub-classification:**

1. **Instrumental Errors**
   - **Zero Error**: Pointer not at zero when no input is applied. (Fixed offset, can be removed by *zero adjustment*.)
   - **Scale Error**: Imperfect graduation on the scale.
   - **Hysteresis Error**: Different readings during ascending vs. descending input.
   - **Aging Error**: Drift in component values over time (e.g., strain gauge fatigue, spring relaxation).
   - **Loading Error**: Disturbance of the measurand by the act of measurement itself (e.g., a heavy voltmeter loading a low-impedance circuit).

2. **Environmental Errors**
   - Effect of **temperature, humidity, pressure, vibration**, or stray **magnetic/electric fields** on the instrument.
   - Compensation methods: Use of *thermostatic chambers, magnetic shielding, vibration isolators, and temperature compensation circuits*.

3. **Observational Errors**
   - **Parallax Error**: Apparent shift in pointer position due to oblique viewing angle. Eliminated by using *mirror-backed scales*.
   - **Interpolation Error**: Estimating between smallest scale divisions by eye.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Systematic errors are **reducible but not eliminable**. They can be corrected by calibration against a higher-grade standard, or by applying correction curves/equations supplied by the manufacturer.

### 2.1.3 Random Errors (Residual Errors)

These are **unpredictable**, **untraceable**, and follow the laws of **probability and statistics**. They arise from:

- **Noise** in transducers and electronics.
- **Fluctuations** in environmental parameters beyond our control.
- **Quantization error** in digital instruments (e.g., a **12-bit ADC** has a quantization step of $\frac{FSR}{2^{12}}$).

Random errors are analyzed using:
- **Arithmetic Mean**
- **Standard Deviation**
- **Gaussian (Normal) Distribution**

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for the KTU university exam. Memorize each row.

| # | Concept | Governing Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Absolute Error | $E = A_m - A_t$ | Same as measurand |
| 2 | Relative Error | $\varepsilon_r = \frac{\vert E \vert}{A_t} = \frac{\Delta A}{A}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 3 | Percentage Error | $\varepsilon_{\%} = \varepsilon_r \times 100$ | Expressed in % |
| 4 | Sum: $R = A + B \pm C$ | $\Delta R = \pm \Delta A \pm \Delta B \pm \Delta C$ | Absolute errors add |
| 5 | Difference: $R = A - B$ | $\Delta R = \pm \Delta A \pm \Delta B$ | Absolute errors add |
| 6 | Product: $R = A \cdot B$ | $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pm \frac{\Delta A}{A} \pm \frac{\Delta B}{B}$ | Relative errors add |
| 7 | Quotient: $R = \frac{A}{B}$ | $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pm \frac{\Delta A}{A} \pm \frac{\Delta B}{B}$ | Relative errors add |
| 8 | Power: $R = A^n$ | $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pm n \frac{\Delta A}{A}$ | Multiplied by exponent |
| 9 | General: $R = f(A,B,C,...)$ | $\Delta R = \pm \left( \frac{\partial R}{\partial A}\Delta A + \frac{\partial R}{\partial B}\Delta B + \frac{\partial R}{\partial C}\Delta C \right)$ | **Most general form** |
| 10 | Limiting (Guaranteed) Error | $\varepsilon_L = \pm \frac{\Delta A_{\max}}{A} \times 100\%$ | Specified by manufacturer |
| 11 | Arithmetic Mean | $\bar{A} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | Best estimate of true value |
| 12 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i^2}{n-1}}$ | $d_i = A_i - \bar{A}$ (deviation) |
| 13 | Variance | $\sigma^2$ | Square of std. deviation |
| 14 | Probable Error | $PE = 0.6745\,\sigma$ | 50% probability |
| 15 | Probable Error of Mean | $PE_m = 0.6745\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | As $n \to \infty$, $PE_m \to 0$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE:** In all the above formulas, $\Delta A$, $\Delta B$, $\Delta C$ denote the **maximum absolute errors** in $A$, $B$, and $C$ respectively. The $\pm$ sign is **NOT** to be summed algebraically; the *worst-case (limiting)* scenario is taken by adding the magnitudes.

## 2.3 Statistical Analysis of Random Errors

When a measurement is repeated $n$ times yielding values $A_1, A_2, ..., A_n$, the random error analysis proceeds as follows:

**Step 1 — Compute the Arithmetic Mean:**
The mean is the **most probable value** of the measurand since it minimizes the sum of squared deviations.

$$
\bar{A} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Deviations:**
The deviation of the $i^{th}$ reading is:

$$
d_i = A_i - \bar{A}
$$

A key identity: $\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i = 0$.

**Step 3 — Compute Standard Deviation:**
The standard deviation is the **measure of precision** of the instrument.

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (A_i - \bar{A})^2}{n-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum d_i^2}{n-1}}
$$

The denominator $n-1$ is called the **degrees of freedom** and is used because one degree is "lost" in estimating the mean $\bar{A}$.

**Step 4 — Gaussian Distribution:**
The probability density function of the random error $x = A_m - \bar{A}$ is:

$$
f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left( -\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)
$$

**Probability Interpretations:**
- 68.27% of readings lie within $\pm 1\sigma$ of the mean.
- 95.45% of readings lie within $\pm 2\sigma$ of the mean.
- 99.73% of readings lie within $\pm 3\sigma$ of the mean.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Error Analysis |
|-------|-------------------------------|
| **Instrumentation** | Calibration certificates specify limiting errors. |
| **Metrology** | Quality control in manufacturing (CMM, gauge R&R). |
| **Process Control** | Propagating transmitter errors through control loops. |
| **Aerospace & Defense** | Sensor fusion in IMUs requires error covariance modeling. |
| **Medical Devices** | ECG, infusion pumps — patient safety requires tight error bounds. |
| **Renewable Energy** | Wind turbine power estimation: $P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ propagates wind speed error cubed. |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Error Propagation: General Function $R = f(A, B, C)$

**Statement:** Given an experimental function $R = f(A, B, C)$ and absolute errors $\Delta A$, $\Delta B$, $\Delta C$ in the independent variables, derive the absolute error $\Delta R$ in the result $R$.

**Starting Point:** Let the measured values be $A + \Delta A$, $B + \Delta B$, $C + \Delta C$. The corresponding value of $R$ is $R + \Delta R$ where:

$$
R + \Delta R = f(A + \Delta A,\; B + \Delta B,\; C + \Delta C)
$$

**Step 1 — Apply Taylor Series Expansion (truncated to first order):**

$$
R + \Delta R = f(A, B, C) + \frac{\partial f}{\partial A}\Delta A + \frac{\partial f}{\partial B}\Delta B + \frac{\partial f}{\partial C}\Delta C + \text{higher order terms}
$$

**Step 2 — Neglect higher-order terms** (valid when $\Delta A$, $\Delta B$, $\Delta C$ are infinitesimally small).

$$
R + \Delta R = f(A, B, C) + \frac{\partial f}{\partial A}\Delta A + \frac{\partial f}{\partial B}\Delta B + \frac{\partial f}{\partial C}\Delta C
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $R = f(A, B, C)$** and cancel from both sides:

$$
\Delta R = \frac{\partial f}{\partial A}\Delta A + \frac{\partial f}{\partial B}\Delta B + \frac{\partial f}{\partial C}\Delta C
$$

**Step 4 — Worst-case (limiting) error:** Since the signs of $\Delta A$, $\Delta B$, $\Delta C$ are unknown, take the absolute value of each term to get the **maximum possible error**:

$$
\Delta R = \pm \left( \left\vert \frac{\partial f}{\partial A} \right\vert \Delta A + \left\vert \frac{\partial f}{\partial B} \right\vert \Delta B + \left\vert \frac{\partial f}{\partial C} \right\vert \Delta C \right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most general formula** for error propagation. The specific cases (sum, product, power) are merely special cases obtained by choosing $f$ accordingly. Always reproduce this derivation in 7-mark KTU questions.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Special Case: Power Function $R = A^n$

**Step 1:** Apply the general formula with $f(A) = A^n$, so $\frac{dR}{dA} = nA^{n-1}$.

$$
\Delta R = nA^{n-1} \cdot \Delta A
$$

**Step 2:** Divide both sides by $R = A^n$ to get relative error:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{nA^{n-1} \cdot \Delta A}{A^n} = n \cdot \frac{\Delta A}{A}
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_r(R) = n \cdot \varepsilon_r(A)}
$$

**Interpretation:** A quantity raised to the $n^{th}$ power has its *relative error multiplied by $n$*. This is why the wind power formula $P \propto v^3$ magnifies a 5% error in wind speed into a 15% error in power — a critical concern in wind farm energy forecasting.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Product of Two Quantities $R = A \cdot B$

**Step 1:** Compute partial derivatives: $\frac{\partial R}{\partial A} = B$, $\frac{\partial R}{\partial B} = A$.

**Step 2:** Substitute into general formula:

$$
\Delta R = B \cdot \Delta A + A \cdot \Delta B
$$

**Step 3:** Divide both sides by $R = AB$:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{B \cdot \Delta A}{AB} + \frac{A \cdot \Delta B}{AB} = \frac{\Delta A}{A} + \frac{\Delta B}{B}
$$

**Step 4 — Worst-case scenario:** Sum the absolute values:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pm \left( \frac{\Delta A}{A} + \frac{\Delta B}{B} \right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_r(R) = \varepsilon_r(A) + \varepsilon_r(B)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Relative errors always add in products and quotients.** This is a recurring KTU exam result.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Sum of Two Quantities $R = A + B$

**Step 1:** Compute partial derivatives: $\frac{\partial R}{\partial A} = 1$, $\frac{\partial R}{\partial B} = 1$.

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$
\Delta R = \Delta A + \Delta B
$$

**Step 3 — Worst case:**

$$
\Delta R = \pm (\Delta A + \Delta B)
$$

**Conclusion:** **Absolute errors add in sums and differences.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common Mistake:** Students add *relative errors* in sums. The correct rule is: **Absolute errors add in sums/differences; relative errors add in products/quotients.** Many students lose marks on this distinction.

## 3.5 Worked Example — Resistance from V and I Measurements

> **Problem (KTU Style):** The resistance of a resistor is computed using Ohm's law $R = \frac{V}{I}$. A voltmeter reads $V = 100 \pm 0.5$ V and an ammeter reads $I = 5 \pm 0.1$ A. Find the resistance and its limiting absolute and percentage errors.

**Step 1 — Compute nominal value:**

$$
R = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{100}{5} = 20 \;\Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the quotient rule for relative error:**

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pm \left( \frac{\Delta V}{V} + \frac{\Delta I}{I} \right) = \pm \left( \frac{0.5}{100} + \frac{0.1}{5} \right)
$$

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pm (0.005 + 0.020) = \pm 0.025
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to percentage:**

$$
\varepsilon_{\%}(R) = \pm 2.5\%
$$

**Step 4 — Absolute error:**

$$
\Delta R = 0.025 \times 20 = 0.5 \;\Omega
$$

**Step 5 — Final answer:**

$$
\boxed{R = 20 \pm 0.5 \;\Omega \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad R = 19.5 \text{ to } 20.5 \;\Omega}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** The ammeter contributes $\pm 2\%$ error while the voltmeter contributes only $\pm 0.5\%$. To improve accuracy, one should use a **more precise ammeter**, not a more precise voltmeter.

## 3.6 Worked Example — Power in a Resistive Load

> **Problem (KTU Style):** Power dissipated in a resistor is $P = V \cdot I$. A voltmeter reads $240 \pm 1.2$ V and an ammeter reads $10 \pm 0.2$ A. Find the power and its percentage error.

**Step 1 — Nominal power:**

$$
P = V \times I = 240 \times 10 = 2400 \;\text{W}
$$

**Step 2 — Product rule:**

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{P} = \pm \left( \frac{\Delta V}{V} + \frac{\Delta I}{I} \right) = \pm \left( \frac{1.2}{240} + \frac{0.2}{10} \right)
$$

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{P} = \pm (0.005 + 0.020) = \pm 0.025 = \pm 2.5\%
$$

**Step 3 — Absolute error in power:**

$$
\Delta P = 0.025 \times 2400 = 60 \;\text{W}
$$

**Step 4 — Final answer:**

$$
\boxed{P = 2400 \pm 60 \;\text{W}}
$$

## 4.2 Error Analysis Functional Flow

This diagram maps the analytical workflow for determining the error in any computed physical quantity:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    I1[Step 1 Identify the Functional Relationship R equals f of A B C] --> I2[Step 2 Determine the Absolute Errors dA dB dC from Instrument Specs]
    I2 --> I3{Step 3 Is the Function a Sum or Difference}
    I3 -- Yes --> I4[Apply Absolute Error Addition Rule dR equals dA plus dB]
    I3 -- No --> I5{Step 4 Is the Function a Product Quotient or Power}
    I5 -- Yes --> I6[Apply Relative Error Addition Rule eR equals eA plus eB]
    I5 -- No --> I7[Step 5 Apply General Partial Derivative Formula]
    I4 --> I8[Step 6 Convert to Percentage if Required]
    I6 --> I8
    I7 --> I8
    I8 --> I9[Step 7 Report Final Result as R plus or minus dR]
```

## 4.3 Statistical Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
graph LR
    J1[Take n Repeated Readings A1 to An] --> J2[Compute Arithmetic Mean Abar]
    J2 --> J3[Compute Deviations di equals Ai minus Abar]
    J3 --> J4[Compute Sum of Squared Deviations]
    J4 --> J5[Apply Formula sigma equals sqrt of sum di squared over n minus 1]
    J5 --> J6[Identify Instrument Precision]
    J5 --> J7[Apply Gaussian Distribution for Probability]
    J6 --> J8[Final Statistical Report]
    J7 --> J8
```

```mermaid
    K1 --> L2[Case 2 Cluster away from Center equals Precise but Inaccurate Systematic Error Dominant]
    K1 --> L3[Case 3 Scattered around Center equals Accurate but Imprecise Random Error Dominant]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Accuracy and Precision with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Accuracy | Precision |
|---------|----------|-----------|
| **Definition** | Closeness of a measured value to the true value | Closeness of two or more measurements to each other |
| **Affected by** | Systematic errors | Random errors |
| **Quantified by** | Absolute or relative error | Standard deviation |
| **Example** | A voltmeter reading 99.8 V when the true value is 100 V is **accurate** | The same voltmeter giving 99.8 V, 99.81 V, 99.79 V on three trials is **precise** |
| **Real-life** | A clock showing correct time at 12:00 noon | A clock gaining exactly 5 seconds every day, consistently |

> A measurement can be precise without being accurate (e.g., a miscalibrated instrument gives the same wrong reading repeatedly).

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain briefly the three types of systematic errors.**

**Model Answer:**

Systematic errors are **reproducible** and **deterministic**. They are classified as:

1. **Instrumental Errors** — Caused by imperfections in the instrument itself: zero error, hysteresis, aging of components, improper calibration, or loading effect.
2. **Environmental Errors** — Caused by external conditions: temperature drift, humidity, atmospheric pressure, stray magnetic or electrostatic fields.
3. **Observational Errors** — Caused by the observer: parallax while reading a pointer, interpolation between scale divisions, or misreading the scale.

Systematic errors can be **minimized** by proper calibration, selecting suitable instruments, using better techniques, and applying correction factors. They are characterized by a **non-zero mean** of repeated readings.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note on KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each 14-mark question carries **two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each**. Sub-part (a) is typically a *definition/derivation*; sub-part (b) is a *numerical application* or *comparison*.

---

### **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the general expression for the propagation of errors for a function $R = f(A, B, C)$. Hence, deduce the limiting error expressions for **(i)** $R = A + B - C$ and **(ii)** $R = A^n B^m / C^p$.

#### Model Solution

**General Derivation:**

Let $R + \Delta R = f(A + \Delta A, B + \Delta B, C + \Delta C)$.

Expanding via Taylor series to first order:

$$
R + \Delta R = f(A, B, C) + \frac{\partial f}{\partial A}\Delta A + \frac{\partial f}{\partial B}\Delta B + \frac{\partial f}{\partial C}\Delta C
$$

Since $R = f(A, B, C)$:

$$
\boxed{\Delta R = \pm\left( \left\vert\frac{\partial f}{\partial A}\right\vert \Delta A + \left\vert\frac{\partial f}{\partial B}\right\vert \Delta B + \left\vert\frac{\partial f}{\partial C}\right\vert \Delta C \right)}
$$

**Special Case (i): $R = A + B - C$**

Partial derivatives: $\frac{\partial R}{\partial A} = 1$, $\frac{\partial R}{\partial B} = 1$, $\frac{\partial R}{\partial C} = -1$.

$$
\Delta R = \pm(\Delta A + \Delta B + \Delta C)
$$

**Special Case (ii): $R = \frac{A^n B^m}{C^p}$**

Taking the natural log: $\ln R = n \ln A + m \ln B - p \ln C$.

Differentiating:

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = n\frac{dA}{A} + m\frac{dB}{B} - p\frac{dC}{C}
$$

Limiting form (worst case):

$$
\boxed{\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pm\left( n\frac{\Delta A}{A} + m\frac{\Delta B}{B} + p\frac{\Delta C}{C} \right)}
$$

This is a **highly tested derivation** in KTU.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A resistance is measured using the voltmeter-ammeter method. The voltmeter reads $V = 50 \pm 0.25$ V and the ammeter reads $I = 2 \pm 0.02$ A. Calculate **(i)** the resistance, **(ii)** the power dissipated, and **(iii)** the maximum percentage error in each.

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Resistance Calculation:**

$$
R = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{50}{2} = 25 \;\Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Power Calculation:**

$$
P = V \cdot I = 50 \times 2 = 100 \;\text{W}
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Error in Resistance (Quotient Rule):**

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pm\left( \frac{\Delta V}{V} + \frac{\Delta I}{I} \right) = \pm\left( \frac{0.25}{50} + \frac{0.02}{2} \right)
$$

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pm(0.005 + 0.010) = \pm 0.015 = \pm 1.5\%
$$

**Absolute error in $R$:** $\Delta R = 0.015 \times 25 = 0.375 \;\Omega$.

**Reported as:** $R = 25 \pm 0.375 \;\Omega$.

**Step 4 — Percentage Error in Power (Product Rule):**

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{P} = \pm\left( \frac{\Delta V}{V} + \frac{\Delta I}{I} \right) = \pm(0.005 + 0.010) = \pm 1.5\%
$$

**Absolute error in $P$:** $\Delta P = 0.015 \times 100 = 1.5 \;\text{W}$.

**Reported as:** $P = 100 \pm 1.5 \;\text{W}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often confuse **relative error** and **absolute error** while reporting.
> 2. The voltmeter and ammeter errors contribute *equally* in relative terms to both $R$ and $P$ since both are products/quotients of $V$ and $I$.
> 3. Always state the **final result with explicit units** (e.g., $\Omega$, W) — failure to do so costs 0.5 marks.
> 4. Show *all* intermediate steps; the formula alone is not sufficient for full marks.

---

### **Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are the different types of random errors? Explain the statistical analysis of data with $n$ repeated measurements. Define **(i)** arithmetic mean, **(ii)** standard deviation, and **(iii)** probable error.

#### Model Solution

**Types of Random Errors:**

1. **Environmental Fluctuations** — Unpredictable variation in temperature, pressure, or humidity.
2. **Transducer Noise** — Johnson-Nyquist thermal noise, shot noise, flicker noise (1/f).
3. **Quantization Error** — Inherent in ADC conversion.
4. **Mechanical Vibrations** — Random vibrations in the test setup.
5. **Statistical Variation** — Quantum mechanical uncertainty at microscopic levels.

**Statistical Analysis of $n$ Repeated Measurements:**

**Step 1 — Arithmetic Mean:**

$$
\bar{A} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i
$$

This is the **best estimate of the true value** because it minimizes the sum of squared deviations $\sum d_i^2$.

**Step 2 — Deviation:**

$$
d_i = A_i - \bar{A}
$$

A fundamental property: $\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i = 0$.

**Step 3 — Standard Deviation:**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i^2}{n-1}}
$$

- Measures the **dispersion** of measurements.
- A small $\sigma$ indicates **high precision**.
- The denominator $n-1$ is the Bessel's correction for finite samples.

**Step 4 — Probable Error:**

$$
PE = 0.6745 \cdot \sigma
$$

There is a **50% probability** that any single reading deviates from the mean by less than $PE$.

**Step 5 — Probable Error of the Mean:**

$$
PE_m = \frac{0.6745 \cdot \sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

As $n \to \infty$, $PE_m \to 0$, meaning the arithmetic mean converges to the true value.

**Gaussian Distribution (Normal Distribution):**

$$
f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

Where $x$ is the random error. The probability that a reading lies in $[-\sigma, +\sigma]$ is **68.27%**, in $[-2\sigma, +2\sigma]$ is **95.45%**, and in $[-3\sigma, +3\sigma]$ is **99.73%**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A set of 8 repeated measurements of a voltage source gives the following readings (in V): 10.12, 10.15, 10.09, 10.13, 10.11, 10.14, 10.10, 10.16. Calculate the **(i)** arithmetic mean, **(ii)** standard deviation, **(iii)** probable error, and **(iv)** probable error of the mean.

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Arithmetic Mean:**

$$
\bar{V} = \frac{10.12 + 10.15 + 10.09 + 10.13 + 10.11 + 10.14 + 10.10 + 10.16}{8}
$$

$$
\bar{V} = \frac{81.00}{8} = 10.125 \;\text{V}
$$

**Step 2 — Deviations and Their Squares:**

| Reading $V_i$ (V) | $d_i = V_i - 10.125$ | $d_i^2$ |
|-------------------|----------------------|---------|
| 10.12 | $-0.005$ | $0.000025$ |
| 10.15 | $+0.025$ | $0.000625$ |
| 10.09 | $-0.035$ | $0.001225$ |
| 10.13 | $+0.005$ | $0.000025$ |
| 10.11 | $-0.015$ | $0.000225$ |
| 10.14 | $+0.015$ | $0.000225$ |
| 10.10 | $-0.025$ | $0.000625$ |
| 10.16 | $+0.035$ | $0.001225$ |
| **Sum** | $\approx 0$ ✓ | $0.004200$ |

**Step 3 — Standard Deviation:**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{0.004200}{8-1}} = \sqrt{0.000600} = 0.0245 \;\text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Probable Error:**

$$
PE = 0.6745 \times 0.0245 = 0.0165 \;\text{V}
$$

**Step 5 — Probable Error of the Mean:**

$$
PE_m = \frac{0.6745 \times 0.0245}{\sqrt{8}} = \frac{0.0165}{2.828} = 0.00584 \;\text{V}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{V = 10.125 \pm 0.0245 \;\text{V (1}\sigma\text{)} \;\;\text{or} \;\; V = 10.125 \pm 0.00584 \;\text{V (mean)} }
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Forgetting to use $n-1$ instead of $n$ in the denominator of $\sigma$ is the **single most common mistake** — it costs up to **2 marks**.
> 2. Students often forget to verify that $\sum d_i = 0$ — this is a free correctness check worth showing.
> 3. Do not confuse **probable error of a single reading** with **probable error of the mean** — the latter is divided by $\sqrt{n}$.
> 4. Express $\sigma$ to *at least 3 significant figures*; rounding too early leads to cumulative error in $PE$ and $PE_m$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Errors in Measurements**

### **A. Concept Definitions (Part A Ready)**

- **Error**: $E = A_m - A_t$ (Deviation of measured from true value)
- **Accuracy**: Closeness to true value (affected by systematic errors)
- **Precision**: Closeness among repeated readings (affected by random errors)
- **Sensitivity**: $\Delta q_o / \Delta q_i$
- **Resolution**: Smallest measurable increment
- **Calibration**: Comparison against a higher-grade standard
- **Zero Error**: Reading offset when no input is applied
- **Hysteresis Error**: Different readings for ascending vs. descending input
- **Loading Error**: Disturbance caused by the act of measurement
- **Parallax Error**: Pointer apparent shift due to oblique viewing
- **Limiting/Guaranteed Error**: Maximum error specified by manufacturer

### **B. Error Classification (Syllabus Mandate)**

- **Gross Errors**: Human mistakes → eliminated by careful reading
- **Systematic Errors**: Deterministic, reproducible → eliminated by calibration
- **Random Errors**: Statistical, unpredictable → analyzed by probability theory

### **C. Critical Formula Set (Memorize)**

- **Sum/Difference**: $\Delta R = \pm(\Delta A + \Delta B + \Delta C)$  [Absolute errors add]
- **Product/Quotient**: $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pm(\frac{\Delta A}{A} + \frac{\Delta B}{B})$  [Relative errors add]
- **Power**: $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pm n\frac{\Delta A}{A}$
- **General**: $\Delta R = \pm\left( \left\vert\frac{\partial R}{\partial A}\right\vert \Delta A + \left\vert\frac{\partial R}{\partial B}\right\vert \Delta B + ... \right)$
- **Mean**: $\bar{A} = \frac{1}{n}\sum A_i$
- **Standard Deviation**: $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum d_i^2}{n-1}}$
- **Probable Error**: $PE = 0.6745\,\sigma$
- **Probable Error of Mean**: $PE_m = \frac{0.6745\,\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

### **D. Gaussian Distribution Probabilities (Memorize)**

- $P(\vert x \vert \le \sigma) = 68.27\%$
- $P(\vert x \vert \le 2\sigma) = 95.45\%$
- $P(\vert x \vert \le 3\sigma) = 99.73\%$

### **E. Common Pitfalls to Avoid**

- ❌ Adding **relative** errors in sums (you must add **absolute** errors).
- ❌ Using $n$ instead of $n-1$ in standard deviation.
- ❌ Confusing probable error of a single reading vs. of the mean.
- ❌ Forgetting to convert percentage error to absolute when required.
- ❌ Missing units in the final answer.
- ❌ Treating systematic and random errors as identical (they are NOT — different mitigation strategies).

### **F. Frequently Asked Derivations (Must Be Ready)**

- ✅ General error propagation: $R = f(A, B, C)$
- ✅ Sum, difference, product, quotient, power rule derivations
- ✅ Limiting error in a multi-variable function
- ✅ Statistical analysis of random errors (mean, std. dev., probable error)
- ✅ Standard deviation formula derivation and significance of $n-1$

### **G. High-Yield Numerical Problem Types**

- Voltage, current, resistance, and power error analysis
- Voltmeter-Ammeter method for resistance
- Power in AC circuits: $P = V I \cos\phi$ (three-variable error)
- Power in DC circuits: $P = V^2 / R$ (power of voltage)
- Specific resistance, strain, stress, and flow-rate problems

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: True Value, Static Error, Static Correction, Scale Range and Scale Span, Error Calibration Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT205 - TRANSDUCERS & MEASUREMENTS (MECHATRONICS ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** True Value, Static Error, Static Correction, Scale Range and Scale Span, Error Calibration Curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 | Topic: Static Performance Characteristics of Instruments

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 True Value (Actual Value)

**Formal Definition:**
The *true value* of a measurand is the value that would be obtained by a *perfect* measurement — i.e., a measurement completely free from all systematic and random errors. Since no measurement can be absolutely perfect, the true value remains an *idealized* concept that is operationally approximated using primary standards, national standards (e.g., NPL — National Physical Laboratory, India), or internationally accepted reference instruments.

In KTU parlance, the true value is also called the **actual value (Aₜ)** or **reference value**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> In all numerical problems, the "true value" given in the question must be treated as *absolute* and *error-free*. All instrument readings are compared against this reference.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Bull's-Eye" Picture:**
Imagine you are throwing darts at a dartboard.
