- The **center of the bull's-eye** = the *true value* (the exact quantity you want to measure).
- **Each dart that lands on the board** = a *measured value* you obtain from your instrument.
- The **distance from each dart to the bull's-eye** = the *error* in that measurement.
- A good instrument makes the darts cluster tightly near the bull's-eye (low error); a poor instrument scatters them widely.

---

### 1.2 Static Error (Absolute Error under Static Conditions)

**Formal Definition:**
The *static error* of an instrument is the numerical difference between the **measured value (Aₘ)** and the **true value (Aₜ)** when the measurand is held constant (i.e., in *steady state* / *static* condition — no time variation).

$$e_s = A_m - A_t$$

It can be positive (over-reading) or negative (under-reading).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
If a weighing scale shows **52 kg** when your *true* weight is **50 kg**, the static error is +2 kg (the scale *over-reads*). If it shows 48 kg, the error is −2 kg (under-reads).

---

### 1.3 Static Correction

**Formal Definition:**
The *static correction* is the **negative of the static error** — the quantity that must be *added* algebraically to the measured value to obtain the true value.

$$C = -e_s = A_t - A_m$$

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Going back to the weighing scale that showed 52 kg (true = 50 kg):
- Error = +2 kg → we must **subtract 2** to get the true value.
- Correction = **−2 kg** (a negative correction here).
- Adding the correction: 52 + (−2) = 50 kg ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember the Sign Convention (KTU Frequently Tested):**
> - *Correction* is always the **opposite sign** of *Error*.
> - A positive error demands a **negative correction**, and vice-versa.

---

### 1.4 Scale Range vs. Scale Span

| Term | Definition | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **Scale Range** | The overall spread of *input values* the instrument is designed to measure, from minimum to maximum. | $R = A_{max,input} - A_{min,input}$ |
| **Scale Span** | The numerical extent of the *graduated scale* itself — the highest minus the lowest scale marking. | $S = X_{max,scale} - X_{min,scale}$ |

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a car's speedometer that:
- Is built (designed) to read from **0 to 200 km/h** → this is its **Scale Range**.
- Its dial has markings from **0 to 200 km/h** → this is its **Scale Span**.

In many instruments, scale range and scale span have the same *numerical magnitude* but refer to *different physical quantities* (input measurand vs. dial graduation).

---

### 1.5 Error Calibration Curve

**Formal Definition:**
A *calibration curve* is a graph plotted between:
- **True value (Aₜ)** on the x-axis, and
- Either **measured value (Aₘ)**, **static error (eₛ)**, or **correction (C)** on the y-axis.

It is obtained by applying a series of **known input standards** (calibrated by NPL or equivalent) to the instrument, recording its output at each input, and computing the corresponding error. The resulting curve is then used to determine the correction at any intermediate reading.

> [!TIP]
> **Best-Fit Calibration Curve:**
> In KTU problems, when multiple calibration points are given, a *least-squares straight line* is fitted through them. This minimizes the sum of squared deviations and provides a continuous correction function.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Errors (Static Context)

Under static (steady-state) conditions, measurement errors are broadly grouped into three families:

1. **Gross Errors** — human mistakes (parallax, misreading, transcription).
2. **Systematic Errors** — repeatable, deterministic (instrument offset, zero drift, scale nonlinearity, loading effect).
3. **Random Errors** — unpredictable statistical fluctuations (treated via probability theory).

The **static error** addressed in this topic primarily deals with **systematic (bias) errors** that manifest under static inputs.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Framework

### Absolute Form
The simplest form is the *absolute static error* expressed in the same engineering units as the measurand.

$$e_s = A_m - A_t$$

### Relative Form (Normalized)
A dimensionless form obtained by dividing by the true value.

$$\epsilon_r = \frac{e_s}{A_t} = \frac{A_m - A_t}{A_t}$$

### Percentage Form
Relative error scaled to 100 — convenient for comparing accuracy across different instruments.

$$\epsilon_{\%} = \epsilon_r \times 100\% = \frac{A_m - A_t}{A_t} \times 100\%$$

### Full-Scale (FS) or Deflection Form
Often used in instrument specifications, where error is normalized with respect to the **full-scale deflection (FSD)** rather than the local true value.

$$\epsilon_{FS} = \frac{e_s}{A_{FS}} \times 100\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use Full-Scale Form?**
> A single percentage reading does not communicate the true *worst-case* error. For example, an instrument with ±1% reading error at 10% of full scale has ±10% *true* error at that low reading. Hence datasheets prefer **% of FS** or specify both.

---

## 2.3 Static Correction & Recovery of True Value

The relationship between measured value, error, correction, and true value forms a *closed algebraic loop*:

$$A_t = A_m - e_s = A_m + C$$

**Why this matters in engineering:**
- A pressure transducer might consistently over-read due to mechanical offset. The static correction can be stored in software (or trim potentiometer) to *auto-correct* every reading.
- In a clinical weighing scale, calibration factors are applied in firmware so that the displayed mass equals the true mass.

---

## 2.4 Scale Range and Scale Span — Operational Distinction

| Quantity | Refers to | Units | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scale Range | Measurand input range | Same as measurand (Pa, V, °C) | Defining instrument applicability |
| Scale Span | Dial graduation range | Same as scale units | Defining resolution & readability |

**Resolution** of an instrument = (Scale Span) / (Number of divisions on the dial).

Example: A voltmeter with scale span = 0–100 V and 200 divisions has resolution = **0.5 V/division**.

---

## 2.5 The Error Calibration Curve — Engineering Utility

The calibration curve serves four engineering purposes:

1. **Determination of Corrections** at any arbitrary input (interpolation).
2. **Linearity Assessment** — deviation of the curve from a straight line indicates nonlinearity.
3. **Hysteresis Detection** — comparing up-scale and down-scale curves.
4. **Compliance Documentation** — ISO/IEC 17025 requires traceable calibration certificates.

### Two Common Plotting Conventions

| Convention | x-axis | y-axis | What slope tells us |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type 1** (Measured vs True) | True Value Aₜ | Measured Value Aₘ | A 45° line = ideal instrument; deviation = bias |
| **Type 2** (Error vs True) | True Value Aₜ | Static Error eₛ | Horizontal line at 0 = no bias; slope = scale-factor error |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Absolute Static Error | $e_s = A_m - A_t$ | Same as measurand | Signed quantity |
| 2 | Static Correction | $C = -e_s = A_t - A_m$ | Same as measurand | Opposite sign to error |
| 3 | True Value from Measurement | $A_t = A_m + C$ | Same as measurand | Used after applying correction |
| 4 | Relative Static Error | $\epsilon_r = \dfrac{e_s}{A_t}$ | Dimensionless | Ratio form |
| 5 | Percentage Static Error | $\epsilon_{\%} = \dfrac{e_s}{A_t} \times 100$ | % | Most common in KTU numericals |
| 6 | Full-Scale Percentage Error | $\epsilon_{FS} = \dfrac{e_s}{A_{FS}} \times 100$ | % | Datasheet standard |
| 7 | Scale Range | $R = A_{max} - A_{min}$ | Same as measurand | Input measurand span |
| 8 | Scale Span | $S = X_{max} - X_{min}$ | Scale units | Dial graduation span |
| 9 | Resolution | $r = S / N$ | Scale units/div | N = number of divisions |
| 10 | Least-Squares Slope | $m = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | — | For best-fit calibration line |
| 11 | Least-Squares Intercept | $b = \bar{y} - m\bar{x}$ | — | Used with slope m |
| 12 | Calibration Line (Error Form) | $e_s(x) = mx + b$ | Same as measurand | Linear calibration model |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Strategy Tip:**
> When asked "find the correction at a particular reading", always: **(1)** fit a calibration line using *all given data points*, **(2)** substitute the target reading into the line equation, **(3)** apply $C = -e_s$. Students often forget to negate the error → sign error → **full mark loss**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Calibration Line via Least Squares

We are given *n* calibration points $(x_i, y_i)$, where:
- $x_i$ = true value (input standard),
- $y_i$ = corresponding measured value (or error).

We seek the best-fit straight line $y = mx + c$ (using $c$ for intercept to avoid conflict with correction notation) that minimizes the sum of squared residuals.

### Step 1 — Define the Residual
At each calibration point, the residual (vertical deviation from the fitted line) is:

$$r_i = y_i - (mx_i + c)$$

### Step 2 — Define the Sum of Squared Residuals

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i^{\,2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \bigl[y_i - (mx_i + c)\bigr]^2$$

### Step 3 — Minimize S with Respect to m and c

Setting $\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial m} = 0$ and $\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial c} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial S}{\partial c} &= -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \bigl[y_i - mx_i - c\bigr] = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \sum y_i &= m \sum x_i + nc
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial S}{\partial m} &= -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \bigl[y_i - mx_i - c\bigr] = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \sum x_i y_i &= m \sum x_i^2 + c \sum x_i
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve the Normal Equations

From the first equation:

$$c = \bar{y} - m\bar{x} \quad \text{where} \quad \bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i,\;\; \bar{y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum y_i$$

Substituting into the second and simplifying yields the textbook slope formula:

$$
m = \frac{n\sum x_i y_i - \bigl(\sum x_i\bigr)\bigl(\sum y_i\bigr)}{n\sum x_i^2 - \bigl(\sum x_i\bigr)^2}
$$

Equivalently, in deviation form (more intuitive):

$$m = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

This derivation shows *why* the best-fit line is the unique line that *minimizes* the total squared vertical distance from data points.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Typical KTU 14-Mark Style Numerical

### Problem Statement
A pressure gauge was calibrated against a dead-weight tester (taken as the standard / true value source). The readings obtained are:

| Trial | True Pressure $A_t$ (kPa) | Gauge Reading $A_m$ (kPa) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 100.5 |
| 2 | 200 | 200.8 |
| 3 | 300 | 301.2 |
| 4 | 400 | 401.0 |
| 5 | 500 | 500.6 |

**(a)** Compute the static error at each calibration point.
**(b)** Determine the corresponding static correction at each point.
**(c)** Fit a straight-line calibration curve (error vs. true value) using least squares.
**(d)** Find the correction at a measured reading of **250 kPa**, and hence compute the true pressure.

### Solution

#### (a) Static Error at Each Point

Using $e_s = A_m - A_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{s1} &= 100.5 - 100 = +0.5 \text{ kPa} \\
e_{s2} &= 200.8 - 200 = +0.8 \text{ kPa} \\
e_{s3} &= 301.2 - 300 = +1.2 \text{ kPa} \\
e_{s4} &= 401.0 - 400 = +1.0 \text{ kPa} \\
e_{s5} &= 500.6 - 500 = +0.6 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (b) Static Correction at Each Point

Using $C = -e_s$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= -0.5 \text{ kPa}, \quad C_2 = -0.8 \text{ kPa}, \quad C_3 = -1.2 \text{ kPa}, \\
C_4 &= -1.0 \text{ kPa}, \quad C_5 = -0.6 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (c) Best-Fit Calibration Line

Let $x = A_t$ and $y = e_s$. Tabulate the required sums:

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 0.5 | −200 | −0.32 | +64 | 40000 |
| 2 | 200 | 0.8 | −100 | −0.02 | +2 | 10000 |
| 3 | 300 | 1.2 | 0 | +0.38 | 0 | 0 |
| 4 | 400 | 1.0 | +100 | +0.18 | +18 | 10000 |
| 5 | 500 | 0.6 | +200 | −0.22 | −44 | 40000 |
| Σ | 1500 | 4.1 | — | — | **+40** | **100000** |

Compute means:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1500}{5} = 300 \text{ kPa}, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{4.1}{5} = 0.82 \text{ kPa}$$

Compute slope:

$$m = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2} = \frac{40}{100000} = 0.0004$$

Compute intercept:

$$c = \bar{y} - m\bar{x} = 0.82 - (0.0004)(300) = 0.82 - 0.12 = 0.70 \text{ kPa}$$

Therefore the **calibration line** is:

$$\boxed{e_s(x) = 0.0004 \, x + 0.70 \;\; \text{kPa}}$$

#### (d) Correction at 250 kPa

The question gives a **measured reading** of 250 kPa. We use the calibration curve (which is in terms of true value) iteratively. Since the error is small relative to the reading, we can approximate by treating $A_m \approx A_t$ as the abscissa. A more rigorous iteration:

**First Approximation:**
Use $A_m = 250$ kPa directly:

$$e_s(250) = 0.0004(250) + 0.70 = 0.10 + 0.70 = 0.80 \text{ kPa}$$

The correction at this reading:

$$C = -e_s = -0.80 \text{ kPa}$$

True pressure:

$$A_t = A_m + C = 250 + (-0.80) = 249.20 \text{ kPa}$$

**Refined Iteration:**
Plug $A_t = 249.20$ back into the calibration equation:

$$e_s(249.20) = 0.0004(249.20) + 0.70 = 0.0997 + 0.70 = 0.7997 \text{ kPa}$$

The change is negligible ($\Delta e_s \approx 0.0001$ kPa). Hence:

$$\boxed{A_t \approx 249.20 \text{ kPa}, \quad C \approx -0.80 \text{ kPa}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Single-pass approximation is sufficient** for KTU unless the question explicitly demands iteration. Document this assumption in your answer.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Each Quantity Derives from the Others

| **Given** | **Find** | **Apply** |
|---|---|---|
| $A_m, A_t$ | $e_s$ | $e_s = A_m - A_t$ |
| $A_m, A_t$ | $C$ | $C = A_t - A_m$ |
| $A_m, e_s$ | $A_t$ | $A_t = A_m - e_s$ |
| $A_m, C$ | $A_t$ | $A_t = A_m + C$ |
| $e_s, A_t$ | $e_{\%}$ | $e_{\%} = (e_s / A_t) \times 100$ |
| $A_{max}, A_{min}$ | Range $R$ | $R = A_{max} - A_{min}$ |
| $N$ divisions on dial, span $S$ | Resolution | $r = S / N$ |
| Multiple $(A_t, A_m)$ pairs | Calibration line | Least-squares fit (slope, intercept) |
| $A_m$, calibration line $e_s(x)$ | $A_t$ | Iterate: $A_t^{(k+1)} = A_m - e_s(A_t^{(k)})$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — The Calibration Loop

The following block-flow diagram depicts how a static calibration is performed in an instrumentation laboratory and how it feeds back into operational use:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["STANDARD INPUT SOURCE\nNPL Traceable Dead Weight Tester or Primary Standard"]
    nodeB["APPLY KNOWN INPUT\nIncrement True Value Step by Step"]
    nodeC["RECORD INSTRUMENT OUTPUT\nNote Measured Value at Each Input"]
    nodeD["COMPUTE STATIC ERROR\ne_s = A_m minus A_t"]
    nodeE["PLOT CALIBRATION CURVE\nError vs True Value"]
    nodeF["FIT BEST FIT LINE\nLeast Squares Regression"]
    nodeG["STORE CORRECTION TABLE\nor Equation in Memory"]
    nodeH["FIELD USE OF INSTRUMENT\nReal Time Measurement"]
    nodeI["APPLY CORRECTION\nA_t = A_m plus C"]
    nodeJ["DISPLAY TRUE VALUE\nCorrected Reading"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left branch** (nodes A → G) is the *offline* calibration process done in the lab.
- The **right branch** (nodes H → J) is *online* field operation, where the correction table/equation derived earlier is applied automatically.
- The two branches are linked via the stored correction model — this embodies the *closed-loop* philosophy of measurement science.

---

## 4.2 Schematic — Error / Correction Sign Convention

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["MEASURED VALUE\nA_m = 250 kPa"]
    nodeQ["TRUE VALUE\nA_t = 249.20 kPa"]
    nodeR["STATIC ERROR\ne_s = plus 0.80 kPa"]
    nodeS["CORRECTION\nC = minus 0.80 kPa"]

    nodeP -->|subtract e_s| nodeQ
    nodeP -->|compute e_s equals A_m minus A_t| nodeR
    nodeR -->|negate to get correction| nodeS
    nodeS -->|add C to A_m| nodeQ
```

**Interpretation:**
- A measured value *higher* than the true value (over-reading) yields a **positive error** and a **negative correction**.
- The correction is the *algebraic* quantity that, when added to $A_m$, recovers $A_t$.

---

## 4.3 Scale Range vs. Scale Span — Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeZ["INSTRUMENT DESIGN"]
    nodeR1["SCALE RANGE\nDesigned Input Span\nExample 0 to 200 kPa"]
    nodeS1["SCALE SPAN\nDial Graduation Span\nExample 0 to 200 kPa marked"]
    nodeRes["RESOLUTION\nScale Span divided by Number of Divisions\nExample 200 divided by 100 equals 2 kPa per div"]
    nodeAcc["ACCURACY CLASS\nSpecification in percent of FS\nDefines Maximum Static Error"]

    nodeZ --> nodeR1
    nodeZ --> nodeS1
    nodeS1 --> nodeRes
    nodeR1 --> nodeAcc
```

---

## 4.4 Block Architecture — Instrument with Built-In Calibration Memory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["SENSOR\nPhysical to Electrical Conversion"]
    node2["SIGNAL CONDITIONER\nAmplification and Filtering"]
    node3["ADC\nAnalog to Digital Conversion"]
    node4["MICROCONTROLLER\nApply Correction Equation A_t equals A_m plus C"]
    node5["CALIBRATION MEMORY\nStores Slope m and Intercept c"]
    node6["DISPLAY UNIT\nShows True Value"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node5 --> node4
    node4 --> node6
```

**Engineering Insight:**
Modern digital instruments (e.g., smart pressure transmitters, clinical weighing scales) embed the calibration polynomial *inside firmware*. Each time the instrument is recalibrated, the new (m, c) coefficients are written into EEPROM — a real-world embodiment of static correction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the following terms with one suitable example each:**
**(i)** True value
**(ii)** Static error
**(iii)** Static correction

**Model Answer:**

**(i) True Value:**
The true value of a measurand is the value that would be obtained by a perfect, error-free measurement. Since no measurement is perfectly free from error, the true value is approximated by reference standards (e.g., a dead-weight tester for pressure). *Example:* The true value of a 1 kg standard mass kept at NPL.

**(ii) Static Error:**
Static error is the algebraic difference between the measured value ($A_m$) and the true value ($A_t$) under steady-state (static) conditions, $e_s = A_m - A_t$. *Example:* If an ammeter reads 5.2 A while the true current is 5.0 A, the static error is +0.2 A.

**(iii) Static Correction:**
Static correction is the negative of the static error, i.e., $C = A_t - A_m$. It is the quantity added algebraically to the measured value to recover the true value. *Example:* For the ammeter above, correction = −0.2 A, so true current = 5.2 + (−0.2) = 5.0 A.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between *scale range* and *scale span* of an instrument. Why are both quantities important from an engineering design perspective?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Scale Range | Scale Span |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | The difference between maximum and minimum values of the *input measurand* the instrument is designed to handle. | The difference between the *highest* and *lowest* graduations marked on the dial. |
| Formula | $R = A_{max} - A_{min}$ | $S = X_{max} - X_{min}$ |
| Units | Same as measurand (Pa, V, °C). | Same as the scale units (often identical numerically). |
| Refers to | The physical quantity being sensed. | The visual numerical scale. |

**Engineering importance:**
- *Scale range* determines the **applicability** of the instrument — whether it can handle the expected operating range of the process.
- *Scale span* determines the **resolution and readability** — finer divisions yield better readability and lower measurement uncertainty.
- An instrument may have a small scale range but a large scale span (e.g., a narrow-range voltmeter with extended suppression).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** A temperature transducer was tested against a calibrated reference thermometer. The readings are tabulated below:

| Trial | Reference Temperature (°C) | Transducer Output (°C) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 50.4 |
| 2 | 100 | 100.7 |
| 3 | 150 | 151.5 |
| 4 | 200 | 201.8 |
| 5 | 250 | 251.0 |

Calculate the static error and correction at each calibration point. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the data above, determine the **least-squares straight-line calibration equation** relating transducer output to reference temperature. Using this equation, find the **true temperature** when the transducer reads **175 °C**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Errors and Corrections**

Using $e_s = A_m - A_t$ and $C = -e_s$:

| Trial | $A_t$ (°C) | $A_m$ (°C) | $e_s$ (°C) | $C$ (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 50.4 | +0.4 | −0.4 |
| 2 | 100 | 100.7 | +0.7 | −0.7 |
| 3 | 150 | 151.5 | +1.5 | −1.5 |
| 4 | 200 | 201.8 | +1.8 | −1.8 |
| 5 | 250 | 251.0 | +1.0 | −1.0 |

All errors are positive, indicating a consistent *over-reading* bias that grows with temperature (suggesting a **scale-factor error** in addition to a small zero offset).

**Part (b) — Least-Squares Fit and Correction at 175 °C**

Let $x = A_t$ (true temperature) and $y = A_m$ (measured temperature).

Means:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{50+100+150+200+250}{5} = 150, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{50.4+100.7+151.5+201.8+251.0}{5} = 151.08$$

Deviation table:

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i - 150$ | $y_i - 151.08$ | Product | $(x_i-150)^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 50 | 50.4 | −100 | −100.68 | 10068 | 10000 |
| 100 | 100.7 | −50 | −50.38 | 2519 | 2500 |
| 150 | 151.5 | 0 | +0.42 | 0 | 0 |
| 200 | 201.8 | +50 | +50.72 | 2536 | 2500 |
| 250 | 251.0 | +100 | +99.92 | 9992 | 10000 |
| Σ | — | — | — | **25115** | **25000** |

Slope:

$$m = \frac{25115}{25000} = 1.0046$$

Intercept:

$$c = \bar{y} - m\bar{x} = 151.08 - (1.0046)(150) = 151.08 - 150.69 = 0.39$$

Calibration equation (measured vs. true):

$$A_m = 1.0046 \, A_t + 0.39$$

To find the **true temperature** when $A_m = 175$ °C, invert:

$$A_t = \frac{A_m - c}{m} = \frac{175 - 0.39}{1.0046} = \frac{174.61}{1.0046} \approx 173.81 \text{ °C}$$

$$\boxed{A_t \approx 173.81 \text{ °C}}$$

Equivalently, the **error** at $A_t \approx 173.81$ is $e_s = 1.0046(173.81) + 0.39 - 173.81 = 1.19$ °C, giving correction $C = -1.19$ °C and true value $175 - 1.19 = 173.81$ °C ✓.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of an **error calibration curve** with a neatly labelled sketch. Discuss how it is used to determine the *correction* at any arbitrary reading of the instrument. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A voltmeter has a scale range of **0–300 V** with **150 divisions** on its scale. When calibrated against a standard, the following readings were noted:

| Standard Voltage $A_t$ (V) | Voltmeter Reading $A_m$ (V) |
|---|---|
| 60 | 61 |
| 120 | 122 |
| 180 | 183 |
| 240 | 244 |
| 300 | 305 |

**(i)** Compute the percentage static error at each reading.
**(ii)** Find the **scale range, scale span, and resolution** of the voltmeter.
**(iii)** What is the **correction** that must be applied to a voltmeter reading of **150 V**? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Error Calibration Curve**

A *calibration curve* is the graphical relationship between the *true value* (input standard) and the corresponding *measured value* (output) of an instrument, plotted from data obtained during calibration.

Two principal forms exist:

1. **Measured vs True Value** — a 45° straight line indicates an ideal instrument.
2. **Error vs True Value** — a horizontal line at zero indicates absence of bias.

**Construction:**
- Step 1: Apply a series of accurately known input standards (traceable to NPL or equivalent) covering the full range.
- Step 2: Record the instrument output at each input.
- Step 3: Compute the static error at each point: $e_s = A_m - A_t$.
- Step 4: Plot the curve.
- Step 5: Fit a least-squares straight line.

**Use for correction determination:**
- At any arbitrary reading, drop a vertical to the curve and read off the error.
- Apply $C = -e_s$ to obtain the correction.
- Add the correction to the measured value to recover the true value.

**Sketch description:**
```
   A_m │
       │              ● A_m vs A_t (slightly above 45°)
   305 ─┤            ●
   244 ─┤      ●
   183 ─┤  ●
   122 ─┤●
    61 ─●
       │
       └─┬────┬────┬────┬────┬──── A_t
         60  120  180  240  300
```
- Dashed 45° reference line (ideal behavior).
- Solid line through data = actual calibration curve.

**Part (b) — Voltmeter Calibration**

**(i) Percentage Static Error**

Using $\epsilon_{\%} = \dfrac{A_m - A_t}{A_t} \times 100$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_{\%,1} &= \frac{61 - 60}{60} \times 100 = +1.667\% \\
\epsilon_{\%,2} &= \frac{122 - 120}{120} \times 100 = +1.667\% \\
\epsilon_{\%,3} &= \frac{183 - 180}{180} \times 100 = +1.667\% \\
\epsilon_{\%,4} &= \frac{244 - 240}{240} \times 100 = +1.667\% \\
\epsilon_{\%,5} &= \frac{305 - 300}{300} \times 100 = +1.667\%
\end{aligned}
$$

All five percentage errors are *identical* at **+1.667 %**. This indicates a *pure multiplicative (scale-factor) error* with negligible zero offset.

**(ii) Scale Range, Scale Span, Resolution**

- **Scale Range:** $R = 300 - 0 = \mathbf{300 \text{ V}}$
- **Scale Span:** $S = 300 - 0 = \mathbf{300 \text{ V}}$ (assuming dial starts at 0)
- **Resolution:** $r = S/N = 300/150 = \mathbf{2 \text{ V/division}}$

**(iii) Correction at 150 V Reading**

Since the percentage error is *constant* (≈ +1.667%), the calibration equation is:

$$A_m = 1.01667 \cdot A_t$$

Equivalently: $A_t = A_m / 1.01667 = A_m \times 0.98361$.

At $A_m = 150$ V:

$$A_t = \frac{150}{1.01667} = 147.54 \text{ V}$$

Static error at this reading:

$$e_s = A_m - A_t = 150 - 147.54 = +2.46 \text{ V}$$

Static correction:

$$\boxed{C = -e_s = -2.46 \text{ V}}$$

True voltage corresponding to a voltmeter reading of 150 V is approximately **147.54 V**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error on Correction:** Students compute $C = +e_s$ instead of $C = -e_s$. This single error flips the sign of the final true value and costs **3–5 marks** immediately. Memorize: *Correction = True − Measured = −Error*.
>
> 2. **Confusing Scale Range with Scale Span:** In instruments with *suppressed zero* (e.g., a gauge that reads 50–150 kPa where the dial still reads 0–100), the scale range = 100 kPa but the scale span = 100 divisions. Always state *what* the difference refers to.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Units in Slope and Intercept:** The slope of an *error vs true value* plot is **dimensionless** if you plot $e_s$ and $A_t$ in identical units — but if the y-axis is in different units (e.g., mV transducer output vs. pressure in kPa), the slope **must carry units**. KTU examiners deduct for missing units.
>
> 4. **Applying the Calibration Equation at the Wrong Argument:** When asked "true value at measured reading = X", students plug X into the $A_t$ slot of the equation. The correct procedure is to **invert** the calibration equation (or iterate). The pressure-gauge worked example in Section 3.2 demonstrates this carefully.
>
> 5. **Using Percentage Error = Error / Full Scale Instead of Error / True Value:** Read the question word-for-word. "% of reading" and "% of full scale" give *very different* answers, especially near the bottom of the scale.
>
> 6. **Omitting the Best-Fit Line Calculation:** When 4 or more data points are given, examiners expect a *least-squares fit*, not picking two arbitrary points and drawing a line through them. Always show the table of deviations, slope formula, and intercept computation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Static Characteristics:**

- **True Value (Aₜ):** Idealized error-free reference value; approximated by NPL-traceable standards.
- **Measured Value (Aₘ):** The actual reading shown by the instrument.
- **Static Error (eₛ):** $e_s = A_m - A_t$. *Signed quantity.* Positive ⇒ over-reading; negative ⇒ under-reading.
- **Static Correction (C):** $C = -e_s = A_t - A_m$. *Always opposite sign to error.*
- **True Value Recovery:** $A_t = A_m + C = A_m - e_s$.
- **Relative Error:** $\epsilon_r = e_s / A_t$ (dimensionless).
- **Percentage Error:** $\epsilon_{\%} = (e_s / A_t) \times 100$ (most commonly tested form).
- **Full-Scale Error:** $\epsilon_{FS} = (e_s / A_{FS}) \times 100$ — datasheet convention.
- **Scale Range:** Span of the *input measurand* the instrument can handle.
- **Scale Span:** Span of *dial graduations*; same units as scale.
- **Resolution:** Scale Span ÷ Number of Divisions.
- **Calibration Curve:** Plot of $A_m$ (or $e_s$) vs $A_t$ from known standards.
- **Best-Fit Line:** Slope $m = \dfrac{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})}{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2}$, intercept $c = \bar{y} - m\bar{x}$.
- **Engineering Use:** Smart sensors store (m, c) in firmware for *real-time auto-correction*.
- **KTU Frequent Pattern:** Compute errors → fit line → use line to find correction at arbitrary reading.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "T-M-E-C":**
> **T**rue value comes from **M**easured value plus **C**orrection; **E**rror = Measured − True; **C**orrection = True − Measured. Always remember the **circular relationship** among these four quantities.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reproducibility and Drift
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT205 - TRANSDUCERS & MEASUREMENTS (MECHATRONICS ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reproducibility and Drift

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Reproducibility — Formal Definition

In the context of measurement science and transducer characteristics, **Reproducibility** is defined as the ability of a measuring instrument or transducer to produce the *same* (or very nearly the same) output reading when the same measurand is applied repeatedly, but under *changed conditions* of measurement.

These "changed conditions" typically include:
- Different operators
- Different ambient environmental conditions (temperature, humidity, pressure)
- Different times of measurement (after recalibration, after prolonged use)
- Different locations
- Different measurement setups or auxiliary equipment

> [!NOTE]
> **Reproducibility (KTU Syllabus Definition):** *"The closeness of the agreement between the results of measurements of the same measurand carried out under changed conditions of measurement."* — As per ISO/IEC Guide 99 (VIM).

The numerical metric that quantifies this property is called the **Reproducibility Index (R)** or **Reproducibility Error (e_r)**, usually expressed as a percentage of the Full-Scale Output (FSO) or Full-Scale Reading (FSR).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often test the distinction between **Repeatability** and **Reproducibility**. Remember: Repeatability = *same* conditions; Reproducibility = *changed* conditions. This is a **favourite 3-mark question**.

### 1.2 Drift — Formal Definition

**Drift** is the slow, gradual, and undesired change in the output signal (or reading) of a measuring instrument over a period of time, while the input (measurand) remains constant. Drift represents a *temporal instability* in the static calibration characteristic of the transducer.

Mathematically, if the output at time $t_0$ is $Y(t_0)$ and the output at time $t$ is $Y(t)$, then the drift $D$ is:

$$D = Y(t) - Y(t_0) \quad \text{(for constant input } x(t) = \text{const)}$$

Drift is generally classified into:

1. **Zero Drift** — Change in output at *zero input* over time.
2. **Sensitivity (Span) Drift** — Change in the *slope* (sensitivity) of the calibration curve over time.
3. **Thermal Drift** — Drift caused specifically by ambient temperature variation.
4. **Time Drift** — Drift caused by aging, component wear, or self-heating (independent of temperature).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Two Coffee Mugs 🏷️**
> 
> Imagine you and your friend both own a *digital kitchen weighing scale*. You both decide to weigh a 1 kg sugar packet every day for a month.
> 
> 🔹 **Reproducibility:** Even if your friend uses *her* scale in *her* kitchen, in *cooler* weather, in the *morning* — both your scales should consistently report values that *closely agree* (say, both 1.00 kg ± 0.02 kg). The agreement across *different conditions/users* is reproducibility.
> 
> 🔹 **Drift:** Now suppose the scale sits on the same shelf, untouched, for six months. One day, you place the same 1 kg packet and it reads 1.05 kg. The reading has *silently shifted* over time, even though the load is identical. This is drift — a temporal *sneak attack* on accuracy.

### 1.4 Significance in Mechatronics

In a closed-loop mechatronic system (robotic arm, CNC, autonomous vehicle), the **sensor reading is the system's only window into the physical world**. If a position transducer *drifts* or shows *poor reproducibility*, the controller will make decisions based on *false feedback*, leading to:

- Steady-state positional error
- Loss of calibration in the feedback loop
- Cumulative errors during long-duration operations
- In safety-critical systems (e.g., surgical robots), potential catastrophic failure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bottom Line:** Reproducibility governs how *reliably* a sensor performs across varied conditions. Drift governs how *stably* a sensor performs across *time*. Both together define the **long-term trustworthiness** of a measurement system.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Reproducibility — Analytical Structure

Reproducibility is a *meta-characteristic* — it does not describe a single measurement but rather the *statistical agreement* among many measurement series taken under varying conditions. It is typically expressed in one of three equivalent forms:

**Form 1 — Absolute Reproducibility Error:**

$$e_R = \max \vert Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_i \vert \quad \text{(across all conditions } j \text{)}$$

**Form 2 — Percentage of Full Scale (%FS):**

$$\%R = \frac{e_R}{\text{Full-Scale Output}} \times 100$$

**Form 3 — Percentage of Reading (%Rdg):**

$$\%R = \frac{e_R}{Y_{\text{actual}}} \times 100$$

Where:
- $Y_{ij}$ = measured value in the $j^{th}$ set of conditions for the $i^{th}$ measurand
- $\bar{Y}_i$ = mean of all measured values for the $i^{th}$ measurand across conditions
- $e_R$ = maximum deviation from the mean

### 2.2 Reproducibility vs. Repeatability — The Subtle Distinction

| Feature | Repeatability | Reproducibility |
|---|---|---|
| **Conditions** | Same | Changed |
| **Operator** | Same | May differ |
| **Instrument** | Same | May differ (or same, re-calibrated) |
| **Time Gap** | Short (back-to-back) | Long (over days/weeks) |
| **Location** | Same laboratory | Different laboratories/sites |
| **KTU Keyword** | "Closeness of agreement … same conditions" | "Closeness of agreement … changed conditions" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic:** **R**epeatability = **R**epeat (same way). **R**eproducibility = **R**eplicate (different ways).

### 2.3 Drift — Mathematical Structure

For a linear transducer with calibration equation:

$$Y(t) = S(t) \cdot x + Z(t)$$

Where:
- $Y(t)$ = output at time $t$
- $S(t)$ = sensitivity at time $t$
- $Z(t)$ = zero offset at time $t$
- $x$ = measurand (input)

The **total drift** at any time $t$ (with input $x$) is the deviation from the calibration at reference time $t_0$:

$$D(t, x) = Y(t, x) - Y(t_0, x) = [S(t) - S(t_0)] \cdot x + [Z(t) - Z(t_0)]$$

Splitting into two canonical components:

**Zero Drift:**

$$D_Z(t) = Z(t) - Z(t_0)$$

**Sensitivity Drift (Span Drift):**

$$D_S(t, x) = [S(t) - S(t_0)] \cdot x$$

The **Total Drift** is therefore the sum:

$$D(t, x) = D_S(t, x) + D_Z(t)$$

### 2.4 Common Sources of Drift

| Source | Effect | Typical Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermal expansion** of sensing element | Zero & span shift | Temperature compensation, thermostatted housing |
| **Aging of components** (resistors, springs) | Slow monotonic drift | Periodic re-calibration |
| **Self-heating** of active electronics | Localized thermal drift | Duty cycling, low-power design |
| **Mechanical creep** (e.g., strain gauges on polymers) | Time-dependent deformation | Pre-loading, age-hardened alloys |
| **Hysteresis memory** (after large input excursions) | Output not returning to prior zero | Mechanical limit protection, de-perm routines |
| **Contamination / corrosion** of sensing surface | Surface property change | Hermetic sealing, inert coatings |

### 2.5 Reproducibility — Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Reproducibility Matters |
|---|---|
| **Multi-sensor industrial networks** (Industry 4.0) | Interchangeability of identical sensors on different machines |
| **Calibration laboratories** | Traceability across geographically separated setups |
| **Field-deployed IoT sensors** | Performance stability across varied micro-climates |
| **Redundant sensor arrays** (avionics, nuclear) | Voting algorithms require agreement across units |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reproducibility Error | $e_R = \max \vert Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_i \vert$ | Same units as output $Y$ |
| 2 | % Reproducibility (FS) | $\%R_{FS} = \dfrac{e_R}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$ | % of Full-Scale Output |
| 3 | % Reproducibility (Rdg) | $\%R_{rdg} = \dfrac{e_R}{Y_{\text{actual}}} \times 100$ | % of true reading |
| 4 | Drift (general) | $D = Y(t) - Y(t_0) \vert_{x = \text{const}}$ | Same units as output |
| 5 | Zero Drift | $D_Z = Z(t) - Z(t_0)$ | Evaluated at $x = 0$ |
| 6 | Sensitivity Drift | $D_S = [S(t) - S(t_0)] \cdot x$ | Proportional to $x$ |
| 7 | Total Drift | $D = D_Z + D_S$ | Additive decomposition |
| 8 | Calibration Eqn. (time-variant) | $Y(t) = S(t) \cdot x + Z(t)$ | Linear model |
| 9 | Combined Reproducibility | $R_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{R_1^2 + R_2^2 + \dots}$ | RSS form (independent sources) |
| 10 | Drift Rate | $\dot{D} = \dfrac{dD}{dt}$ | Units: output-unit / time |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to *define* reproducibility or drift, the **exact phrasing matters**. A 3-mark question requires (a) the *quantitative metric*, (b) the *conditions under which it is evaluated*, and (c) the *unit of expression*. Missing any one costs marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Decomposition of Total Drift into Zero and Sensitivity Components

**Given:** A linear transducer obeys the time-varying calibration equation:

$$Y(t, x) = S(t) \cdot x + Z(t)$$

**Step 1 — Define the reference calibration at time $t_0$:**

At the reference instant $t_0$, the calibration is:

$$Y(t_0, x) = S(t_0) \cdot x + Z(t_0)$$

**Step 2 — Express the instantaneous output at a later time $t$:**

For the *same* input $x$, the output at time $t$ is:

$$Y(t, x) = S(t) \cdot x + Z(t)$$

**Step 3 — Subtract to obtain the deviation (drift):**

$$D(t, x) = Y(t, x) - Y(t_0, x)$$

Substituting both expressions:

$$D(t, x) = \big[S(t) \cdot x + Z(t)\big] - \big[S(t_0) \cdot x + Z(t_0)\big]$$

**Step 4 — Group the sensitivity terms and zero terms separately:**

$$D(t, x) = \big[S(t) - S(t_0)\big] \cdot x + \big[Z(t) - Z(t_0)\big]$$

**Step 5 — Define the two component drifts:**

$$\boxed{D(t, x) = D_S(t, x) + D_Z(t)}$$

Where:
- $D_S(t, x) = \big[S(t) - S(t_0)\big] \cdot x$ → depends on input $x$ (multiplicative)
- $D_Z(t) = Z(t) - Z(t_0)$ → independent of input $x$ (additive)

**Interpretation:** The *additive* zero-drift shifts the entire calibration curve up or down (parallel shift). The *multiplicative* sensitivity drift rotates the line about the origin. Together, they produce the *general affine transformation* of the static calibration line.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Reproducibility Calculation

**Problem (Typical KTU style):**

A pressure transducer of full-scale 100 kPa is tested in three different laboratories (A, B, C) using different operators and ambient conditions. For an applied pressure of 50 kPa, the following readings are obtained:

| Lab | Reading (kPa) | Operator | Temp (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 49.8 | X | 22 |
| B | 50.4 | Y | 28 |
| C | 49.6 | Z | 25 |

Calculate the **reproducibility error** and express it as **% of Full-Scale Output**.

**Step 1 — Compute the mean reading:**

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{49.8 + 50.4 + 49.6}{3} = \frac{149.8}{3} = 49.9333 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the absolute deviation of each reading from the mean:**

$$\Delta_1 = \vert 49.8 - 49.9333 \vert = 0.1333 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\Delta_2 = \vert 50.4 - 49.9333 \vert = 0.4667 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\Delta_3 = \vert 49.6 - 49.9333 \vert = 0.3333 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the maximum deviation (this is the reproducibility error):**

$$e_R = \max(\Delta_1, \Delta_2, \Delta_3) = 0.4667 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4 — Express as % of Full-Scale Output:**

$$\%R_{FS} = \frac{0.4667}{100} \times 100 = 0.4667\,\%\,\text{FS}$$

**Step 5 — Express as % of Reading (alternative form):**

$$\%R_{rdg} = \frac{0.4667}{50} \times 100 = 0.9334\,\%\,\text{Rdg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** Reproducibility error = $\pm\,0.47$ kPa, i.e., $\pm\,0.47\%$ of Full Scale.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Drift Over Time

**Problem:**

A temperature sensor has a calibration equation at $t_0 = 0$ (factory calibration):

$$Y(t_0) = 0.100 \cdot T + 0.50 \quad [\text{output in V, input } T \text{ in } ^\circ\text{C}]$$

After 12 months of use, the zero offset has shifted to $Z(12) = 0.65$ V and the sensitivity has drifted to $S(12) = 0.0985$ V/°C. Find the **drift** when the input is $T = 80\,^\circ$C.

**Step 1 — Compute output at reference time $t_0$:**

$$Y(t_0, 80) = 0.100 \times 80 + 0.50 = 8.00 + 0.50 = 8.50 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute output at current time $t = 12$ months:**

$$Y(12, 80) = 0.0985 \times 80 + 0.65 = 7.88 + 0.65 = 8.53 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total drift:**

$$D(12, 80) = 8.53 - 8.50 = 0.03 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Decompose into the two components:**

$$D_Z = Z(12) - Z(0) = 0.65 - 0.50 = 0.15 \text{ V}$$

$$D_S = [S(12) - S(0)] \cdot T = (0.0985 - 0.100) \times 80 = (-0.0015) \times 80 = -0.12 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Verify the additive decomposition:**

$$D = D_S + D_Z = -0.12 + 0.15 = 0.03 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Although the *individual* zero and span drifts are large (0.15 V and −0.12 V), the *net* drift is only 0.03 V because the two errors partially cancel. This is why a *single point check* (e.g., at zero input) is insufficient — drift must be evaluated at **multiple points** along the calibration curve.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Method to Compensate

| Symptom | Suspected Drift Type | Compensation Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Output reads non-zero when input is zero | **Zero Drift** | Re-zero, or use DAC-based offset compensation |
| Slope of calibration curve has changed | **Sensitivity Drift** | Apply software gain correction factor |
| Both zero and slope affected | **Combined Drift** | Two-point re-calibration: re-zero + re-span |
| Output varies with ambient temperature | **Thermal Drift** | Temperature sensor + look-up table (LUT) compensation |
| Gradual monotonic shift over months | **Aging/Time Drift** | Schedule periodic re-calibration (e.g., every 6 months) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Time-Variant Calibration and Drift Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Measurand x] --> B[Sensor Element]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning]
    C --> D[Output Y t]
    E[Time t] --> F[Aging Effect]
    G[Temperature T] --> H[Thermal Effect]
    F --> C
    H --> C
    I[Reference Calibration Y t0] --> J[Subtraction Node]
    D --> J
    J --> K[Drift D t x]
    J --> L[Zero Drift Dz]
    J --> M[Sensitivity Drift Ds]
    K --> N[Compensation Module]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Corrected Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *measurand* and *time/environmental disturbances* both feed into the signal path. The drift is computed by comparing the *live output* against the *factory reference*. The decomposition into zero and span drift enables a *two-point compensation* strategy at the output.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Reproducibility Testing Protocol (Inter-Laboratory Comparison)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Reference Measurand] --> L1[Lab A Operator X Temp 22C]
    P --> L2[Lab B Operator Y Temp 28C]
    P --> L3[Lab C Operator Z Temp 25C]
    L1 --> M1[Reading Y1]
    L2 --> M2[Reading Y2]
    L3 --> M3[Reading Y3]
    M1 --> Stat[Statistical Aggregator]
    M2 --> Stat
    M3 --> Stat
    Stat --> N1[Compute Mean Y bar]
    Stat --> N2[Compute Max Deviation]
    N2 --> Out[Reproducibility Error eR]
    N1 --> Out
    Out --> Pct[Express as percent FS or percent Rdg]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Diagram Question Pattern:** When asked to *"draw a block diagram of the reproducibility test setup"*, draw a *flow from measurand → multiple parallel test stations → statistical aggregator → result*. The above is the canonical layout.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Graph — Distinction Between Repeatability, Reproducibility, and Drift

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Measurement Performance Indices] --> B[Same Conditions]
    A --> C[Changed Conditions]
    A --> D[Over Time]
    B --> B1[Repeatability]
    B1 --> B2[Short-term, same operator, same setup]
    C --> C1[Reproducibility]
    C1 --> C2[Different operators, labs, environments]
    D --> D1[Drift]
    D1 --> D2[Zero Drift plus Sensitivity Drift]
    D1 --> D3[Thermal Drift]
    D1 --> D4[Time Aging Drift]
    B1 --> Metric1[Quantified by std deviation]
    C1 --> Metric2[Quantified by max deviation across conditions]
    D1 --> Metric5[Quantified by output change at constant input]
```

---

### 4.4 Functional Flow — Two-Point Drift Compensation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Apply Known Input x1 typically zero] --> B[Measure Y1]
    C[Apply Known Input x2 typically span] --> D[Measure Y2]
    B --> E[Compute New Zero Correction Zc]
    D --> F[Compute New Sensitivity Sc]
    E --> G[Update Calibration Equation]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Y corrected t equals Sc times x plus Zc]
    H --> I[Deployment in Mechatronic System]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** This is the standard *online* (or *runtime*) calibration routine in industrial PLCs, robotic controllers, and ECUs. The two-point calibration corrects BOTH zero drift and sensitivity drift simultaneously — this is why the KTU syllabus places both topics in the same module.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define reproducibility of a measuring instrument. How is it distinguished from repeatability?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Reproducibility** is the ability of a measuring instrument to provide closely agreeing values for the same measurand when measurements are carried out under *changed conditions* of measurement, such as different operators, different ambient temperatures, different locations, or different times.

It is **quantified** as the maximum deviation (or alternatively a multiple of the standard deviation) of the readings obtained under varied conditions, expressed as a percentage of the full-scale output or true reading.

**Distinction from Repeatability:**

| Repeatability | Reproducibility |
|---|---|
| Measurements under the *same* conditions | Measurements under *changed* conditions |
| Same operator, instrument, environment | Different operator, instrument, or environment |
| Evaluates the *precision* of the instrument alone | Evaluates the *robustness* of the instrument to external influences |

---

**Q2. What is drift in a transducer? Mention its types and their physical origins.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**Drift** is the slow, unwanted, and gradual change in the output of a measuring instrument over a period of time, even when the input (measurand) is held constant. It represents the *temporal instability* of the static calibration.

**Types and Origins:**

1. **Zero Drift** — A shift in the output reading at *zero* input over time. Caused by aging of electronic components, thermal stress, or mechanical relaxation of sensing elements.

2. **Sensitivity (Span) Drift** — A change in the *slope* of the calibration curve over time. Caused by changes in the elastic modulus of sensing materials, aging of transducer springs, or oxidation of strain gauge elements.

3. **Thermal Drift** — A drift specifically caused by variation in ambient temperature. Caused by differential thermal expansion of dissimilar materials (e.g., bimetallic effects) and temperature-dependent electrical parameters.

4. **Time / Aging Drift** — Caused by long-term chemical or physical changes in the material (e.g., corrosion, fatigue, polymer creep).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **total drift** of a linear transducer in terms of zero drift and sensitivity drift. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A load cell has a full-scale capacity of 500 N and a nominal sensitivity of 2 mV/V. At the time of factory calibration, the zero output is 0.0 mV/V. After one year of field use, when a load of 250 N is applied with a 10 V excitation, the output reads 4.95 mV/V. Determine the **total drift, zero drift, and sensitivity drift**. Comment on whether the load cell is still within its specified accuracy of ±0.5% FS. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The transducer behaves *linearly* over the operating range.
2. The drift is *small* compared to the nominal sensitivity (first-order approximation).
3. Zero drift and sensitivity drift are *independent* of each other.

**Derivation:**

The linear calibration equation of the transducer at any time $t$ is:

$$Y(t, x) = S(t) \cdot x + Z(t)$$

Where:
- $Y$ = output (here, in mV/V — a ratiometric output),
- $S(t)$ = sensitivity at time $t$ (mV/V per unit input),
- $Z(t)$ = zero offset at time $t$,
- $x$ = measurand (input).

At the reference time $t_0$ (factory calibration):

$$Y(t_0, x) = S(t_0) \cdot x + Z(t_0)$$

By definition, the **total drift** is the difference between the *current* output and the *reference* output, for the same input $x$:

$$D(t, x) = Y(t, x) - Y(t_0, x)$$

Substituting the two calibration equations:

$$D(t, x) = \big[S(t) \cdot x + Z(t)\big] - \big[S(t_0) \cdot x + Z(t_0)\big]$$

Regrouping the sensitivity and zero terms:

$$D(t, x) = \big[S(t) - S(t_0)\big] \cdot x + \big[Z(t) - Z(t_0)\big]$$

Defining the component drifts:

$$\boxed{D(t, x) = D_S(t, x) + D_Z(t)}$$

where
$$D_S = \big[S(t) - S(t_0)\big] \cdot x \quad \text{(sensitivity drift, multiplicative)}$$
$$D_Z = Z(t) - Z(t_0) \quad \text{(zero drift, additive)}$$

The zero drift is *input-independent* (shifts the entire curve in parallel), while the sensitivity drift is *input-dependent* (rotates the curve). This decomposition is the foundation of *two-point re-calibration* procedures. ∎

---

**Model Solution to A(b):**

**Given:**
- Full-Scale (FS) = 500 N
- Nominal Sensitivity $S(t_0)$ = 2 mV/V (per N of load)
- Excitation voltage = 10 V
- Reference zero $Z(t_0)$ = 0.0 mV/V
- Current applied load $x$ = 250 N
- Current output reading $Y(t, 250)$ = 4.95 mV/V
- Allowed accuracy = ±0.5% of FS

**Step 1 — Compute the reference output at 250 N:**

$$Y(t_0, 250) = S(t_0) \cdot 250 + Z(t_0) = 2 \times 250 + 0.0 = 500 \text{ mV/V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total drift at 250 N:**

$$D(t, 250) = Y(t, 250) - Y(t_0, 250) = 4.95 - 500 = -495.05 \text{ mV/V}$$

This enormous value immediately signals that one of the data is dimensionally misinterpreted. *Re-interpretation:* Since the load cell sensitivity is 2 mV/V *at full scale 500 N*, the **sensitivity per unit load** is:

$$S(t_0) = \frac{2 \text{ mV/V}}{500 \text{ N}} = 0.004 \text{ mV/V per N}$$

Re-computing the reference output:

$$Y(t_0, 250) = 0.004 \times 250 + 0 = 1.00 \text{ mV/V}$$

Total drift:

$$D = 4.95 - 1.00 = 3.95 \text{ mV/V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the zero drift (by independent measurement at zero load — assumed here from problem context):**

Without explicit information, the conventional assumption in such KTU problems is that the *zero* has remained stable ($Z(t) = 0.0$ mV/V) and *all* drift is sensitivity drift.

Therefore:

$$D_Z = Z(t) - Z(t_0) = 0.0 \text{ mV/V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the sensitivity drift:**

$$D_S = D - D_Z = 3.95 - 0.0 = 3.95 \text{ mV/V}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the new sensitivity:**

$$S(t) \cdot 250 = Y(t, 250) = 4.95 \text{ mV/V}$$

$$S(t) = \frac{4.95}{250} = 0.0198 \text{ mV/V per N}$$

Compared to original $S(t_0) = 0.004$ mV/V per N, the sensitivity has *increased* (likely indicating mechanical fatigue / load-cell calibration change).

**Step 6 — Check against the ±0.5% FS specification:**

Full-scale output = 2.0 mV/V (at 500 N). Permissible error = $\pm\,0.5\% \times 2.0 = \pm\,0.010$ mV/V.

Measured error at 250 N: $\vert Y(t, 250) - Y(t_0, 250) \vert = 3.95$ mV/V $\gg$ 0.010 mV/V.

**Conclusion:** The load cell has *grossly exceeded* its specified accuracy. It must be **re-calibrated or replaced** before further use. ✗

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Define **reproducibility error** mathematically and explain the procedure to evaluate it for a pressure transducer using measurements from three different laboratories. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A temperature sensor is calibrated at $t_0 = 0$ with the equation $Y = 0.05\,T + 0.10$ V, where $T$ is in °C. After 6 months, the zero offset has shifted to 0.18 V and the sensitivity has decreased to 0.049 V/°C. Calculate the **drift** at $T = 100\,^\circ$C. If the maximum allowable error is ±0.3 V at 100°C, determine whether the sensor is acceptable. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to B(a):**

**Mathematical Definition:**

For a measurand $x_i$ measured under $N$ different sets of conditions (producing $N$ readings $Y_{i1}, Y_{i2}, \dots, Y_{iN}$), the reproducibility error is:

$$e_R(x_i) = \max_{1 \le j \le N} \big\vert Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_i \big\vert$$

where
$$\bar{Y}_i = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} Y_{ij}$$

The result is then expressed as a percentage:

$$\%R = \frac{e_R}{\text{FSO}} \times 100 \quad \text{(or \% of true reading)}$$

**Procedure to evaluate (3-Lab Protocol):**

1. **Select the test measurand** — Choose a set of at least 3 standard pressures spanning the transducer range (e.g., 25%, 50%, 75% of FS).

2. **Dispatch identical test units / measurand samples** to three different laboratories (Lab A, Lab B, Lab C), each with:
   - Different operators
   - Different ambient temperatures (e.g., 20°C, 27°C, 32°C)
   - Different humidity and possibly different excitation power supplies.

3. **Apply the same standard pressure** in each lab and record the reading.

4. **Repeat for each test pressure.**

5. **Compute the mean** $\bar{Y}_i$ across the three labs for each pressure level $i$.

6. **Compute the absolute deviation** $\Delta_{ij} = \vert Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_i \vert$ for every reading.

7. **Identify the maximum deviation** $e_R = \max(\Delta_{ij})$ — this is the reproducibility error.

8. **Express as %FS** and compare with the transducer's specification.

9. **If the result exceeds the spec**, identify the lab causing the largest deviation and investigate environmental / operator / calibration differences.

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**Given:**
- Reference calibration at $t_0$: $Y(t_0) = 0.05\,T + 0.10$ V
- Time $t = 6$ months: $Z(6) = 0.18$ V, $S(6) = 0.049$ V/°C
- Input $T = 100\,^\circ$C
- Allowable error = ±0.3 V at 100°C

**Step 1 — Reference output at $T = 100\,^\circ$C:**

$$Y(t_0, 100) = 0.05 \times 100 + 0.10 = 5.00 + 0.10 = 5.10 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Current output at $T = 100\,^\circ$C:**

$$Y(6, 100) = 0.049 \times 100 + 0.18 = 4.90 + 0.18 = 5.08 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Total drift at 100°C:**

$$D(6, 100) = 5.08 - 5.10 = -0.02 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Decomposition:**

$$D_Z = Z(6) - Z(0) = 0.18 - 0.10 = 0.08 \text{ V}$$

$$D_S = [S(6) - S(0)] \times T = (0.049 - 0.050) \times 100 = -0.10 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Verification:**

$$D = D_S + D_Z = -0.10 + 0.08 = -0.02 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Acceptability check:**

$$\vert D \vert = 0.02 \text{ V} < 0.3 \text{ V}$$

**Conclusion:** The sensor drift is well within the allowable limit. **The sensor is acceptable.** ✓

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **partial cancellation effect** — zero drift (+0.08 V) and sensitivity drift (−0.10 V) work in opposite directions. The net error is much smaller than either component. This is why a *single-point* check (e.g., at zero input) can be misleading.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Reproducibility & Drift Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing reproducibility with repeatability** — Many students interchange the two. The KTU examiner *specifically* tests this. **Memorize the exact VIM-style definitions.**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the units in drift** — Drift is in the *output units* (V, mV, mA), not in the *input units*. Writing "drift = 0.02 °C" when the output is in volts will cost 1 mark immediately.
> 
> 3. **Single-point drift check** — A common student mistake is to evaluate drift *only at zero input* and conclude the sensor is fine. KTU's 14-mark problem will *deliberately* ask for drift at a *non-zero* point, where sensitivity drift dominates.
> 
> 4. **Missing the decomposition step** — When asked to "find total drift", always show the decomposition into zero + sensitivity components. Examiners award **1–2 marks** specifically for the decomposition step.
> 
> 5. **Using % FS vs. % Rdg incorrectly** — For a 100-kPa transducer reading 50 kPa with an error of 0.5 kPa, the %FS value is 0.5% and the %Rdg value is 1.0%. Mixing these up loses 1 mark.
> 
> 6. **Ignoring the "changed conditions" qualifier** — In a reproducibility definition, *omit* the phrase "under changed conditions" and you lose the core 1 mark of the definition. This phrase is the *only* differentiator from repeatability.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> 📌 **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- [x] **Reproducibility** = agreement under *changed* conditions; **Repeatability** = agreement under *same* conditions. *VIM definitions are non-negotiable.*
- [x] Reproducibility error $e_R = \max \vert Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_i \vert$, expressed as %FS or %Rdg.
- [x] **Drift** = temporal change in output at *constant* input. NOT the same as measurement error.
- [x] Drift decomposes into **Zero Drift** (additive, input-independent) and **Sensitivity Drift** (multiplicative, input-dependent).
- [x] Total drift: $D = D_S + D_Z = [S(t) - S(t_0)]x + [Z(t) - Z(t_0)]$.
- [x] **Thermal drift** is temperature-induced; **Time/Aging drift** is material-degradation-induced.
- [x] **Two-point re-calibration** (re-zero at $x = 0$ and re-span at $x = x_{\max}$) corrects BOTH drift types simultaneously.
- [x] Drift is reported in **output units**, not input units.
- [x] *Single-point drift checks are insufficient*; multi-point calibration curves are needed.
- [x] Reproducibility is quantified via *inter-laboratory* comparison; drift is quantified via *longitudinal* (over time) measurement.
- [x] In mechatronics, both directly affect **closed-loop control accuracy** — poor reproducibility/drift leads to steady-state error in the feedback loop.
- [x] Always specify the *time interval* (e.g., "drift over 6 months") and the *condition change* (e.g., "reproducibility across 3 labs") when quoting either metric.

> **End of Notes — Module 3: Reproducibility and Drift** ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accuracy and Precision, Linearity, Hysteresis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT205 - TRANSDUCERS & MEASUREMENTS (MECHATRONICS ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Accuracy and Precision, Linearity, Hysteresis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Accuracy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Accuracy** is defined as the degree of closeness (or conformity) of a measured value to the **true value** (or actual value) of the quantity being measured.

In the context of KTU Mechatronics, accuracy represents the systematic error or bias of an instrument. An instrument with **high accuracy** will produce readings that cluster tightly around the true reference value, irrespective of whether the readings themselves are tightly grouped (precise) or scattered.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a darts player throwing three darts at a dartboard:
- If all three darts land **inside the small red bullseye**, the throws are highly **accurate** (close to true center) AND highly **precise** (close to each other).
- Accuracy is essentially asking: *"How close did I get to the center of the target?"*

### Mathematical Expression
The **absolute error** of a measurement is expressed as:

$$A_e = \vert M_v - T_v \vert$$

where $M_v$ is the measured value and $T_v$ is the true value. Accuracy is then evaluated as a percentage using:

$$\% \text{ Accuracy} = \left( 1 - \frac{\vert M_v - T_v \vert}{T_v} \right) \times 100$$

---

## 1.2 Precision

> [!NOTE]
> **Precision** is defined as the degree of reproducibility or repeatability of a measurement — i.e., the degree to which repeated measurements of the same quantity under unchanged conditions produce the same result.

Precision quantifies the **random error** (or noise) of an instrument. A precise instrument will yield nearly identical outputs when the same input is applied repeatedly, regardless of whether those outputs are actually correct (accurate) or systematically off-target.

### Conceptual Analogy
Returning to the darts analogy:
- A **precise but inaccurate** player may hit the same spot on the dartboard three times in a row — but that spot is far from the bullseye. The throws are tightly grouped (precise), but consistently wrong (inaccurate).
- Precision is asking: *"If I repeat the experiment, will I get the same answer?"*

### Precision Index
Precision is commonly quantified using standard deviation, where a smaller $\sigma$ indicates higher precision:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

---

## 1.3 The Accuracy vs. Precision Matrix

| Combination | Accuracy | Precision | Real-world Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Case 1** | High | High | Excellent instrument; tight cluster near true value (Ideal) |
| **Case 2** | High | Low | Random scatter around true value; no systematic bias |
| **Case 3** | Low | High | Systematic bias; tightly clustered but offset from true value (Calibration needed) |
| **Case 4** | Low | Low | Completely unreliable instrument |

---

## 1.4 Linearity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linearity** is the property of an instrument whereby the output reading is **directly proportional** to the magnitude of the input quantity being measured across the entire operating range.

An instrument is said to be **linear** if its calibration curve (a plot of output vs. input) forms a **straight line** when fitted through the data points. Most ideal transducers (e.g., ideal strain gauges, ideal LVDTs) are designed to be linear.

### Non-Linearity (Linearity Error)
The deviation of the actual calibration curve from the idealized straight line is termed the **non-linearity** or **linearity error**. It is usually expressed as a percentage of the full-scale (FS) output:

$$\% \text{ Non-linearity} = \frac{\Delta Y_{max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$$

where $\Delta Y_{max}$ is the maximum deviation of the actual output from the fitted straight line, and $Y_{FS}$ is the full-scale output.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a weighing scale:
- A **linear scale** gives: 1 kg $\rightarrow$ 1 unit, 2 kg $\rightarrow$ 2 units, 5 kg $\rightarrow$ 5 units (a perfect 1:1 straight-line relationship).
- A **non-linear scale** might give: 1 kg $\rightarrow$ 1.0 unit, 2 kg $\rightarrow$ 2.2 units, 5 kg $\rightarrow$ 6.5 units (the readings curve away from a straight line).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Engineers often prefer linear instruments because they are easier to calibrate, easier to interpret, and require simpler signal-conditioning circuitry.

---

## 1.5 Hysteresis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hysteresis** is the phenomenon in which the output of an instrument **depends not only on the present value of the input but also on the past history (i.e., the previous values) of the input**.

In mechanical and electromagnetic transducers, hysteresis manifests as a **lag** between the input and the output during dynamic cycling. It arises from:
- **Friction** (in mechanical components),
- **Magnetic domain friction** (in ferromagnetic materials),
- **Molecular friction** (in elastomers and polymers).

### Hysteresis Error
When a transducer is subjected to a full input cycle (ascending input followed by descending input), the output traces two different paths, producing an open loop rather than a single line. The **maximum vertical separation** between the ascending and descending curves at any input value is the **hysteresis error**:

$$\varepsilon_H = \Delta Y_{H,max}$$

It is often expressed as a percentage of full-scale output:

$$\% \text{ Hysteresis} = \frac{\Delta Y_{H,max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$$

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **car tire's response on sand**:
- When you press the accelerator (input increases), the tire digs in and the car moves forward a certain distance.
- When you release the accelerator (input decreases), the car may coast a different distance for the same pedal release, because the sand has compacted.
- The car's *current position* depends not only on *current pedal input* but also on the *previous pedal inputs* — this is hysteresis.

In an **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)**, hysteresis arises from residual magnetism in the core. In a **bourdon tube pressure gauge**, hysteresis arises from mechanical backlash and friction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static Characteristics Overview

All four terms (Accuracy, Precision, Linearity, Hysteresis) are classified as **static characteristics** of a measurement system, meaning they describe the instrument's behavior when the measured quantity is either constant or changing very slowly (quasi-static condition). Together, they define the **ideal static response** of a transducer.

The **ideal static input-output relationship** for a measurement system is given by:

$$Y = K \cdot X$$

where:
- $Y$ = Output reading
- $X$ = Input (measurand)
- $K$ = Static sensitivity (slope of the calibration curve)

Any real instrument deviates from this ideal linear relationship due to the static errors discussed below.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### A. Accuracy — The 'Closeness to Truth' Metric
1. **Systematic error component**: Inaccuracy is primarily caused by **systematic errors** (bias), such as a miscalibrated zero, a worn sensor element, or an environmental drift.
2. **Affected by**: Calibration quality, environmental conditions, aging of components.
3. **Improved by**: Regular recalibration, zero-adjustment, and proper installation.
4. **Why it matters**: A high-accuracy instrument provides data with low systematic error, which is essential when absolute physical quantities (e.g., flow rate, temperature) must be trusted.

### B. Precision — The 'Repeatability' Metric
1. **Random error component**: Imprecision is caused by **random errors** (noise), such as electrical noise, vibration, and stochastic fluctuations.
2. **Affected by**: Resolution of the instrument, signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), observer parallax.
3. **Improved by**: Increasing the number of readings and using statistical averaging, increasing SNR, or upgrading the sensor's resolution.
4. **Why it matters**: High precision is required in **closed-loop control systems** (e.g., robotics) where repeatable sensor feedback is critical for stability.

### C. Linearity — The 'Proportionality' Metric
1. **Geometric property**: Linearity refers to the **shape** of the calibration curve, not the closeness to the true value.
2. **Affected by**: Non-ideal physical laws governing the sensor (e.g., quadratic thermal expansion), non-linear electronic components, and mechanical saturations.
3. **Improved by**: 
   - **Hardware linearization** (using op-amp circuits, log amplifiers).
   - **Software linearization** (using lookup tables or polynomial curve fitting in the digital domain).
4. **Methods of fitting a straight line to calibration data**:
   - **End-Point Fit**: Line passing through the first and last calibration points.
   - **Best-Fit (Least Squares) Line**: Minimizes the sum of squared deviations of all data points.
   - **Terminal-Base Fit**: Line passing through the lower end-point with the slope adjusted to minimize maximum deviation.

### D. Hysteresis — The 'Memory Effect' Metric
1. **Path-dependent behavior**: Output for a given input is different depending on whether the input is increasing or decreasing.
2. **Affected by**: Internal friction, magnetic domain lag, elastic hysteresis in springs, backlash in gears, thermal inertia.
3. **Improved by**: 
   - Using low-hysteresis materials (e.g., Mu-metal for magnetic cores).
   - Mechanical design to reduce friction (e.g., jeweled bearings in analog meters).
   - Software compensation using input-history-aware algorithms.
4. **Why it matters**: Hysteresis causes **deadband** errors in actuators and sensors, which can lead to limit-cycle oscillations in feedback control.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Key Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Absolute Error | $A_e = \vert M_v - T_v \vert$ | Same as measurand | Always positive magnitude |
| 2 | % Accuracy | $\% \text{ Acc} = \left( 1 - \frac{\vert M_v - T_v \vert}{T_v} \right) \times 100$ | Percentage | Closer to 100% = better |
| 3 | Relative Accuracy | $A_{rel} = \frac{A_e}{T_v}$ | Dimensionless | Ratio of error to true value |
| 4 | Precision (Std. Dev.) | $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Same as measurand | Lower $\sigma$ = higher precision |
| 5 | Sensitivity (Linear) | $K = \frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X}$ | Output units / Input units | Slope of calibration curve |
| 6 | % Non-linearity | $\% NL = \frac{\Delta Y_{max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Max deviation from best-fit line |
| 7 | Hysteresis Error | $\varepsilon_H = \Delta Y_{H,max}$ | Same as output | Max vertical loop width |
| 8 | % Hysteresis | $\% H = \frac{\Delta Y_{H,max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Expressed as % of FS |
| 9 | Total Static Error | $e_T = e_{NL} + e_H + e_{Z} + e_{S} + \dots$ | Output units | Sum of all static error components |
| 10 | Resolution | $R = \frac{Y_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ | Output units | Smallest measurable increment |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Accuracy** is paramount in **medical instrumentation** (e.g., blood pressure monitors) and **process industries** (e.g., pharmaceutical manufacturing), where absolute correctness of the measured value is critical.
- **Precision** is critical in **robotic joint encoders** and **navigation systems (GPS, IMU)**, where repeatable incremental measurements are needed for trajectory tracking.
- **Linearity** is required in **ADC front-ends**, **anti-lock braking systems (ABS)**, and **aerospace control surfaces**, where the mathematical relationship between input and output must be predictable.
- **Hysteresis** must be minimized in **force-feedback joysticks**, **precision weighing balances**, and **MEMS accelerometers**, where deadband would degrade user experience and measurement fidelity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Linearity Error (End-Point Fit Method)

**Problem Setup:** A pressure transducer is calibrated and yields the following readings:

| True Pressure $X$ (kPa) | 0 | 20 | 40 | 60 | 80 | 100 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Output $Y$ (mV) | 0 | 19.8 | 40.4 | 60.9 | 80.7 | 100.0 |

**Step 1:** Identify the end-points of the calibration data.

The end-points are at $X = 0$ and $X = 100$ kPa. The corresponding output values are $Y = 0$ mV and $Y = 100.0$ mV respectively.

**Step 2:** Determine the equation of the end-point fit straight line.

The line passes through $(0, 0)$ and $(100, 100)$:

$$m = \frac{Y_2 - Y_1}{X_2 - X_1} = \frac{100.0 - 0}{100 - 0} = 1.0 \ \text{mV/kPa}$$

$$Y_{line} = m \cdot X + c = 1.0 \cdot X + 0 = X$$

**Step 3:** Compute the deviation of each actual reading from the fitted line.

| True $X$ (kPa) | Actual $Y$ (mV) | $Y_{line}$ (mV) | Deviation $\Delta Y = Y - Y_{line}$ (mV) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 |
| 20 | 19.8 | 20.0 | $-0.2$ |
| 40 | 40.4 | 40.0 | $+0.4$ |
| 60 | 60.9 | 60.0 | $+0.9$ |
| 80 | 80.7 | 80.0 | $+0.7$ |
| 100 | 100.0 | 100.0 | 0.0 |

**Step 4:** Identify the maximum absolute deviation.

$$\Delta Y_{max} = \vert +0.9 \vert = 0.9 \ \text{mV}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the percentage non-linearity (using $Y_{FS} = 100$ mV):

$$\% NL = \frac{\Delta Y_{max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100 = \frac{0.9}{100} \times 100 = 0.9\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> The maximum deviation occurs at $X = 60$ kPa, giving a linearity error of **0.9% of full scale**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hysteresis Error in a Thermistor Calibration

**Problem Setup:** A thermistor is cycled between $25^\circ$C and $75^\circ$C, and the resistance readings are:

| Temperature $T$ ($^\circ$C) | Heating (Ascending) $R \uparrow$ ($\Omega$) | Cooling (Descending) $R \downarrow$ ($\Omega$) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 25 | 1100 | 1100 |
| 35 | 980 | 985 |
| 45 | 870 | 880 |
| 55 | 760 | 775 |
| 65 | 650 | 665 |
| 75 | 550 | 550 |

**Step 1:** At each intermediate temperature, compute the hysteresis gap $\Delta R = R \uparrow - R \downarrow$.

| $T$ ($^\circ$C) | $R \uparrow$ ($\Omega$) | $R \downarrow$ ($\Omega$) | $\Delta R$ ($\Omega$) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 25 | 1100 | 1100 | 0 |
| 35 | 980 | 985 | $-5$ |
| 45 | 870 | 880 | $-10$ |
| 55 | 760 | 775 | $-15$ |
| 65 | 650 | 665 | $-15$ |
| 75 | 550 | 550 | 0 |

**Step 2:** Identify the maximum magnitude of the loop width.

$$\Delta R_{max} = \vert -15 \vert = 15 \ \Omega$$

**Step 3:** Determine the full-scale output.

$$Y_{FS} = R_{max} - R_{min} = 1100 - 550 = 550 \ \Omega$$

**Step 4:** Compute the percentage hysteresis.

$$\% H = \frac{\Delta R_{max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100 = \frac{15}{550} \times 100 = 2.727\%$$

$$\boxed{\% H \approx 2.73\% \text{ of full scale}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The hysteresis error is **maximum at $T = 55^\circ$C and $T = 65^\circ$C**, where the thermal mass of the thermistor causes the largest lag between the actual and indicated temperature.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Accuracy and Precision Combined Analysis

**Problem Setup:** A voltmeter is used to measure a true voltage of $V_T = 50.00$ V. Five repeated measurements yield: 49.8, 50.1, 50.0, 49.9, 50.2 V.

**Step 1:** Compute the mean (average) measured value.

$$\bar{V} = \frac{49.8 + 50.1 + 50.0 + 49.9 + 50.2}{5} = \frac{250.0}{5} = 50.00 \ \text{V}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the absolute error (using mean value as best estimate).

$$A_e = \vert \bar{V} - V_T \vert = \vert 50.00 - 50.00 \vert = 0.00 \ \text{V}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the percentage accuracy.

$$\% \text{ Accuracy} = \left( 1 - \frac{0.00}{50.00} \right) \times 100 = 100\%$$

**Step 4:** Compute the standard deviation (precision metric).

| $x_i$ (V) | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 49.8 | $-0.2$ | $0.04$ |
| 50.1 | $+0.1$ | $0.01$ |
| 50.0 | $0.0$ | $0.00$ |
| 49.9 | $-0.1$ | $0.01$ |
| 50.2 | $+0.2$ | $0.04$ |
| **Sum** | — | **0.10** |

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2} = \sqrt{\frac{0.10}{4}} = \sqrt{0.025} \approx 0.1581 \ \text{V}$$

**Step 5:** Interpret the result.

> [!NOTE]
> - **Accuracy = 100%** $\Rightarrow$ the mean is exactly at the true value.
> - **Precision ($\sigma$) = 0.158 V** $\Rightarrow$ the readings scatter within $\pm 0.2$ V of the mean.
> - This instrument is **highly accurate and reasonably precise** (an ideal scenario for a calibrated laboratory voltmeter).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Type of Error | Affected By | Reduction Strategy | Typical Magnitude |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Inaccuracy** | Systematic | Calibration, drift, bias | Recalibration, zero-setting | 0.1% – 5% FS |
| **Imprecision** | Random | Noise, resolution, observer | Averaging, filtering, better sensor | 0.01% – 1% FS |
| **Non-linearity** | Systematic | Non-ideal physics, electronics | Linearization (HW/SW) | 0.1% – 2% FS |
| **Hysteresis** | Path-dependent | Friction, magnetism, elasticity | Material selection, design compensation | 0.5% – 5% FS |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Accuracy vs. Precision: Dartboard Visualization

The following Mermaid diagram conceptually illustrates the four combinations of accuracy and precision using target-style flow representations.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Instrument Output Distribution] --> B{Mean Position vs True Value}
    A --> C{Spread of Repeated Readings}
    
    B -->|Close to True Value| D[Accurate]
    B -->|Far from True Value| E[Inaccurate]
    C -->|Small Spread| F[Precise]
    C -->|Large Spread| G[Imprecise]
    
    D --> H[Case 1: HIGH Accuracy and HIGH Precision]
    E --> I[Case 3: LOW Accuracy and HIGH Precision]
    D --> J[Case 2: HIGH Accuracy and LOW Precision]
    E --> K[Case 4: LOW Accuracy and LOW Precision]
    F --> H
    F --> I
    G --> J
    G --> K
    
    H --> L[IDEAL INSTRUMENT - REPEATABLE and CORRECT]
    I --> M[NEEDS RECALIBRATION - SYSTEMATIC BIAS PRESENT]
    J --> N[NEEDS NOISE FILTERING - RANDOM ERROR DOMINANT]
    K --> O[UNRELIABLE - REJECT THE SENSOR]
    
    style H fill:#90EE90
    style I fill:#FFD700
    style J fill:#87CEEB
    style K fill:#FF6B6B
    style L fill:#00C853
    style M fill:#FFA000
    style N fill:#0288D1
    style O fill:#D32F2F
```

---

## 4.2 Static Characteristics Block Architecture

This diagram maps how the four static characteristics interact within a generalized measurement system.

```mermaid
graph LR
```
