# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Define the term 'Liquid Crystal' and explain why it is suitable for use in display devices."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **liquid crystal (LC)** is an organic phase of matter that possesses **long-range orientational order** (like a solid crystal) but **no long-range positional order** (like a liquid). The molecules are typically **rod-shaped (calamitic)** and align along a common director **n̂**.

**Suitability for displays (2 marks):**
1. **Dielectric anisotropy** ($\Delta\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{\parallel} - \varepsilon_{\perp} \neq 0$): allows electric fields to reorient molecules — providing an **electrical control knob** for optical properties.
2. **Optical birefringence** ($\Delta n = n_{e} - n_{o} \neq 0$): makes the LC layer an **electrically tunable wave plate**, which in conjunction with crossed polarizers produces bright/dark pixel states.
3. **Low driving voltage** ($V_{th} \sim 1$ V) and **low power consumption** (no light generation, only modulation) make LC ideal for portable and battery-powered mechatronic HMIs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** A 3-mark answer must cover: (1) definition, (2) at least two physical properties enabling display use, (3) one engineering advantage. Skipping any of these costs 1 mark.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between emissive and non-emissive displays. Give one example of each and state why LCDs are classified as non-emissive."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Emissive Display | Non-Emissive Display |
|---|---|---|
| **Light Source** | Generates its own light | Modulates external/ambient light |
| **Power Use** | High (needs to produce photons) | Low (only modulates) |
| **Examples** | LED, OLED, Plasma | **LCD**, E-paper, Electrophoretic |
| **Visibility in Dark** | Excellent | Requires backlight or ambient light |

**Why LCD is non-emissive (1 mark):** The liquid crystal layer in an LCD **does not emit photons**; it merely **rotates or maintains the polarization** of light from a separate **backlight unit (BLU)** or ambient source. The image you see is the result of **selective transmission or blocking** of this external light by the LC layer combined with the crossed polarizers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part B questions offer **internal choice**. The module-level KTU pattern typically gives two questions of 14 marks, with two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. You may attempt EITHER set.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned with 2024 Scheme, Module 4, PCMRT205]**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a Twisted Nematic (TN) Liquid Crystal Display (LCD)**. Clearly state the role of the front polarizer, rear polarizer, ITO electrodes, alignment layers, and the backlight. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A TN-LCD cell is designed with a **liquid crystal birefringence $\Delta n = 0.12$** and is intended to operate at the **first Gooch-Tarry minimum** for a green reference wavelength $\lambda = 550$ nm. Calculate:
- (i) The required **optical retardance $\Delta n \cdot d$**.
- (ii) The **physical cell gap d**.
- (iii) The **threshold voltage $V_{th}$**, given elastic constant $K = 12 \times 10^{-12}$ N and dielectric anisotropy $\Delta\varepsilon = 11.5$. ($\varepsilon_{0} = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

**Construction (3.5 marks):** The TN-LCD cell, from rear to front, consists of:
1. **Backlight Unit (BLU):** A cold-cathode fluorescent lamp (CCFL) or LED array that provides uniform white light.
2. **Rear Polarizer:** A dichroic sheet that transmits only one polarization direction (say, 0°).
3. **Rear ITO Electrode:** A thin (~100 nm) transparent conductive layer of Indium Tin Oxide, with a **polyimide alignment layer** unidirectionally rubbed (e.g., along 0°).
4. **Liquid Crystal Layer:** A **nematic LC** (e.g., 5CB or E7) of thickness $d \approx 3\text{–}10\;\mu m$, in which the director twists by **90°** from the rear to the front alignment layer.
5. **Front ITO Electrode** with alignment layer rubbed at **90°** to the rear.
6. **Front Polarizer (Analyzer):** Crossed (90°) to the rear polarizer.
7. **Color Filter Array (for color displays):** Red, Green, Blue sub-pixels.

**Working (3.5 marks):**
- **OFF state (V = 0):** The 90° helix acts as an **optical waveguide** for the polarized light (Mauguin condition $u \gg 1$ satisfied). The polarization plane rotates by 90° as light traverses the cell. The analyzer (also at 90°) is **parallel** to this rotated polarization → **light passes → pixel appears bright (white).**
- **ON state (V > Vth):** The applied electric field exerts a dielectric torque ($\vec{\tau} = \vec{P} \times \vec{E}$) on the LC molecules. Because $\Delta\varepsilon > 0$, molecules align **parallel to the field** (homeotropic). The helix is destroyed, so the LC layer now behaves as a **uniform birefringent slab** whose optic axis is perpendicular to the substrates. Polarized light enters and exits with its polarization **unchanged**. The crossed analyzer **blocks** the light → **pixel appears dark (black).**
- **Grayscale:** Intermediate voltages give partial tilt $\theta_m(V)$, modulating $\Delta n_{eff}$ and hence the transmission $I = I_0 \sin^2(\pi \Delta n_{eff} d / \lambda)$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Optical retardance $\Delta n \cdot d$ (2 marks):**

At the first Gooch-Tarry minimum, $u = \sqrt{2}$:

$$
u = \frac{\pi\,\Delta n\,d}{\lambda} = \sqrt{2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \Delta n \cdot d = \frac{\sqrt{2}\,\lambda}{\pi}
$$

$$
\Delta n \cdot d = \frac{1.414 \times 550 \times 10^{-9}}{3.1416} = 2.476 \times 10^{-7}\;m \approx 0.248\;\mu m
$$

**(ii) Cell gap d (2 marks):**

$$
d = \frac{\Delta n \cdot d}{\Delta n} = \frac{2.476 \times 10^{-7}}{0.12} = 2.063 \times 10^{-6}\;m \approx 2.06\;\mu m
$$

**(iii) Threshold voltage $V_{th}$ (3 marks):**

$$
V_{th} = \pi\sqrt{\frac{K}{\varepsilon_{0}\,\Delta\varepsilon}} = \pi\sqrt{\frac{12 \times 10^{-12}}{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 11.5}}
$$

$$
V_{th} = \pi\sqrt{\frac{12 \times 10^{-12}}{1.0182 \times 10^{-10}}} = \pi\sqrt{0.11786} = \pi \times 0.3433
$$

$$
V_{th} \approx 1.078\;V
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned with 2024 Scheme, Module 4, PCMRT205]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **transmitted intensity of light through a TN-LCD pixel** placed between crossed polarizers, in terms of the optical retardance $\delta$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Passive Matrix (PM) and Active Matrix (AM / TFT) LCD addressing schemes** with respect to: (i) image quality, (ii) maximum number of addressable rows (N), (iii) presence/absence of cross-talk, and (iv) power consumption. State **two specific mechatronic applications** where TFT-LCDs are indispensable. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The liquid crystal layer is modeled as a **uniaxial wave plate** of retardance $\delta = 2\pi \Delta n_{eff} d / \lambda$.
2. The mid-rotation angle is **$\alpha = 45°$** (valid in the OFF state of a TN cell).
3. Polarizers are **ideal (extinction ratio = ∞)** and **crossed (90°)**.
4. Light is **monochromatic** with intensity $I_0$ after the rear polarizer.

**Derivation using Jones Calculus (6 marks):**

**Step 1 — Light after the rear polarizer** (along x-axis):
$$
\mathbf{E}_{in} = \begin{pmatrix} E_{0} \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad I_{0} = |E_{0}|^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Rotate coordinate frame by $\alpha$** so that the LC's fast axis is along the new x'-axis:
$$
R(\alpha) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\alpha & \sin\alpha \\ -\sin\alpha & \cos\alpha \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the wave-plate Jones matrix** (retardance $\delta$):
$$
W = \begin{pmatrix} e^{-i\delta/2} & 0 \\ 0 & e^{+i\delta/2} \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Rotate back** by $-\alpha$:
$$
R(-\alpha) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\alpha & -\sin\alpha \\ \sin\alpha & \cos\alpha \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the analyzer** (90° to the input polarizer):
$$
P(90^\circ) = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply matrices and take the squared magnitude** of the output amplitude. The full computation yields:

$$
I_{out} = I_{0}\,\sin^{2}(2\alpha)\,\sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $\alpha = 45°$** (so $\sin^2 2\alpha = 1$) and $\delta = 2\pi \Delta n_{eff} d / \lambda$:

$$
\boxed{\;I_{out} = I_{0}\,\sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{\pi\,\Delta n_{eff}\,d}{\lambda}\right)\;}
$$

This equation governs **OFF-state transmission, ON-state extinction, and grayscale levels** of the LCD.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

| Criterion | Passive Matrix (PM-LCD) | Active Matrix (AM / TFT-LCD) |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Image Quality** | Low contrast; visible cross-talk; poor at high row counts | High contrast, sharp image, no cross-talk |
| **(ii) Max Rows N** | Limited to **N < ~100** (N²-RMS voltage selection rule) | **N > 1000** easily achievable (each pixel has its own TFT switch) |
| **(iii) Cross-Talk** | Severe — neighboring pixels receive partial selection voltage | Negligible — TFT isolates each pixel |
| **(iv) Power Consumption** | Lower static power, but inefficient backlight | Slightly higher (TFT gate bias), but enables **local dimming** and lower BLU power |

**Two specific mechatronic applications of TFT-LCDs (2 marks):**
1. **CNC Machine HMI Panels:** Require high-resolution, sunlight-readable, multi-color graphical displays for G-code visualization, toolpath rendering, and alarm logging — only TFT-LCDs can deliver this fidelity in a rugged form factor.
2. **Robotic Surgical Consoles / Tele-operated Robots:** Demand zero cross-talk, fast refresh (>120 Hz), and accurate color rendering for endoscopic video feedback — TFT-LCDs are the industry standard.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in $d$ and $\Delta n \cdot d$ calculations — always convert $\mu m$ ↔ $m$ explicitly. KTU examiners deduct ½ to 1 mark per numerical if units are inconsistent.
> 2. **Confusing $\alpha$ with the helical twist angle** — in a TN cell, the twist is 90° but the Jones-matrix mid-rotation is **45°**. This distinction is tested frequently.
> 3. **Not specifying the Gooch-Tarry order** — students often write $\Delta n \cdot d = \lambda/2$ without stating "**at the first minimum**." You must specify which order.
> 4. **Skipping the assumption statement** in derivations — the examiner reserves **1 full mark** for a clear list of assumptions.
> 5. **Misclassifying LCDs as "emissive"** — always state explicitly: *"The LC layer modulates but does not generate light; the backlight is the actual source."*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision kit — read this section 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **LCD = Light modulator, NOT light emitter.** Image is created by **polarization rotation** in an LC layer, gated by **crossed polarizers**.
- ✅ **Three physical pillars of LCD operation:** (1) **Birefringence** ($\Delta n$), (2) **Dielectric anisotropy** ($\Delta\varepsilon$), (3) **Polarization optics** (Mauguin waveguide effect).
- ✅ **Mauguin condition $u = \pi \Delta n d / \lambda \gg 1$** must be satisfied in the OFF state for the 90° twist to act as a polarization waveguide.
- ✅ **Freedericksz threshold:** $V_{th} = \pi \sqrt{K / (\varepsilon_0 \Delta\varepsilon)}$ — the minimum voltage at which molecular reorientation begins.
- ✅ **Gooch-Tarry first minimum:** $\Delta n \cdot d = \sqrt{2}\,\lambda / \pi \approx 0.48\;\mu m$ (at $\lambda = 550$ nm) — gives wavelength-independent dark state.
- ✅ **Master transmission equation:** $I_{out} = I_0 \sin^2(\pi \Delta n_{eff} d / \lambda)$ — the equation you should be able to write **in your sleep** for KTU.
- ✅ **Active Matrix (TFT) > Passive Matrix** in every metric: contrast, N, cross-talk, refresh. TFT-LCDs dominate modern mechatronic HMIs.
- ✅ **Typical layer order (rear → front):** BLU → Rear polarizer → Rear ITO + alignment → LC layer → Front ITO + alignment → Analyzer → Color filter → Eye.
- ✅ **OFF = bright, ON = dark** (for a normally-white TN-LCD) — remember this polarity for image-formation questions.
- ✅ **LCD advantages:** Low power, lightweight, EMI-immune, no X-ray emission, scalable to large sizes.
- ✅ **LCD disadvantages:** Requires backlight (no true black in dark scenes), slow response vs OLED, viewing-angle limits in TN (mitigated by IPS / VA).
- ✅ **KTU favourite 14-mark question format:** Layered construction explanation (7 m) + numerical on retardance / cell gap / $V_{th}$ (7 m). Practice this hybrid format at least 3 times.
- ✅ **Remember to convert** all lengths to **metres** before plugging into formulas; $\mu m$ and $nm$ are *not* SI and cost marks if you forget to convert.
- ✅ **Crossed polarizer transmission = 0** when retardance $\delta = 0$ OR when fast axis aligns with the polarizer — memorize this boundary condition.

> **End of Module 4 — LCD Displays Notes. Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE!**
